Those effects could, however, be offset by lower energy input costs to Eni’s refining, biorefining, and petrochemical plants.

Assuming a price scenario reflecting the wholesale gas price trend implied by evolution of the forward curves in the days immediately following the issuance of the decree, the overall estimated impact on the Group’s consolidated operating results would not be significant.
Regarding the increase in the Italian Regional Income tax rate, the effect is negligible, also considering the expected evolution of the taxable base.
The Group’s operations expose Eni to a wide range of significant health, safety, security, and environmental risks.

Flammability and toxicity of hydrocarbons, technical faults, malfunctioning of plants, equipment and facilities, control systems failure, human errors, acts of sabotage, attacks, loss of containment and climate-related hazards can trigger adverse consequences such as explosions, blow-outs, fires, oil and gas spills from wells, pipeline and tankers, release of contaminants and pollutants in the air, ground and water, toxic emissions, and other negative events.
The magnitude of these risks is influenced by scale, geographical reach, operational diversity, and technical complexity of Eni’s activities.
Eni’s future results of operations, cash flow and liquidity depend on its ability to identify and address the risks and hazards inherent to operating in those industries.

b) Eni expects to incur material operating expenses and expenditures in future years in relation to compliance with applicable environmental, health and safety regulations, including compliance with any national or international regulation on greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, as well as to retain high standards of reliability in its industrial operations Eni’s activities are highly regulated.

Laws and regulations intended to preserve the environment and to safeguard health and safety of workers and communities impose several obligations, requirements, and prohibitions to the Company’s businesses due to their inherent risky nature because of flammability, dangerousness, and toxicity of hydrocarbons and of objective complexities of industrial processes to explore, develop, extract, refine, handle and transport oil, natural gas, liquefied natural gas and products.

Breaches of environmental, health and safety laws and regulations as in the case of negligent or willful release of pollutants and contaminants into the atmosphere, the soil, water or groundwater or exceeding the concentration thresholds of contaminants set by the law expose the Company to the incurrence of liabilities associated with compensation for environmental, health or safety damage and expenses for environmental remediation and clean-up, as well as damage to reputation.

Furthermore, in the case of violation of certain rules regarding the safeguard of the environment and the health and safety of employees, contractors, and other collaborators of the Company, and of communities, the Company may incur liabilities in connection with the negligent or willful violations of laws by its employees as per Italian Law Decree No.
Furthermore, in certain situations where Eni is not the operator, the Company may have limited influence and control over third parties, which may limit its ability to manage and control such risks.
c) The Group is exposed to operational risks in connection with the transportation of hydrocarbons All of Eni’s segments of operations involve, to varying degrees, the transportation of hydrocarbons.

Risks in transportation activities depend on several factors and variables, including the hazardous nature of the products transported due to their flammability and toxicity, the transportation methods utilized (pipelines, shipping, river freight, rail, road and gas distribution networks), the volumes involved and the sensitivity of the regions through which the transport passes (quality of infrastructure, population density, environmental considerations).

All modes of transportation of hydrocarbons are particularly exposed to risks of blowout, fire, release of toxic agents in the atmosphere, spillover of oil and other pollutants and loss of containment and, given that normally high volumes are involved, could present significant risks to people, environment and property.

d) The Group is not insured against all potential HSE risks Eni retains worldwide third-party liability insurance coverage, which is designed to hedge part of the liabilities associated with possible incidents occurring at the Group plants and installations resulting in damage to third-parties, loss of value to the Group’s assets related to adverse events and in connection with environmental clean-up and remediation.
Management believes that its insurance coverage is in line with industry practice and is enough to cover normal risks in its operations.
However, the Company is not insured against all potential risks.

In the event of a major environmental disaster, such as the incident which occurred at the Macondo well in the Gulf of Mexico several years ago, Eni’s third-party liability insurance would not provide any material coverage and thus the Company’s liability would far exceed the maximum coverage provided by its insurance.

The loss Eni could suffer in case of a disaster of material proportions would depend on all the facts and circumstances of the event and would be subject to a whole range of uncertainties, including legal uncertainty as to the scope of liability for consequential damages, which may include economic damage not directly connected to the disaster.

The Company cannot guarantee that it will not suffer any uninsured loss and there can be no guarantee, particularly in the case of a major environmental disaster or industrial accident, that such a loss would not have a material adverse effect on the Company.

The Company has invested and will continue to invest significant financial resources to continuously upgrade the methods and systems for safeguarding the reliability of its plants, production facilities, well execution, vessels, transport and storage infrastructures, the safety and the health of its employees, contractors, local communities, and the environment, to prevent risks, to comply with applicable laws and policies and to respond to and learn from unforeseen incidents.
However, these measures may ultimately not be completely successful in preventing and/or altogether eliminating risks of adverse events.
For example, in December 2024, a fire occurred at a fuel storage site operated by Eni, which caused the death of five people while working at site operations, several wounded and damage to property.
The Group made a loss provision to account for all damage to people and property because insurance coverage was not enough.

LEGAL, IT AND FINANCIAL RISKS a) Eni is exposed to the risk of material environmental liabilities in connection with pending litigation Eni has incurred in the past and may incur in the future material environmental liabilities in connection with the alleged breach of environmental laws claimed by administrative bodies and thirdparties at industrial hubs where the Group is currently performing its activities or where the Group has ceased to operate and is performing decommissioning and remediation activities.
Eni is also exposed to claims under environmental requirements and, from time to time, such claims have been made against the Company.
Furthermore, environmental regulations in Italy and elsewhere typically impose strict liability.

Strict liability means that in some situations Eni could be exposed to liability for clean-up and remediation costs, environmental damage, and other damages because of Eni’s conduct of operations that was lawful at the time it occurred or of the management of industrial hubs by prior operators or other third parties, who were subsequently taken over by Eni.

In addition, plaintiffs may seek to obtain compensation for damage resulting from events of contamination and pollution or in case the Company is found liable for violations of any environmental laws or regulations.
Due to the history and development of the Group, Eni is particularly exposed to this kind of risk in Italy.

130 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX The Group is performing remediation and cleaning-up activities at several Italian industrial hub where the Group’s products were produced, processed, stored, distributed, or sold, such as chemical plants, mineral-metallurgic plants, refineries, and other facilities, which were subsequently disposed of, liquidated, closed, or shut down.
Eni has been alleged to be liable for having polluted and contaminated proprietary or concession areas where those dismissed industrial hubs were located.

State or local public administrations have sued Eni for environmental and other damages and for clean-up and remediation measures in addition to those which were performed by the Company, or which the Company has committed to performing, including allegations of violations of criminal laws (for example for alleged environmental crimes such as failure to perform soil or groundwater reclamation, environmental disaster and contamination, illegal discharge of toxic materials, amongst others).

Although Eni believes that it may not be held liable for having exceeded in the past pollution thresholds that are unlawful according to current regulations, but were allowed by laws then effective, or because the Group took over operations from third parties, it cannot be excluded that Eni could potentially incur such environmental liabilities.

Eni’s financial statements account for provisions relating to the expected costs to clean up and remediate contaminated areas and groundwater at Eni’s shut-down or operational Italian hubs, where legal or constructive obligations exist and the associated costs can be reasonably estimated in a reliable manner, representing management’s best estimates of the Company’s existing environmental liabilities.

Although the Company has provided for known environmental obligations that are probable and reasonably estimable, it is likely that the Company will continue to incur additional liabilities in the future.

The additional costs are not fully determinable due to such factors as the unknown magnitude of possible contamination, the unknown timing and extent of the remediation actions that may be required, the determination of the company’s liability in proportion to other responsible parties, and the extent to which such costs are recoverable from third parties.

These future costs may be material to results of operations in the period in which they are recognized, but the Company does not expect these costs will have a material effect on its consolidated financial position or liquidity.
b) Risks related to legal proceedings and compliance with anti-corruption legislation Eni is the defendant in several civil and criminal actions and administrative proceedings.

Certain legal proceedings and investigations in which Eni or its subsidiaries or its officers and employees are defendants might involve allegations of breaching anti-bribery and anti-corruption laws and regulations and other ethical misconduct.
Such proceedings are described in the Notes to the Consolidated Financial Statements (note no.

Ethical misconduct and non-compliance with applicable laws and regulations, including non-compliance with anti-bribery and anti-corruption laws, by Eni, its officers and employees, its partners, agents or others acting on the Group’s behalf, could expose Eni and its employees to criminal and civil penalties and could be damaging to Eni’s reputation, business prospects and results of operations.

c) Risks from acquisitions Eni is constantly monitoring the market in search of opportunities to acquire individual assets or companies with a view of achieving its growth targets or complementing its asset portfolio.
Acquisitions entail an execution risk - the risk that the acquirer will not be able to effectively integrate the purchased assets to achieve expected synergies.
In addition, acquisitions entail a financial risk - the risk of not being able to recover the purchase costs of acquired assets, in case of a prolonged decline in the market prices of commodities.
Eni may also incur unanticipated costs or assume unexpected liabilities and losses in connection with companies or assets it acquires.

If the integration and financial risks related to acquisitions materialize, expected synergies from acquisition may fall short of management’s targets and Eni’s financial performance and shareholders’ returns may be adversely affected.
d) Eni’s crisis management systems may be ineffective Eni has developed contingency plans to continue or recover operations following a disruption or incident.

An inability to restore or replace critical capacity to an agreed level within an agreed period could prolong the impact of any disruption and could severely affect business, operations and financial results.
Eni has crisis management plans and the capability to deal with emergencies at every level of its operations.

If Eni does not respond or is not seen to respond in an appropriate manner to either an external or internal crisis, this could adversely impact the Group’s reputation, its business prospects and results of operations.

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 131 e) Cyberattacks, disruption to or breaches of Eni’s critical IT services or digital infrastructure and security systems could adversely affect the Group’s business, increase costs and damage Eni’s reputation The Group’s activities depend heavily on the reliability and security of its information technology (IT) systems and digital security.

The Group’s IT systems, some of which are managed by third parties, are susceptible to being compromised, damaged, disrupted or shutdown due to failures during the process of upgrading or replacing software, databases or components, power or network outages, hardware failures, cyberattacks (e.g., viruses, computer intrusions), user errors or natural disasters.
Cyber threat is constantly evolving.
The oil and gas industry is subject to fast-evolving risks from cyber threat actors, including nation states, criminals, terrorists, hacktivists and insiders.
Attacks are becoming more sophisticated with regularly renewed techniques while the digital transformation amplifies exposure to these cyber threats.

The adoption of new technologies, such as the Internet of Things (IoT) or the migration to the cloud, as well as the evolution of architectures for increasingly interconnected systems, are all areas where cybersecurity is a very important issue.

The Group and its service providers may not be able to prevent third-parties from breaking into the Group’s IT systems, disrupting business operations or communications infrastructure through denial of service, attacks, or gaining access to confidential or sensitive information held in the system.
The Group, like many companies, has been and expects to continue to be the target of attempted cybersecurity attacks.

While the Group has not experienced any such attack that has had a material impact on its business and results of operations, the Group cannot guarantee that its security measures will be sufficient to prevent a material disruption, breach, or compromise in the future which could negatively and significantly affect the Company, its reputation and results of operations.

As a result, the Group’s activities and assets could sustain serious damage, services to clients could be interrupted, material intellectual property could be divulged and, in some cases, personal injury, property damage, environmental harm and regulatory violations could occur.

f) Violations of data protection laws carry fines and expose the Company and/or its employees to criminal sanctions and civil suits Data protection laws and regulations apply to Eni and its joint ventures and associates in most countries in which they do business.
The General Data Protection Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR) came into effect in May 2018 and increased penalties up to a maximum of 4% of global annual turnover for breach of the regulation.
The GDPR requires mandatory breach notification, a standard also followed outside the EU (particularly in Asia).
Non-compliance with data protection laws could expose Eni to regulatory investigations, which could result in fines and penalties as well as harm the Company’s reputation.
In addition to imposing fines, regulators may also issue orders to stop processing personal data, which could disrupt operations.
The Company could also be subject to litigation from persons or corporations allegedly affected by data protection violations.
Violation of data protection laws is a criminal offence in some countries, and individuals can be imprisoned or fined.

If any of the risks set out above materialize, they could adversely impact the Group’s results of operations, cash flow, liquidity, business prospects, financial condition, and shareholder returns, including dividends, the amount of funds available for stock repurchases and the price of Eni’s shares.

g) Eni is exposed to treasury and trading risks, including liquidity risk, interest rate risk, foreign exchange risk, commodity price risk and credit risk and may incur substantial losses in connection with those risks Eni’s business is exposed to the risk that changes in interest rates, foreign exchange rates or the prices of energy commodities will adversely affect the value of assets, liabilities or expected future cash flows.
The Group does not hedge its exposure to volatile hydrocarbons prices in its business of developing and extracting hydrocarbons reserves and other types of commodity exposures (e.g.
exposure to the volatility of refining margins and of certain portions of the gas long-term supply portfolio) except for specific markets or business conditions.
The Group has established risk management procedures and enters financial derivatives contracts to hedge its exposures to different commodity indexations and to currency and interest rates risks.
However, hedging may not function as expected.
In addition, Eni undertakes commodity derivatives contracts to optimize commercial margins or with a view of profiting from expected movements in market prices.
Those derivatives may or may not be risk-reducing.

Although Eni believes it has established sound risk management procedures to monitor and control commodity trading, this activity involves elements of forecasting and Eni is exposed to the risk of incurring significant losses if prices develop contrary to management expectations and to the risk of default of counterparties.

Eni is exposed to the risks of unfavorable movements in the Euro vs. the U.S. dollar exchange rates primarily because Eni’s consolidated financial statements are prepared in Euros, whereas Eni’s main subsidiaries in the Exploration & Production sector are utilizing the U.S. dollar as their functional currency.
This translation risk is unhedged.

132 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX the decrease in U.S. dollar-denominated expenses and may also result in significant translation adjustments that impact Eni’s shareholders’ equity.

In 2025, the Euro appreciated considerably versus the U.S. dollar (the average exchange rate for the year rose by 4.4%) and that trend negatively and significantly affected our reported results of operations and cash flow by an estimated €0.5 billion amount.

The appreciation recorded on the last day of the year of the Euro vs. the U.S. dollar exchange rate was even larger than the average (up 15%) and reduced the Group’s net equity by an estimated €6.4 billion, negatively affecting balance sheet ratios.
Eni’s credit ratings are exposed to risk from possible reductions of the sovereign credit rating of Italy.

Based on the methodologies used by Standard & Poor’s and Moody’s, a potential downgrade of Italy’s credit rating may have a potential knock-on effect on the credit rating of Italian issuers such as Eni and make it more likely that the credit rating of the debt instruments issued by us could be downgraded.
Eni is exposed to credit risk.
Eni’s counterparties could default, could be unable to pay the amounts owed to us in a timely manner or meet their performance obligations under contractual arrangements.
These events could cause the Company to recognize loss provisions with respect to amounts owed to it by debtors and cashflow shortfall.

Liquidity risk is the risk that suitable sources of funding for the Group may not be available, or that the Group is unable to sell its assets on the marketplace to meet short-term financial requirements and to settle obligations.

Such a situation would negatively affect the Group’s results of operations and cash flows as it would result in Eni incurring higher borrowing expenses to meet its obligations or, under the worst conditions, the inability of Eni to continue as a going concern.

If any of the risks set out above materialize, this could adversely impact the Group’s results of operations, cash flow, liquidity, business prospects, financial condition, and shareholders returns, including dividends, the amount of funds available for stock repurchases and the price of Eni’s shares.

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 137 Executive summary In a global context marked by increasingly complex energy, environmental and social challenges, Eni recognises energy as a key enabler of development and inclusion.

In line with its Mission and Code of Ethics, the Company integrates a responsible approach into its industrial activities, with the aim of creating long-term value for stakeholders and the communities in which it operates.

In 2025, Eni reaffirmed its commitment to achieving Carbon Neutrality by 2050; key action areas include the reduction of GHG emissions (Scope 1, 2 and 3), the development and deployment of mitigation technologies, the expansion of its portfolio of lower carbon products and services, and the offsetting of residual emissions.

Eni also assesses climate‑related risks and opportunities through medium and long‑term scenario analyses and remains committed to transparent climate-related disclosure and ongoing dialogue with stakeholders.

Eni has defined its strategy to provide for a progressive diversification of its energy portfolio, leveraging a range of industrial and technological solutions aimed at the gradual reduction of GHG emissions and the development of lower‑emission‑intensity solutions including renewable power generation, biofuels and renewable‑based chemicals, alongside the contribution of natural gas.

Environmental topics, together with Health and Safety, are managed through an integrated HSE framework which, supported by certified management systems, clearly defines roles, responsibilities and operating processes for environmental management across all business sectors.

Eni promotes the responsible management of natural resources by adopting innovative solutions to limit the impacts of its activities, increasing material recovery in line with circular economy principles, reducing the use of virgin and non‑renewable resources, and fostering a shared sustainability culture through targeted awareness‑raising initiatives.
On social matters, Eni continues to place strong emphasis on respect for human rights across the value chain and in relation to workers, communities and customers.
The Company’s approach has been recognised as particularly robust and structured by the World Benchmarking Alliance, which ranked Eni first in the Corporate Human Rights Benchmark1.
For its people, Eni continued to support skills development through upskilling and reskilling initiatives aligned with the Company’s industrial transition and transformation.
Efforts on diversity and inclusion led to Eni SpA obtaining gender-equality certification under UNI/PdR 125:2022.

In the Countries where it operates, Eni carries out initiatives aimed at supporting the sustainable development of local communities, with projects dedicated to access to energy and water, local economic growth, education and environmental protection.
In 2025, these initiatives reached approximately 3 million people.

Through Plenitude, Eni also provides energy, energy-efficiency solutions and electric‑mobility services to 10 million customers, integrating renewable power generation, sale of energy and an extensive charging‑point network.

In 2025, the Company further strengthened the engagement of its supply chain in a continuous improvement path through initiatives such as Open‑es2, financial support via Sustainable Supply Chain Finance, and the Supply Chain Award to share best practices and recognise the most innovative companies in ESG performance.
Furthermore, sustainability criteria have been increasingly embedded across all procurement phases.
From a governance perspective, 2025 saw continued compliance checks on risk‑exposed activities and further training and awareness initiatives on anti‑corruption.
To further promote the evolution and progressive strengthening of risk and control systems across the value chain, Eni launched the ‘G•row - Evolving through Risk & Control Governance’ initiative.
Eni’s ESG commitments and actions are structurally embedded within its Business Model, supporting the evolution and growth of its activities while creating value for stakeholders.
For an overview of Eni’s business strategy — within which the sustainability strategy is integrated — refer to the Strategy section.

(1) The CHRB assesses approximately 100 companies operating across five high-risk sectors and examines their performance across five measurement areas: corporate policy commitments, board‑level accountability, integration of respect for human rights into corporate culture and management systems, human rights due diligence, and grievance and remedy mechanisms.

(2) Initiative launched in 2021 with Boston Consulting Group and Google Cloud, a platform designed to support companies in measuring and improving ESG performance, with the aim of fostering value creation and generating positive impacts across the broader business ecosystem.

Alongside the environmental and climate sustainability of the energy mix, the need to ensure security of supply and affordable access to energy is becoming ever more urgent — the dimensions of what is commonly referred to as the energy trilemma.

The energy transition — built on the growing integration of renewable sources, low-emission technologies and more efficient and innovative solutions — is shaped by geographical specificities, supporting policies, technological evolution and trends in energy demand.
The energy system is also closely interconnected with environmental and social issues such as biodiversity, water‑resource management and community well‑being.
Access to energy remains a critical challenge in many regions, particularly in Sub‑Saharan Africa.

At the same time, climate change and pressures on ecosystems are affecting the availability of natural resources, with around half of the world’s population exposed to periods of severe water stress each year.

In this complex context — which requires concrete and differentiated solutions capable of responding to the needs of each territory — Eni is actively committed to a Just Transition, considered one of the energy sector’s key strategic challenges.

The transition is deeply linked to technological innovation and requires industrial capability and collaboration across the value chain, with the aim of generating benefits for people and communities involved in transformation processes.

For this reason, Eni operates responsibly, engaging stakeholders and working to prevent and mitigate potential social and environmental impacts associated with both traditional activities and new energy projects.

The industrial and financial transformation initiated in 2014 enables the Company to respond more effectively to global challenges and to develop a strategy aimed at progressively implementing more environmentally, socially and economically sustainable solutions.
Within this framework lies Eni’s pathway towards Carbon Neutrality by 2050, supported by intermediate milestones and based on scientific research, innovation and diversification of energy sources.
At the same time, Eni promotes a strong health and safety culture focused on risk prevention and the protection of people — employees and contractors — and company assets.
Human capital is further enhanced through continuous skills development, essential to meet the evolving demands of the sector.
The Company also commits to transparency, combating corruption and working collaboratively with partners, suppliers and customers, supporting them in their sustainable development journey.

In the Countries where it operates, Eni contributes to local growth through development projects carried out in collaboration with national and international development‑cooperation actors, as well as through activation of local supply chains, provision of energy to local markets, training initiatives and capacity‑building activities.

These commitments form the foundation of Eni’s Business Model, within which sustainability is embedded through five levers: Carbon neutrality by 2050, Environmental protection, Value of our people, Alliances for development and Sustainability in the value chain.

140 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX To implement its mission and achieve the targets and commitments undertaken, Eni has developed a structured Internal Regulatory System3 governing all corporate activities and clearly defining roles and responsibilities.
A central pillar of this framework is the Code of Ethics4, updated in 2020.
This document not only sets out the Company’s core values — including integrity, respect for human rights, transparency and innovation — establishes concrete standards of conduct.

Its application extends across all levels of the organisation, from members of corporate bodies to Eni employees and those of its subsidiaries and also includes external partners such as suppliers and consultants.

On this basis, the broader regulatory system is articulated through the following key documents: (i) ECG Policies5: public documents approved in their fundamental principles, by the Board of Director (hereinafter referred to as the BoD), which define the rules applicable to Eni SpA and its subsidiaries; (ii) Management System Guidelines (MSGs): internal documents that guide the management of processes at Eni SpA and its subsidiaries.
These are developed with the involvement of internal stakeholders, approved by process owners and underpin all corporate processes.

142 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX STATEMENT ON DUE DILIGENCE [ESRS 2 GOV-4] Eni has long established corporate management processes and systems covering social, environmental, climate‑related and business conduct matters, inspired by leading industry standards.

These processes and systems are integrated into the Company’s governance and strategic framework to ensure compliance with applicable national and international regulations and to promote responsible business practices across Eni’s operations.

With specific reference to human rights, Eni’s due diligence process is aligned with the United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights and the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises.

With regard to climate‑related due diligence, this has been explicitly recognised in the OECD Guidelines (OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises on Responsible Business Conduct - June 2023); to date, its implementation is influenced by the absence of prescriptive regulatory requirements and consolidated reference best practices8 and its application therefore remains subject to interpretation.

In this context, based on the analyses carried out, Eni considers its approach to be substantially aligned with the principles set out by the OECD, as described in the Climate Change chapter, while continuing to monitor developments in the relevant regulatory framework and emerging best practices.

To facilitate consultation of the Sustainability Statement, a mapping of the information disclosed with respect to the due diligence process is presented below, noting that certain activities may not be attributable exclusively to a single phase of the due diligence process as outlined.

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 143 STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT [ESRS 2 SBM-2] Eni considers stakeholder engagement and dialogue to be a key enabler for achieving its sustainability objectives, as set out in its Strategic and long‑term Plan, as well as for preventing and mitigating potential negative impacts of the energy transition, including impacts on the workforce.

In line with the Code of Ethics, Eni fosters relationships with stakeholders based on fairness, legality, transparency, traceability, respect for human rights, inclusion, gender equality, and the protection of the environment and local communities.

Operating in 62 Countries characterised by diverse socioeconomic contexts, understanding the expectations of stakeholders affected by the Group’s activities, particularly vulnerable groups, and maintaining continuous, transparent dialogue are essential to building strong and trust‑based relationships.
This dialogue is supported by the disclosure of corporate objectives and results and by ongoing stakeholder participation.

This approach is consistent with the recommendations of the Corporate Governance Code adopted by Eni, under which the BoD promotes dialogue with shareholders and other relevant stakeholders through appropriate channels, based on the principles defined by the Board itself, the Code of Ethics and the Policy on investor engagement.
In relation to these groups of right-holders, Eni has developed a human rights management model that is integrated into its core corporate processes.
To support engagement with local stakeholders, Eni as adopted a ‘Stakeholder Management System’9 (SMS), operating at both central and subsidiary level.
The system maps over 7,400 stakeholders and supports the definition of engagement strategies, as well as the management of requests and issues raised by stakeholders.
The table below provides, for each stakeholder category, information on objectives, activities carried out during the year, and the outcomes achieved through this dialogue.
Stakeholder feedback is taken into account in defining the Company’s strategy and Other ESG commitments and targets, as well as of the Business Model.

Eni adopts differentiated engagement tools and levels of stakeholder engagement — including workshops, institutional meetings, consultations, training programmes, information events, collaborations with other entities, dedicated channels, and partnerships for sustainable development and innovation — managed by dedicated corporate functions with clearly defined responsibilities for each stakeholder category.
This approach varies according to stakeholder type and ensures structured, coherent dialogue in line with the principles of the Code of Ethics.

In order to share Eni’s approach to Sustainability and its Energy Transition pathway with the various reference stakeholders, in 2025 numerous initiatives were implemented, both at local level (including Ravenna, Côte d’Ivoire, Mexico and Mozambique) and at central level (Gazometro in Rome and Brussels), also leveraging voluntary sustainability reports, published at local level.
To further facilitate interaction, Eni has established a single Whistleblowing Reporting Platform, shared with its subsidiaries and accessible both online and via the intranet.
The platform allows all internal and external stakeholders to report, including anonymously, any suspected violations of the Code of Ethics.
Further details are provided in the Business Conduct section.

146 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX PROCESS AND RESULTS OF THE DOUBLE MATERIALITY ASSESSMENT [ESRS 2 IRO-1; GOV-2] The 2025 materiality assessment, aimed at identifying the sustainability topics that are most relevant to Eni and its stakeholders, builds on the results of the 2024 assessment, which was already carried out in accordance with the ESRS and the double materiality principle.

Under this principle, Eni identifies its most significant impacts — both positive and negative, actual and potential — on the environment and people, including human rights (the ‘impact materiality’ or ‘inside-out’ perspective), as well as the sustainability-related risks and opportunities that may significantly affect the Company’s development, performance and financial position over the short, medium or long-term (the ‘financial materiality’ or ‘outside-in’ perspective).

During 2025, changes in both the Group’s business activities and the external context were assessed; these analyses did not identify the emergence of new material topics compared to the previous reporting cycle, confirming the continued relevance of the materiality outcomes identified in the prior year.

To further validate the materiality results related to the Company’s activities, the topics and concerns of the different stakeholder categories were reviewed through the involvement of the corporate functions responsible for managing the relevant relationships, in order to assess whether any new aspects not identified in the previous cycle had emerged (see Stakeholder Engagement).

With regard to impacts classified as non-material in 2024, additional in-depth analyses were carried out, also involving corporate functions and companies representing the different business segments: these impacts were confirmed as non-material also for 2025.

From a disclosure perspective, in order to enhance the usability of information, the presentation of social impacts was updated through a greater level of aggregation compared to the 2024 Eni Sustainability Statement, without any changes to the underlying content.
The financial materiality assessment was updated based on the results of the Integrated Risk Management process (hereinafter, IRM), which did not identify changes in material risks compared to 2024.

In addition, a consistency check was performed on the opportunities identified as material in the previous year against the current Strategic Plan, in order to assess their continued validity for 2025.

Finally, building on the value chain materiality analysis carried out in the previous reporting cycle, targeted in‑depth analyses of potential environmental impacts were conducted during 2025, as described below.

Phases of the materiality process Eni’s materiality process, defined in the previous reporting year and confirmed as valid for the current year, is structured around the following phases: • IdentificationofthelistofpotentiallymaterialtopicsrelatedtoEni’sactivitiesanditsvaluechain,bothupstreamanddownstream, based on a top‑down approach.

This approach considered the Company’s objectives, insights from benchmarking and contextual analyses, the requirements set out in the ESRS and the relevant GRI sector standards, as well as the outcomes of the human rights due diligence process, including the update of Eni’s mapping of the so called Salient Human Rights Issues10 (see Human Rights for Eni).
The analysis also took into account Eni’s materiality assessments carried out in the previous year, at Group level, and by subsidiaries that prepared a sustainability report.
In addition, to reflect stakeholders’ interests, priority topics identified during the year by the corporate functions engaging with the different stakeholder categories were considered.
• Identificationofimpacts,risksandopportunities(IROs)associatedwithpotentiallymaterialtopics.
For the impact assessment, publicly available sources were reviewed, and relevant internal functions were involved.
Based on their experience in the respective areas of responsibility, they identified impacts related to the Company’s activities, considering also relevant aspects across the value chain.
During the analysis, impacts were also disaggregated, where necessary, in order to identify specific geographical areas or business segments in which such impacts are particularly significant.

The process for identifying risks associated with potentially material topics is based on the broader IRM process (see Integrated Risk Management), under which risks are identified and assessed in relation to the achievement of Eni’s main objectives.

The outcomes of the assessment also include risks related to ESG topics, including those arising from dependencies on natural, human and social resources, as well as risks linked to impacts on the environment and people.
Opportunities were identified with reference to the Strategic Plan, with a focus on initiatives that are effectively being pursued by the Company.

• Definitionofthemodelfortheassessmentofimpacts,risksandopportunities(IROs), under which, in accordance with the ESRS and the EFRAG Guidance on materiality, assessment scales were defined, based on specific drivers depending on the topic, as follows: (i) for impact materiality, impacts are assessed in terms of significance, determined through a combination of scale, scope and irremediable character (the latter applicable exclusively to negative impacts), and the likelihood of occurrence11.

The negative impact related to climate change is considered material based on recognised scientific consensus; (ii) for financial materiality, in line with the Integrated Risk Management framework, risks are assessed by combining the likelihood of occurrence with the magnitude of effects, measured using both quantitative and qualitative metrics.

These include, for example, economic and financial metrics, based on reductions in operating cash flow or net profit, (10) Limited to social topics, the analysis considered the mapping of so-called Salient Human Rights Issues and therefore includes potential negative impacts on the most significant human rights matters, in line with the provisions of the relevant international instruments.
The reporting of impacts actually verified during the year is provided in the ‘Actions’ sections within the various social thematic chapters.
(11) The probability of current impacts was not assessed, as the impact has already occurred.
MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 147 and reputational metrics, assessed in terms of the duration of reputational impacts and the stakeholders affected.

For both perspectives, the model provides likelihood assessments as well as evaluations of significance (for impact materiality) and magnitude of effects (for financial materiality), using rating scales ranging from 1 to 5.

For opportunities, relevance is assessed by combining likelihood and relevance; relevance is evaluated through both a qualitative scale, defined by two levels of significance, and a quantitative scale based on capital expenditure (CapEx) and operating expenditure (OpEx).
With regard to likelihood, a two‑dimensional probability scale is applied, with the highest level attributed to opportunities included in the Strategic Plan.

• AssessmentofthesignificanceoftheIROs: (i) for impact materiality, the assessment was carried out by the relevant corporate functions through an IT platform12 that enables the traceability of the evaluation process.
Subsequently, the companies representing the different business segments were involved to identify and assess any additional impacts specific to their activities/sectors13.

Based on the overall assessments, impacts were classified as material where they exceeded the internally defined materiality threshold, as determined through a two-dimensional matrix combining likelihood and significance.

These correspond to Tier 1 and Tier 2 impacts, out of a total of three tiers; (ii) for financial materiality, risks are assessed in terms of likelihood and magnitude of effects and represented within a matrix structured into three tiers (Tier 1, Tier 2 and Tier 3, in descending order of relevance); risks classified within Tier 1 and Tier 2 represent Eni’s main risks (Top Risks - see the Integrated Risk Management section) and are therefore considered material for the purposes of financial materiality.

The assessments are based on data and assumptions that vary depending on the nature of the risk and, where available and relevant, leverage both historical data on past events and forward‑looking estimates, including those developed with the support of relevant functions (e.g.

The scope of risk assessment activities is defined by selecting the subsidiaries included in the assessment process, based on qualitative and quantitative drivers, in order to ensure adequate coverage of the Company’s strategic objectives.

The assessment of opportunities is conducted through an integrated process, involving the Sustainability, Integrated Risk Management and Strategic Planning functions, with a focus on consistency with corporate plans and with initiatives that are already implemented or planned.
Based on the assessment of strategic and economic relevance, only opportunities classified as Tier 1 were considered.

• Discussion of the impact materiality results emerging from the assessment, through direct engagement with experts, via dedicated meetings, on the topics assessed and/or on CSRD matters, including international sustainability organisations, audit and consulting firms and financial institutions.
• Definition of the list of material IROs and calibration of the results, based on the assessments carried out and, where applicable, on predefined thresholds.
This process involved the prioritisation of the impacts, risks and opportunities relevant to the Company and the exclusion of all IROs below the materiality threshold.

The outcomes of the analysis were then reviewed from an overall perspective, also taking into account inputs gathered from subject-matter experts, in order to refine the final list of impacts and opportunities, where necessary.

The results of the analysis, with specific reference to material IROs, were shared14 with the Control and Risk Committee, the Sustainability and Scenarios Committee and the Board of Statutory Auditors, and with the BoD in occasion of the approval of the Sustainability Statement.

150 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX VALUE CHAIN AND MAIN IMPACTS Eni is present across the entire energy value chain, from the exploration, development and extraction of resources15 to the marketing of energy, products and services to end customers (for further details see Activities).

Through its Business model, Eni combines the use of largely proprietary technologies, with the development of an innovative satellite model, based on the establishment of dedicated companies capable of independently accessing markets to finance their growth.

In Eni’s operating context, the ability to build a network of partnerships and alliances with stakeholders and host Countries is a key factor in ensuring their involvement in the Company’s activities and in the complex process of energy system transformation.

From this perspective, Eni develops and manages its operations in close interconnection with upstream and downstream actors along the value chain, while monitoring the potentially significant impacts that may arise from these interconnections.

In fact, to identify the most significant impacts across its value chain, the materiality assessment did not focus exclusively on Eni’s direct activities — which already cover a substantial portion of the energy value chain — but also included, starting from the previous reporting cycle, dedicated in‑depth analyses of the value chain.
For 2025, the results of the analysis carried out in the previous reporting cycle were confirmed.
This analysis focused on impacts that occur broadly across the entire value chain, regardless of business segment, specific activities or geographical areas.

In particular, among the impacts identified, the relevance of climate change both upstream and downstream is confirmed for the environment topic, with the main emission‑related impacts concentrated in the use phase of the products and services sold (Scope 3 emissions; for further details see Climate Change/Metrics).

With regard to social matters, material impacts were identified in relation to human rights and workforce health and safety, particularly within so‑called labour‑intensive supply‑chain activities, such as plant construction and maintenance and general services16.
Along the downstream value chain, potential impacts emerged in the interactions with customers and consumers, mainly linked to unclear commercial or communication practices.
Finally, in the area of governance, impacts were identified in relation to cybersecurity, loss of information confidentiality and corruption episodes, particularly in upstream activities.

Conversely, potential positive impacts include support for the sustainable growth of the business ecosystem through the dissemination of sustainability principles, the promotion of sustainable energy use among customers and consumers, and the development of initiatives aimed at protecting the health of value chain workers and promoting community health.
These initiatives are implemented in partnership with actors along the value chain and other relevant stakeholders.
Moreover, during 2025, the value chain analysis was expanded and deepened to identify additional potential environmental impacts specific to the Oil & Gas value chain.

This in-depth analysis, focused on strategic suppliers within the value chain, was developed through multiple streams: (i) analysis of sustainability information published by companies; (ii) direct engagement with selected suppliers through interviews; (iii) identification of the outcomes of suppliers’ materiality analyses available via Open‑es17; and (iv) analysis of selected data providers, such as Encore, which, depending on the sector, support the identification of environmental impacts, risks and dependencies.

Based on the outcomes of these analyses, in addition to the topics already identified in the previous year, further potential environmental impacts were considered material for Eni’s value chain where they consistently emerged across all analytical streams (as reported in the materiality results table).

In particular, these include aspects related to potential water and soil pollution from accidental spills and potential impacts on biodiversity, both associated with drilling activities, plant construction, the manufacture of components and equipment, chemical production and maritime transport carried out by suppliers on behalf of Eni.

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 151 The resilience of the strategy to material IROs [ESRS 2 SBM-3] The assessment of the resilience of the strategy with respect to material impacts, risks and opportunities is integrated into the process for defining the Strategic Plan, starting from the preparation of the proposal, taking into account the underlying risk profile, through to its review by the BoD.
The BoD is required to assess the degree of compatibility of the identified risks with a management approach that is consistent with the strategic objectives set.

The assessment of the risks compatibility with strategic objectives is supported by the Integrated Risk Management process, which provides a comprehensive view of the Company’s main risks, including those related to sustainability matters, and their evaluation, taking into account the mitigation actions implemented.

The risk profile underlying the Strategic Plan is further analysed through an integrated assessment of the effects of risks on financial objectives, as well as through the analysis of the Plan’s de-risking actions related to the Company’s strategic risks.

In addition, the process for defining the Strategic Plan, incorporates considerations regarding measures to mitigate negative impacts, as well as aspects concerning the achievability of positive impacts and material opportunities identified, to ensure the achievement of the defined objectives.
With regard to mitigation actions, the Company adopts a set of measures designed to reduce material impacts and its exposure to the main risks to which it is subject.
These measures are summarised in the Integrated Risk Management chapter and in the individual thematic chapters of the Sustainability Statement.

Eni also carries out dedicated scenario analyses to assess the resilience of its strategy with respect to climate‑related impacts and risks (as detailed in the Climate Change chapter), as well as similar analyses concerning nature‑related and Biodiversity risks.
For social matters, Eni has implemented corporate Due Diligence processes and systems aligned with relevant frameworks and best practices.
These enable the Company to identify potential negative impacts related to its operations, value chain, products or services and business relationships (see Human Rights for Eni).

152 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX Climate Change [ESRS E1] POLICIES [ESRS 2 MDR-P; E1-2] As stated in its Mission, Eni ‘concretely supports a just energy transition […] and promotes efficient a sustainable access to energy’; this commitment is reflected in the company’s policies, as well as in the Code of Ethics, and is substantively implemented through the definition and execution of a Decarbonisation Plan.

POLICY KEY CONTENTS: Code of Ethics • Commitment to contribute positively to the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals and to support a lower carbon and socially just energy transition, also through innovative solutions aimed at reducing the impact of its operations by promoting the efficient use of natural resources, the protection of biodiversity and water resources, and by supporting mitigation and adaptation actions in the local contexts in which it operates.
• Commitment, as a signatory to the Paris Pledge, to support the objectives set out in the Paris Agreement.

Supplier Code of Conduct • Requirement for its suppliers to commit to environmental protection, optimisation of resource use and contribution to the achievement of plant efficiency and emission reduction targets, thereby supporting Eni along its pathway towards Carbon Neutrality to 2050.

Internal Regulatory System • Specific procedures defining, in line with the main international standards, the methodologies for emissions reporting (for further information, please refer to the Metrics section).
CLIMATE GOVERNANCE [ESRS 2 GOV-3; E1 GOV-3] The decarbonisation plan forms an integral part of Eni’s business strategy, in line with the Company’s Mission.

Under the adopted governance model, the management of the Company is entrusted by the Board of Directors (BoD) to the Chief Executive Officer (CEO), with the exception of the most significant strategic, operational and organisational powers, which remain reserved to the BoD.
Within this framework, both the CEO and the BoD play a central role in managing key aspects related to climate change.

The CEO is responsible for defining the climate strategy and, following its approval by the BoD, for implementing it and its related decarbonisation targets, ensuring their integration into the Strategic Plan and corporate decision‑making processes.

The BoD reviews and approves, upon proposal of the CEO, the Strategic Plan, which includes industrial business targets as well as financial results and sustainability objectives, including decarbonisation targets.

In carrying out these responsibilities, the BoD is supported by the Sustainability and Scenarios Committee (SSC), an internal Board committee dedicated to assisting the Board in its assessments and decisions regarding processes, initiatives and activities aimed at overseeing the Company’s commitment to sustainable development across the entire value chain.
For the topics examined by the SSC during the year and additional details on the role of the corporate bodies, please refer to the Governance section.

The effective implementation of the corporate strategy is further supported by Eni’s Remuneration Policy through incentive systems designed for Directors, General Managers, Executives with strategic responsibilities and other Executive Managers.
This target has a weighting of 20% for the CEO, while for management the weighting varies according to assigned responsibilities.
For further information, please refer to the Report on the 2026 Remuneration Policy and Remuneration paid 202518.
Eni’s organisational structure is also evolving in line with its energy‑transition path.

Lastly, an equally important aspect of the transition process is Eni’s engagement with policymakers — both directly and through industry associations — to contribute, in line with respective roles and responsibilities, and drawing on its experience as an international energy company, to the development of strategies and regulations aimed at promoting the path towards Carbon Neutrality by 2050 (see Business Conduct).

(18) The remuneration linked to the Short‑Term and Long‑Term Incentive Plans for 2025 was paid on the basis of the performance of the Net Carbon Footprint Upstream indicator, defined on an equity boundary (see Metrics).

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3; E1 IRO-1] Material impacts The process carried out by Eni to identify its climate‑related impacts considered scientific19, regulatory20 and guidance references21, all of which indicate that GHG‑emission estimation is the parameter used to assess the company’s negative outward impact.

Aware of the need for a collective response to the global decarbonisation challenge, Eni has long been committed to a pathway for reducing GHG emissions towards the goal of Carbon Neutrality by 2050 (see Decarbonisation Plan).

In line with major international standards and industry best practices22, Eni has implemented internal procedures for identifying emission sources and defining methodologies for calculating direct and indirect GHG emissions.
These calculations are based on assessments carried out by operators at individual industrial sites and subsequently consolidated centrally (bottom‑up approach).
Through the mapping of emission sources, Eni has established an inventory of actual direct and indirect GHG emissions.
To ensure that the data‑collection and control process is robust and structured, specific procedures and adequate controls have been implemented within the Sustainability Statement control system.
The Group’s emission profile is also assessed on a forward‑looking basis by estimating potential GHG emissions in line with the Strategic Plan projected to 2050.
For further details, please refer to the Metrics section.

PROCESS FOR PRIORITISING EMISSIONS IMPACTS Since the early 2000s, Eni has developed a structured GHG emission inventory that includes detailed information, among other aspects, by business type and emission source.
This inventory enables the identification of priority areas for intervention, while also considering the evolution of the international climate debate.

For example, decisions taken in international forums — including those adopted at the Conferences of the Parties (COP) — as well as energy and climate‑scenario analyses and United Nations studies ‑ such as the scientific reports published by the IPCC23 ‑ provide indications on the main levers available for decarbonisation (see Main reference scenarios).
Analysis of the external context highlights a clear orientation towards prioritising actions that reduce Scope 1 and 2 emissions, for which technologically feasible mitigation options exist.

This also includes the reduction of methane emissions — a greenhouse gas with a higher global‑warming potential24 than carbon dioxide — for which economically and technically viable solutions are already available.

Consistent with this approach and with the assessment of its own emissions inventory, Eni has, since 2016, defined a series of targets aimed at improving the GHG emission performance of its assets, with a specific focus on methane and flaring.
Subsequently, starting in 2020, Eni established a decarbonisation pathway that also includes targets for indirect emissions, with the aim of reaching Carbon Neutrality by 2050.

This extension reflects an incremental approach: after first addressing emissions directly under its control (Scope 1 and 2), Eni progressively expanded its focus to indirect value chain emissions, which by their nature can only be managed indirectly.
The process for identifying, assessing and prioritising emissions impacts is updated annually.
Over the years, this process has been progressively refined, in line with the maturation of scientific evidence and growing global awareness of climate issues.

Material risks Climate‑related risk refers to the possibility that changes in climate scenarios or conditions may generate transition risks (market, regulatory and legal, technological and reputational) and physical risks (acute and chronic) affecting Eni’s businesses in the short, medium and long‑ term.

Risks associated with the implementation of the initiatives included in the Strategic Plan are also considered, as these may influence the achievement of decarbonisation targets in terms of timing, performance or economic viability ‑ for example, in cases of delays in delivering industrial projects, technological under‑performance or higher‑than‑expected development costs.

The process for identifying and assessing climate‑related risks is part of Eni’s Integrated Risk Management model, as described in the section Process and results of the double materiality assessment.

This process ensures the identification, consolidation and analysis of Eni’s risks and supports the Board of Directors in verifying the compatibility of the company’s risk profile with strategic objectives – including from a medium to long‑term perspective – while monitoring the evolution of key risks and de‑risking actions.

Risks, including climate change, are assessed considering both the likelihood of occurrence and the potential effects on Eni’s quantitative and qualitative objectives that would materialise if the risk were to occur.
Based on the metrics defined for risk assessment, climate change is considered by Eni to be a top risk.

(19) The prevailing position of the scientific community identifies GHG emissions as the main cause of climate change, while recognising that there is no linear relationship between GHG emissions and the resulting climate change impacts (see IPCC AR6 and, for example, Rial et al., 2004; Trudinger and Enting, 2005; Millar et al., 2017).
(20) As an example, the climate standard ESRS E1 identifies a company’s GHG emissions as an impact on climate change (Disclosure Requirement ESRS 2 IRO‑1, paragraph 20a).
(21) OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises on Responsible Business Conduct (2023).
(22) WBCSD/WRI GHG Protocol Initiative and the IPIECA/API/IOGP Petroleum Industry Guidelines for Reporting Greenhouse Gas Emissions (2011).

(23) The IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change), established in 1988 by the World Meteorological Organization (WMO) and the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP), is the leading international body for assessing climate change.
(24) It refers to the ability of a gas to persist in the atmosphere for a given period of time.
At the international level, a wide range of scenarios currently exists, developed by numerous analysts, consultants, organisations and energy-sector entities.
These scenarios rely on different construction methodologies and outline a broad spectrum of possible evolutions of the energy system.

They combine decarbonisation levers, technologies, assumptions on the evolution of consumption patterns and policy frameworks in different ways, in order to illustrate potential future trajectories and provide a useful reference framework for guiding strategic choices and policy directions.

In this context, given the absence of universally recognised decarbonisation pathways for the Oil & Gas sector, Eni analyses multiple prospective pathways characterised by heterogeneous mixes of solutions and milestones, among which those developed by the International Energy Agency (IEA) and the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) are particularly relevant.

Specifically, climate scenarios belonging to the IPCC C1-C2 categories were used as a reference for assessing the compatibility of Eni decarbonisation plan25 with trajectories aligned with the 1.5°C limit by 2100, while acknowledging the intrinsic limitations of comparing a corporate context with the global one (see Decarbonisation levers).

The IEA Net Zero Emissions (NZE) scenario was considered for assessing the resilience of Eni’s strategy to transition risks (see Resilience of the strategy to transition scenarios), whereas the IPCC SSP5-RCP8.5 (C8) scenario was used for the analysis of physical risks (see Physical risks).

Main reference scenarios IEA - Net Zero Emissions (NZE) Scenario: in the World Energy Outlook 2025, the IEA revised the assumptions underpinning the NZE scenario (the scenario targeting Net Zero emissions by 2050), shifting from a limited-overshoot pathway to a high-overshoot26 one.

In this scenario — for which the IEA provides numerical detail only at the global level — decarbonisation of the energy system is achieved primarily through increasing electrification and a major scale-up of variable renewables (whose share in the power-generation mix rises from today’s 15% to around 75% in 2050), improved energy efficiency, and rapid deployment of CO2 capture and storage technologies for industrial processes (CCUS), bioenergy with CCS (BECCS) and direct air capture (DACS).

In terms of energy mix, the NZE-IEA scenario features a substantial decline in the role of fossil fuels, whose share in the global mix drops from the current 79% to 16% by 2050, alongside a falling energy demand (0.5% per year on average between 2024 and 2050).

By 2050, the most affected energy source is coal, with emissions falling by 99% compared with 2024 (consumption down 92%), followed by natural gas (95% emissions; consumption down 80%) and oil (90% emissions; consumption down 77%).
Unlike the IEA, the IPCC develops climate scenarios27 by considering all GHG emissions, not only CO2 from the energy sector.
These scenarios are grouped into eight categories (C1-C8)28 based on the projected increase in global temperature by 2100 associated with each pathway.

Categories C1 and C2 include over 200 scenarios capable of limiting global warming to 1.5°C: (i) C1 includes scenarios with no or limited overshoot (with Net Zero GHG emissions reached between 2030 and 2100, depending on the scenario); (ii) C2 includes high-overshoot scenarios.
The decarbonisation levers and technologies deployed in these pathways, while broadly similar to those of the NZE-IEA scenario, are combined in different ways.
For example, electrification in these trajectories does not necessarily rely almost exclusively on variable renewables – it may also derive from an expanded role of nuclear power.

Some scenarios feature a greater use of CCUS and biofuels, which are particularly relevant for decarbonising hard-to-abate sectors, and incorporate land-use measures (e.g., reforestation and afforestation) as mitigation levers.
Overall CO2 emissions in the C1 category decline by 50% between 2019 and 2030 (range: −60% to −40%), whereas in the C2 category they fall by 5% (range: −5% to 0%).
For comparison, over the same period, the NZE-IEA 2025 scenario projects a decrease in global CO2 emissions of 19%29.

In the figure below30, which shows changes in emissions and fossil-fuel consumption between 2019 and 2030, IPCC scenarios display a wide variability in the evolution of Oil & Gas demand, which in C2 pathways is projected to increase by 2030.
By contrast, coal consumption is expected to decline in both categories by 2030.
(25) Defined on Entity-specific targets.
(26) An overshoot refers to the temporary exceeding of a given global-warming level, such as 1.5°C.

Overshoot implies a temperature peak followed by a subsequent decline in global warming, achieved through anthropogenic CO2 removals exceeding the remaining global CO2 emissions (source: IPCC Glossary, Special Report: Global Warming of 1.5°C).

Two types of overshoot are recognised: (i) ‘Limited’ overshoot, indicating an exceedance of up to about +0.1°C above 1.5°C (corresponding to warming of around 1.6°C); ‘High’ overshoot, indicating an exceedance of +0.1°C to +0.3°C (corresponding to warming between ~1.6°C and ~1.8°C).
In both cases, the exceedance persists for several decades (source: IPCC Climate Change Synthesis Report, 2023).
(27) Assessment Report 6, WGIII (2021).

(28) The categories range from very low emissions (C1) to very high emissions (C8), with intermediate categories limiting global warming at different thresholds: C2 at 1.5°C after high overshoot; C3-C4 at 2°C; C5 at 2.5°C; C6 at 3°C; C7 at 4°C (source: IPCC Climate Change Synthesis Report, 2023).
(29) Change in CO2 emissions: 2019 data from IEA, Global CO2 emissions from energy combustion and industrial processes and their annual change, 1900-2023 (link: https://www.

iea.org/data-and-statistics/charts/global-co2-emissions-from-energy-combustion-and-industrial-processes-and-their-annual-change-1900-2023); 2030 data from IEA, World Energy Outlook 2025, Table 7.1, p. 354.
(30) Data from the IPCC Assessment Report 6, WGIII (2021), Chapter 3, Table 3.6 (p. 353).

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 155 Finally, category C8 includes 29 scenarios that project a doubling of global GHG emissions relative to 2015 levels and a rise in average global temperature of more than 4°C.

These scenarios describe the potential increase in the frequency and intensity of certain acute and chronic meteorological and climatic phenomena (e.g., heatwaves, heavy precipitation, glacier retreat).
They assume an energy‑ intensive economic growth pattern (SSP5) and a high concentration of future GHG emissions (RCP8.531).

TRANSITION RISKS The context in which Eni operates is significantly influenced by global commitments to achieving carbon neutrality and by potential shifts in consumer preferences, which could lead to a structural decline in hydrocarbon demand over the medium‑to‑long‑term and to an increase in operating costs for the Oil & Gas sector.
Uncertainties regarding future demand trends and the feasibility or profitability of decarbonisation technologies make long‑term investment decisions inherently risky.

Furthermore, growing public scrutiny of climate issues and increasingly stringent stakeholder expectations may result in more limited access to capital markets and could challenge the licence to operate of Oil & Gas companies.

For a detailed contextual analysis of individual drivers or transition events (market, regulatory and legal, technological and reputational), please refer to the Risk factors and uncertainties section.
In response to these emerging trends, Eni identifies mitigation actions (see Actions taken on material IROs) aimed at minimising risks.
Specifically, Eni evaluates variables that may affect operating costs – such as carbon price signals – and monitors the resilience of its strategy against transition scenarios.

INTERNAL CARBON PRICING [E1-8] In 2025, Eni applied an internal carbon pricing mechanism (shadow price) to assess the economic and financial exposure of its investments to the potential introduction of carbon pricing systems in the Countries where it operates.

The analysis conducted in 2025 used the IEA’s Net Zero scenario (World Energy Outlook), adopting a 2050 price range differentiated by level of economic development (advanced economies, emerging economies with and without Net Zero commitments32).
For major investment projects, the expected return is subjected to a sensitivity analysis aimed at estimating the potential impacts of CO2 costs on project profitability.
This analysis is applied only where the Country’s current legislation does not already provide for an explicit carbon pricing system.

The results are reviewed by the Board of Directors both at the preliminary authorisation stage of each investment falling within its remit (Final Investment Decision ‑ FID) and subsequently as part of the annual project‑monitoring process.

(31) RCP (Representative Concentration Pathways): scenarios that include time series of emissions and concentrations of all greenhouse gases, aerosols and chemically active gases, as well as land‑use/land‑cover changes.
The term ‘representative’ indicates that each RCP provides only one of many possible scenarios that could lead to specific radiative‑forcing characteristics (W m‑2).
The term ‘pathway’ highlights that not only the long‑term concentration levels are relevant, but also the trajectory followed over time to reach these outcomes.
In particular, RCP 8.5 represents a high‑emission scenario that leads to an increase in radiative forcing exceeding 8.5 W/m² by 2100 (source: IPCC Glossary).
(32) The range of values is between 180‑250 USD/tCO2 (real 2024 terms).
For emerging and developing economies (other than Brazil, China, India and South Africa), lower carbon price values are assumed, ranging between 55‑180 USD/tCO2.

156 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX RESILIENCE OF THE STRATEGY TO TRANSITION SCENARIOS [ESRS 2 IRO-1] The Group’s strategic‑planning and investment‑selection/monitoring processes are designed to identify the actions needed to maximise asset value while considering the risks and opportunities associated with the energy transition.

Within this framework, action and spending plans are regularly defined to achieve short, medium and long‑term decarbonisation targets, based on a set of base‑case assumptions concerning the speed of transformation of the energy system and the resulting impacts on prices.
Progress towards the targets is systematically monitored and reported.

One of the tools used ex post to support management in understanding Eni’s exposure to transition risk is the sensitivity analysis of Oil & Gas asset values to alternative price scenarios compared with the base case.

The analysis assesses the variability of asset values and the potential risk of distributing unrealised earnings under stress conditions, namely: (i) a linear 10% reduction in hydrocarbon prices over all years of projected cash flows; (ii) a one‑percentage‑point increase in the discount rate (adjusted WACC) used to determine the net present value of assets in each Country of operation; (iii) application of the IEA Net Zero Emissions 2050 (NZE‑IEA 205033) scenario for hydrocarbon‑price and CO2 cost projections.
The overall value‑in‑use of Oil & Gas properties calculated under the various stress‑test scenarios shows a margin (‘headroom’) above the carrying amounts.
Under the NZE‑IEA 2050 scenario in particular, the headroom stands at 18%, with potential impairments of €3.4 billion.
For further details on the analysis and related results, see Note 15 of the consolidated financial statements.
PHYSICAL RISKS Physical risks arising from climate change may result from single (acute) events or from long‑term (chronic) changes in climatic phenomena.

These risks may generate financial implications for companies, including direct damage to assets and indirect impacts due to disruptions to their own operations or those across the value chain, leading to loss of output and cash flow as well as increased restoration and maintenance costs, including effects on the supply chain.

To this end, Eni has developed a methodology to assess physical‑risk exposure for both its owned assets34 and the main third‑party assets within the value chain whose unavailability could affect the operability of Eni’s assets.

For the identification and assessment of adverse climatic events and their evolution over time, Eni uses the IPCC SSP5‑8.5 scenario ‑ the extreme scenario characterised by a temperature increase of more than 4°C by 2100 compared with preindustrial levels (see IPCC C8 category scenarios in Main reference scenarios).

Main physical climate related hazards35 considered by Eni include: water‑ related phenomena, such as sea‑level rise, water stress, drought, heavy precipitation (rain and hail), and coastal, river and pluvial flooding extreme wind related events, such as cyclones, hurricanes and typhoons; temperature‑related events, particularly wildfires; solid mass‑ related phenomena, such as landslides.
With respect to the physical risks associated with the climatic events listed above, Eni conducts a stress‑testing exercise on its current asset portfolio over a long‑term time horizon.
This assessment is carried out annually and is continuously improved, also in response to the future evolution and increasing accuracy of climate‑projection models36.
The analyses carried out in 2025 covered the entire Eni asset portfolio (around 900 sites).
