158 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX RESILIENCE OF THE STRATEGY TO TRANSITION SCENARIOS [ESRS 2 IRO-1; SBM-3] Once the physical risks associated with Eni’s assets (inherent risk) have been identified, an assessment of existing mitigation measures or barriers is carried out, including both physical protections and organisational systems or procedures in place.
Subsequently, the residual risk is evaluated for each asset.

Where a high level of residual risk is identified, Eni implements different types of actions: (i) for chronic risks (e.g., water stress), monitoring activities are planned and carried out, with potential subsequent definition and follow‑up of an intervention plan; (ii) for acute risks, the asset‑integrity process37 is activated, which may lead to the definition and implementation of an adaptation plan.

The physical‑risk analyses conducted in 2025 on Eni’s operating assets and on the main assets within the value chain showed that Eni’s portfolio is overall resilient to climate‑related physical risks.

The main reasons behind the overall resilience of Eni’s asset portfolio are: (i) the intrinsic resilience of the assets themselves, which are already designed according to strict engineering and safety criteria to withstand extreme natural events; (ii) the geographical diversification of the asset portfolio.

Material opportunities While transition events may entail risks, they can also create opportunities whose realisation requires disciplined capital allocation and a rigorous strategic‑planning process.

To capture such opportunities, Eni is integrating its business model with solutions linked to the energy transition ‑ including the growth of renewable‑energy production, the expansion of the electric‑vehicle charging‑point network through Plenitude, the development of biorefining activities through Enilive (see Operating review/Enilive and Plenitude), and the development of renewable‑based chemicals.

At the same time, Eni is implementing solutions aimed at reducing its own emissions and those of third parties, including the development of Carbon Capture, Utilisation and Storage (CCUS) projects (see Operating review/CCS and Agri), and continues to invest in research and development through innovative projects focused on advanced technologies such as magnetic-confinement fusion.

To enable the development of these opportunities, Eni has adapted its organisational structure (see Climate governance and Policies) in line with the evolution of its strategy, and has implemented a ‘satellite model’38 to reduce the financial burden associated with the growth of new businesses while making their market value more visible.

During this period — in the absence of consolidated regulatory guidance on the approach to be adopted — the company structured its decarbonisation plan through net‑emission reduction targets defined using its own methodology, consistent with major international standards and industry best practices.

The actions undertaken in recent years have enabled progress in reducing emissions in line with the defined intermediate targets (for performance details, see Metrics/Entity-specific indicators - Equity).

In 2024, the first year of CSRD implementation, Eni chose to present its decarbonisation pathway by maintaining the methodological approach adopted in previous years, within an evolving external and regulatory context.

From the 2025 reporting cycle onwards, following the consolidation of the European regulatory framework39, the decarbonisation plan and related targets have been recalibrated for consistencies with the new requirements and enhance comparability within the sector.
Specifically, for Scope 1 and 2 emissions, a financial control boundary is adopted (previously an equity‑share boundary), while maintaining a primary focus on the Upstream sector.
The Net Zero targets for Upstream Scope 1+2 GHG emissions by 2030 and for Eni as a whole by 2035 are confirmed and are redefined as ‘Net Scope 1+2 Upstream’ and ‘Net Scope 1+2 Eni’, respectively.

Scope 1+2+3 emissions include contributions from Scope 1 and 2 as well as Scope 3, the latter reported in accordance with the GHG Protocol classification (previously based on a Lifecycle methodology).

The Scope 1+2+3 target is expressed exclusively in terms of emissions intensity, with the aim of reflecting the diversification of the energy portfolio and highlighting both emission‑mitigation actions and the development of lower carbon businesses.
The Net Zero intensity target for 2050 is confirmed and redefined as ‘Net Intensity Scope 1+2+3’.
For all targets, any residual emissions are compensated through the use of high‑quality carbon credits40.

METHANE AND FLARING EMISSION REDUCTION TARGETS IN THE UPSTREAM BUSINESS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; E1-4] In the Upstream business, where natural gas plays a significant role, methane emission abatement and gas‑flaring reduction are central components of the decarbonisation pathway, having contributed substantially to the reduction of direct GHG emissions (Scope 1).

In this context, as early as 2016, Eni set a target to reduce fugitive methane emissions by 80% by 2025 (vs. 2014), a target achieved well ahead of schedule in 2019 thanks to the systematic implementation of LDAR (Leak Detection And Repair45) campaigns on operated assets.

Since 2022, Eni has also adhered to the ‘Aiming for Zero Methane Emissions’ initiative promoted by the Oil and Gas Climate Initiative (OGCI), which aims to minimise methane emissions from operated assets to the lowest technically achievable levels by 2030.
The initiative identifies an emission‑intensity threshold of 0.2% as the current sectoral benchmark for near‑zero methane emissions.

Scope 3 value chain emissions are calculated in accordance with the GHG Protocol and with available sector methodologies (IPIECA Net Volume Accounting), following a materiality assessment consistent with Eni’s activities.

Under the same initiative, Eni confirms its commitment to maintaining methane emissions intensity below 0.2% for Upstream operated assets through to 2030.

With regard to gas flaring, Eni set in 2015 the objective of eliminating routine flaring and has progressively strengthened initiatives for its reduction, particularly in Congo, Libya and Egypt, where greater logistical, operational and market constraints had limited the utilisation of associated gas.
Eni has confirmed the achievement of the ‘zero routine flaring’ target in 2025 for the operated boundary.
For activities carried out through operating companies, achievement of the target depends on the completion of projects in Libya, expected during 2026.

Eni confirms its intention to maintain these performance levels and to consolidate its commitment to zero routine flaring across operated assets, ensuring continuity of the actions undertaken and in line with the World Bank’s ‘Zero Routine Flaring by 2030’ initiative.

DECARBONISATION LEVERS [E1-1; E1-3] The decarbonisation levers and technologies identified by Eni in its decarbonisation plan apply across the various businesses and are adopted and calibrated in a targeted manner, with time horizons that reflect the technological and commercial maturity of each solution.

Between 2018 and 2025, Eni implemented actions that contributed, on the one hand, to the reduction of Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions from its own operations‑acting primarily on flaring, methane and energy‑efficiency initiatives aimed at reducing fossil‑fuel consumption (see Methane and flaring emission reduction targets in the Upstream business and Energy consumption and energy mix).

On the other hand, these actions also contributed to reducing emissions along the value chain (Scope 3), particularly through synergies between traditional activities and transition‑related businesses, as well as portfolio measures.

Looking ahead, the initiatives planned by Eni to reduce Net Intensity Scope 1+2+3 will be structured along multiple levers that act both on operational emissions and on the evolution of the energy portfolio.
By 2030, a significant contribution will derive from improved operational performance, Oil & Gas portfolio actions and the evolution of the production mix.
In particular, efficiency measures and the rationalisation of industrial assets will help reduce Scope 1 and 2 emissions.

In addition, the progressive increase in the share of gas (including condensates) over total production (above 60% by 2030) will reduce overall emission intensity compared with a scenario characterised by a greater share of oil, due to the lower emission factor of natural gas.
Another key contribution will stem from the progressive diversification of the portfolio towards lower carbon solutions, reducing the carbon intensity of the products and services offered.
The development of biofuels will represent a central element of this strategy, offering an opportunity to convert and scale down existing traditional refining capacity.
Following the conversions of the Porto Marghera (2014) and Gela (2019) biorefineries, the conversion project of the Livorno site was launched in 2024, with start‑up expected by the end of 2026.

In 2025, Eni reached a biorefining capacity of 1.65 million tonnes and expects to reach 5 million tonnes by 2030, with optionality for more than 2 million tonnes of Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF), thereby contributing to the decarbonisation of hard‑to‑abate sectors such as aviation, maritime transport and heavy road transport.
In parallel, renewable‑energy generation developed through Plenitude will support the progressive reduction of the emission intensity of the energy portfolio.
In 2025, Plenitude reached an installed renewable capacity of 5.8 GW, achieving an annual growth rate above 40%, in line with expansion targets to reach 15 GW by 2030.

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 161 lower carbon sectors (e.g., non-energy uses47) and/or the compensation of residual emissions through high-quality carbon credits (offsets), primarily from Natural Climate Solutions (up to a maximum of 15 MtCO2eq.48 by 2030).

In the long-term, Eni’s pathway towards Carbon Neutrality by 2050 will continue along the same directions, with a further strengthening of the gas component in the production mix (around 90% by 2050), the consolidation of lower carbon solutions, and an increase in Plenitude’s renewable capacity to 60 GW by 2050.
Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) is an additional decarbonisation lever.
From the period after 2030 onwards, as technologies advance and projects mature, CCS will help reduce operational emissions linked to energy use in Eni’s activities.
For residual emissions, compensation and removals are envisaged through Natural Climate Solutions (NCS) and technological solutions.

In 2050, achieving the Net Zero Intensity Scope 1+2+3 target will require the use of carbon credits within a limit of 10% of the baseline value of Scope 1+2+3 emissions (see GHG emissions offsets and removals).
Eni also contributes to the energy transition through activities that, while not directly affecting its own emissions, support decarbonisation of third parties and hard-to-abate sectors.

These include research and development (R&D), Plenitude’s offering of solutions and services aimed at the electrification of consumption and efficiency improvement, and the development of CCS projects for industrial clients (see Operating review/CCS and Agri).

The pace of this transformation and the relative contribution of each lever will depend on several variables, including market dynamics, the scientific and technological outlook, and the regulatory framework.

The milestones of Eni decarbonisation pathway have been identified through a prioritisation exercise for intervention levers, based on internal analyses and on the main international scenarios that aim for carbon neutrality by 2050 in order to limit the increase in global temperature to 1.5°C by 2100.

While acknowledging the limitations of comparing a corporate context with the global context, Eni decarbonisation plan49 – both in terms of levers and emission reduction targets – is substantially compatible with such scenarios (see IPCC category C1-C2 scenarios in Main reference scenarios, the section Decarbonisation targets).

162 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX ASSESSMENT OF LOCKED‑IN EMISSIONS [E1-1] Locked‑in emissions represent the estimate of future GHG emissions associated with existing or planned assets and operations in the Upstream and Downstream, based on their technological configuration and remaining useful life.

Under certain energy‑transition scenarios aligned with low emission decarbonisation pathways, these emissions may not be fully compatible with expected emission levels, potentially leading to economic‑ financial implications.

Eni monitors potential locked‑in emissions arising from its main assets and projects, maintaining a medium‑to‑long‑term perspective through the Strategic Plan and assessing progress towards the pathway to Carbon Neutrality by 2050.

Portfolio‑resilience analyses carried out as part of impairment testing have not identified any factors that would compromise the recoverability of asset carrying amounts under the scenarios considered.
In the Upstream sector, Eni continues to adopt an exploration model based on organic growth and selective enhancement of discoveries.

Investment selection considers both economic value and emission intensity; to help reduce the emission footprint, priority is given to investments in resources already in production and to exploration activities adjacent to existing assets and infrastructures.
In Downstream, Eni aims to improve the efficiency of its operations and integrate lower carbon products into its offering, including through the conversion of traditional refining capacity.

Eni also assesses portfolio resilience to mitigate risks associated with stranded assets and applies an internal carbon‑pricing mechanism to verify the consistency of new investments with decarbonisation targets (see Transition risks).

PATENTS AND INNOVATION [ENTITY-SPECIFIC] Eni’s Code of Ethics assigns a central role to innovation (see Policies) and, consistently, Eni is committed to acquiring cutting‑edge technological competences to support this principle.

In particular, the topic of innovation is strongly linked to climate aspects and, for the next strategic plan, the company has set the target of maintaining 70% of R&D expenditure on areas relating to decarbonisation, circular economy and the energy transition.

This expenditure includes, in particular, the areas of biorefining, chemistry and the production of energy from renewable sources (including biomass), energy storage, the capture, transport, storage and reuse of CO2, the reduction of the carbon footprint of processes.

The overall expenditure on innovation, which includes, in addition to Research & Development expenditure, also other innovative activities, including those relating to High Performance Computing, Artificial Intelligence and Open Innovation, is equal to about € 460 million.

GHG EMISSIONS OFFSETS AND REMOVALS [E1-7] Eni supports, through medium and long‑term agreements with partners and local authorities, the development of projects aimed at generating carbon credits in the voluntary market to compensate for residual GHG emissions that cannot otherwise be reduced, monitoring the quality and integrity of such credits.
To achieve the 2050 Net Zero target for Net Intensity Scope 1+2+3, Eni intends to use carbon credits only after reducing 90‑95% of its GHG emissions.
Currently, the carbon credits used by Eni to meet its Net Zero targets are generated by activities that reduce CO2 emissions that would otherwise be released into the atmosphere.
These include Natural Climate Solutions (NCS), which aim to protect and sustainably manage land.
Examples include REDD+53 projects, the first initiatives promoted by Eni, which support forest conservation, biodiversity protection and local sustainable development54.
Alongside NCS initiatives, technological solutions are also deployed.

Among these there is the ‘Eni for Clean Cooking’ programme, launched in 2018, which promotes the introduction of improved cookstoves that reduce the consumption of non‑renewable biomass55, with the dual objective of improving people’s health conditions and contributing to forest conservation.

The programme has been implemented in Congo, Mozambique, Angola, Rwanda, Tanzania, Madagascar and Côte d’Ivoire, reaching so far around 3.7 million people56 (2.2 million in 2025 alone), with the goal of ensuring access to safer, more efficient and more sustainable cooking systems for 20 million people by 2030.
The large‑scale adoption of clean‑cooking systems also fosters the development of local entrepreneurship and economic activity (e.g., production and distribution of cookstoves).

Starting in 2025, the programme began transitioning from improved cookstoves to more advanced technologies ‑ such as induction cookstoves in urban areas and pyrolytic cookstoves powered by agricultural and agroforestry residues in rural areas.
This evolution will make it possible to progressively eliminate the use of non‑renewable biomass, significantly reducing deforestation.

Eni’s strategy foresees a progressive increase in the share of credits derived from so‑called Carbon Dioxide Removal (CDR) projects ‑ activities (NCS or technological solutions) that remove CO2 directly from the atmosphere, such as ecosystem restoration or increased soil‑carbon stocks through appropriate agricultural practices.

In this context, Eni has launched the Makueni Agroforestry Carbon Project (MACP) in Kenya, promoting Sustainable Agriculture and Land Management (SALM)57, and the Conservation & Restoration of Classified Forests (CRCF) project in Côte d’Ivoire.
The carbon credits used by the Group are certified under internationally recognised voluntary‑market standards, such as the Verified Carbon Standard (VCS) by Verra or the Gold Standard (GS).

In addition, the credits are accompanied by supplementary certifications — such as the Climate, Community & Biodiversity Standards (CCBS) or the Sustainable Development Verified Impact Standard (SD VISta) — attesting to their socio‑environmental benefits (e.g., biodiversity conservation, economic development and improved living conditions for local communities).

METRICS [ESRS 2 MDR-M; E1-5; E1-6] GHG Metrics (Scope 1, 2 and 3) Eni reports its GHG emissions in line with major international standards and industry best practices.

Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions are reported by including within the consolidation boundary: (i) subsidiaries (fully consolidated) and (ii) the proportional share of activities in joint operations (incorporated and unincorporated) that are consolidated, as well as activities related to mining initiatives managed by operating companies.
In addition, for operated assets, emissions are reported at 100%.

Scope 3 emissions related to the value chain are reported in accordance with the categories defined by the GHG Protocol and based on available sector methodologies, following a materiality assessment consistent with Eni’s activities.

In addition to the metrics described above, Eni also reports emissions through a set of additional entity‑specific indicators used to track operational performance and progress along the pathway to achieving Carbon Neutrality by 2050.

166 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX Methodological Note Scope 1-2 In line with applicable regulatory requirements and consistent with major international standards (WBCSD/WRI GHG Protocol Initiative Standard and sector best practices), GHG emissions are reported for all relevant emission sources, considering the following gases: CO2, CH4 and N2O.

Other greenhouse gases (HFCs, PFCs, SF6, NF3), based on analysis of available data and in line with Oil & Gas sector practice, are not considered significant as they account for approximately 0.02% of total GHG emissions.
Global Warming Potentials (GWPs) from the IPCC Sixth Assessment Report (AR6) over a 100‑year horizon are used for conversion into CO2eq.
: 29.8 for CH4 and 273 for N2O.

Scope 1 GHG emissions are classified into the following categories: (i) Combustion and process (stationary combustion, mobile sources and industrial‑process operations); (ii) Flaring (controlled combustion of hydrocarbons via flare); (iii) Venting (controlled release to the atmosphere of unburned gas, mainly CH4, during operational activities such as Oil & Gas exploration and production, power generation and gas transportation); (iv) Fugitive emissions (unintentional methane leaks from equipment and components such as pumps, valves, compressor seals, etc.).
Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions are calculated based on measurement or, where not available, estimation (e.g., fuel consumed, electricity purchased, distance travelled).

Depending on their physical origin, data are sourced from: (i) fuel‑meter readings; (ii) utility bills (e.g., electricity consumption); (iii) direct measurement (e.g., LDAR campaigns for fugitive emissions); (iv) other methods applied at specific Eni sites and facilities.
Emission factors are determined based on gas chemical composition or derived from literature sources.

Specifically: (i) for installations subject to the EU ETS, national standard parameters under Regulation (EU) 2018/2066 (updated for 2025) issued by the Ministry for Ecological Transition are applied for natural gas, LPG, refinery fuel gas, petroleum‑derived gas and flare gas; (ii) for other installations, the main references are the IPCC guidelines and the API Compendium of Greenhouse Gas Emissions Methodologies for the Oil and Natural Gas Industry (2009/2021) for CO2, CH4 and N2O.
At Eni sites where LDAR programmes are in place, fugitive CH4 emissions are determined using periodic leak‑measurement campaigns.
Measurement results are used to quantify annual emissions by applying emission factors from API, EPA (e.g., EPA Protocol No.
or on oil and gas production volumes, using standard emission factors (API Compendium).
Biogenic CO2 Scope 1 emissions are reported separately; they amounted to 0.27 and 0.21 MtCO2eq.
in 2024 and 2025 respectively, and derive from biomass combustion and biomethane production processes.

Scope 2 emissions are reported according to two approaches: a) Location‑Based ‑ based on the average electricity‑generation mix of the Country from which third‑party electricity is purchased (IEA Emission Factors database); b) Market‑Based ‑ based on supplier‑specific electricity‑data, considering the share of renewable electricity, the Country residual mix, and contractual instruments linked or unlinked to the supply contracts (in 2025, these instruments covered around 55% of total electricity purchases).

Emissions associated with non‑renewable or non‑certified supply are calculated using supplier‑specific factors or the residual mix where available; otherwise, the Country electricity mix is used (AIB 2024 factors).
Emission factors for steam purchases are derived from the API Compendium.
Biogenic CO2 Scope 2 emissions are not estimated as they are deemed not significant.
Non‑consolidated operated Scope 2 emissions amounted to 0.04 MtCO2eq.
(location‑based) and 0.05 MtCO2eq.
(market‑based), as required by ESRS E1‑6 50b.

Scope 3 GHG emissions associated with Eni’s value chain — not accounted for as Scope 1 or Scope 2 — are reported according to the WBCSD/WRI GHG Protocol, the Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Standard and IPIECA methodologies.
Scope 3 emissions are classified by category and reported following a materiality assessment consistent with Eni’s activities.
For the Oil & Gas sector, the only category considered significant (~92.8% of total) is the use of sold energy products (Category 11).
These emissions are estimated using the Net Volume Accounting criterion defined by IPIECA.
The calculation applies ISPRA emission factors, includes assumptions on end‑use sold products applications, and is entirely based on primary activity data (specifically, sold hydrocarbon production).
Other categories are not reported as they are not considered significant (together 7.2% of total Scope 3, equal to 196.5 MtCO2eq., of which ~0.9% Category 1, ~1.0% Category 10 and ~3.2% Category 15).
For Joint Ventures, Associates, within the value chain and outside it, only Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions are considered.
Biogenic CO2 Scope 3 emissions amounted to approximately 3.1 and 2.9 MtCO2eq.
in 2024 and 2025 respectively, linked to the combustion of biofuels sold and biomethane injected into the grid, calculated using DEFRA factors.

Energy consumption and energy mix The energy efficiency interventions carried out in the year allow an effective saving of primary energy compared to baseline consumption of over 308 ktoe/year, deriving mainly from projects in the Upstream area (77%), with a benefit in terms of emission reduction equal to over 765 kt of CO2eq.

In 2025 Eni’s total energy consumption (equal to 84.4 Million MWh) records a reduction compared to 2024, mainly due to the contraction of fossil energy consumption, in particular in Upstream, to boundaries changes (portfolio operations in Nigeria with a consequent fall in fuel gas consumption and in Congo, with a reduction in diesel consumption and a further contraction in fuel gas consumption) and to the shutdowns of Versalis plants (Brindisi and Priolo, in Italy).

Renewable energy consumption (equal to 548,060 MWh) is overall down, since increases in purchases of electricity covered by guarantees of origin were offset by lower consumption of fuels from renewable sources following the shutdown of biomass boilers at the Versalis plant in Crescentino (Italy).

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 169 TRANSPARENCY AND PARTNERSHIP Transparency in the disclosure Eni communicates climate‑related information in compliance with regulatory sustainability‑disclosure requirements, while also referring to the main voluntary guidelines and best practices for climate reporting, including the OECD Guidelines for the inside‑out perspective and the TCFD framework for the outside‑in view.
Eni contributes to the development of standards and best practices for comprehensive and effective climate disclosure, including through participation in international industry initiatives.
In particular, adherence to the OGMP 2.0 programme strengthens the reliability and transparency of methane emissions reporting in the Oil & Gas sector.

The quality and transparency of Eni’s methane reporting have been recognised by UNEP through the awarding of the OGMP 2.0 Gold Standard Reporting level, as referenced in the IMEO methane emissions reports published in 2024 and 2025.

Eni’s climate‑disclosure quality and transparency have also been positively assessed in the Pledges to Progress 2025 report, produced by UNEP, the IEA and the Environmental Defense Fund (EDF), which evaluates actions undertaken by Oil & Gas companies in support of the decarbonisation commitments set out in the Oil & Gas Decarbonisation Charter (OGDC).
As part of its continuous improvement approach, in 2024 Eni published a dedicated report62 outlining its actions to reduce methane emissions across all operations.

Transparency in climate disclosure, combined with the company’s decarbonisation strategy, has contributed to Eni’s positive performance in major ESG ratings and climate benchmarks (see ESG Ratings and Capital Market Update on Eni’s website).
As part of its advocacy activities, Eni also shares its position on climate change and related climate strategy topics (see Business Conduct).

Partnerships for decarbonisation Eni collaborates and engages with institutions, academia, companies and international organisations to foster the energy transition by generating and sharing knowledge, developing best practices and supporting initiatives that create value both for the company and for its stakeholders.

Eni is a founding member of the UN Environment Programme’s Oil & Gas Methane Partnership (OGMP), the Oil and Gas Climate Initiative (OGCI), and actively participates in expert groups such as IPIECA and IOGP.

Eni is also a signatory of the Oil & Gas Decarbonisation Charter63 (OGDC), a key initiative launched at COP28 to guide the sector towards transparent and concrete actions to reduce emissions, including methane and flaring.

To support these commitments, Eni has joined the Global Flaring and Methane Reduction (GFMR) trust fund, launched by the World Bank, which aims to help governments and national operators eliminate methane emissions and routine flaring by 2030.

These collaborations have contributed to the development of industry best practices for methane emission monitoring, reporting and verification, as well as the promotion of innovative technological solutions at sectoral level, including through the Climate Investment fund established by OGCI.
Eni has also signed cooperation agreements with NOCs and joint venture partners, including EGAS and Sonatrach, with the aim of sharing its expertise in managing and reducing methane emissions.
In parallel, Eni develops partnerships with energy‑intensive companies to promote lower carbon solutions.

In this context, Eni has joined the Patto per la Decarbonizzazione del Trasporto Aereo (PACTA), an initiative promoted with Aeroporti di Roma that brings together institutions, sector stakeholders and third‑sector organisations to define a roadmap for the decarbonisation of the aviation sector by 2050.

Finally, Eni collaborates with universities and start‑ups to develop innovative solutions in the energy field, including emerging technologies with long‑term emission reduction potential, such as magnetic‑confinement fusion.

Climate due diligence reconciliation framework Eni’s tools and practices related to climate change are framed within the due diligence phases identified in the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises on Responsible Business Conduct (2023) and the OECD Due Diligence Guidance for Responsible Business Conduct (2018), as summarised below.

(62) (63) At COP28, more than 50 companies joined the OGDC, of which around 30 — for the first time — committed to achieving Net Zero GHG emissions (Scope 1 and 2) by 2050, reaching Near Zero methane emissions and eliminating routine gas flaring by 2030, in addition to committing to report transparently on the reductions achieved.

170 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX PHASE DESCRIPTION Eni states its commitment to tackling climate change in its corporate Mission and through various policies ‑ particularly the Code of Ethics.
The Company has defined a decarbonisation plan to reach Carbon Neutrality by 2050, as an integral part of its corporate strategy and implemented via a structured Corporate Governance system.
The commitment to tackle climate change is also embedded in management performance through the Remuneration Policy.

Responsible conduct is promoted along the value chain and in advocacy activities, whose cornerstones are set out in the Supplier Code of Conduct and in the Assessment of Industry Associations’ Climate Policy Positions.
Eni has internal tools and processes to identify GHG emission sources.

Material impacts Based on this mapping, Eni develops an emissions inventory, reports emissions and establishes a prioritisation of mitigation actions, also taking into account the external climate debate.

Decarbonisation Plan Partnership for decarbonisation TAKING ACTIONS TO ADDRESS NEGATIVE IMPACTS Recognising the value of collective action to tackle climate change, Eni promotes combined, multi-sector and global initiatives by collaborating with academia, civil society, institutions and businesses to identify and advance actions supporting the energy transition.

Monitoring progress against GHG net-reduction targets follows a structured cycle that includes strategic planning, the allocation of targets linked to management remuneration, and semi‑annual KPI monitoring to analyse gaps and reprioritise actions for the next cycle.

Metrics MONITORING THE EFFECTIVENESS OF INTERVENTIONS COMMUNICATING Eni communicates climate-related information fully and transparently, in line with legal requirements on sustainability reporting, and follows the main voluntary guidelines and best practices on climate disclosure.
Eni also conducts ongoing monitoring of developments in both soft-law and hard-law climate regulations to assess the robustness of its tools and any need for adjustment.

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 171 Environment and Eni’s management system IN THIS CHAPTER: Pollution (E2) Water resources management (E3) Biodiversity (E4) Resource use and circular economy (E5) Eni pays particular attention to the promotion of the efficient use of natural resources, such as water, to the prevention and control of pollutant emissions, to waste management, and to the protection of biodiversity and ecosystem services.

Environmental matters, together with Health and Safety, are managed through a single integrated HSE management system, which defines the roles, responsibilities and management procedures for all business sectors.

Furthermore, to raise awareness among employees and across the supply chain on these aspects, Eni continues a program, launched in 2019, to strengthen environmental culture, involving all company levels; in particular, the program involved operational sites in Italy and is being extended to foreign subsidiaries, also through the signing of Environmental and Safety Pacts, which engage suppliers in tangible and measurable improvement actions.

In addition, in 2025, activities continued to support the adoption of virtuous behaviours by employees and suppliers, in line with Eni’s values, commitment and standards, through the promotion of the Environmental Golden Rules, which have been updated to enhance their effectiveness.

HSE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM [ESRS 2 MDR-A; E2; E3; E4; E5 IRO-1] For the management of the environment, health and safety in the workplace, Eni has adopted a three‑level responsibility model (employers, senior executives at business area level and Eni top management), each supported by a dedicated HSE function.
In order to ensure oversight over activities, Eni, also for the purpose of preventing offences pursuant to Italian Legislative Decree No.
231/2001, has implemented an adequate HSE control model, consistent with the corporate structure, organisational levels and the system of delegated authorities and responsibilities.

In line with ISO 14001:2015 certification, within the Management System each site carries out, in relation to its activities, a process for the identification of environmental aspects, including the assessment of potential associated impacts and risks, as well as the identification and monitoring of possible opportunities.
The assessment process considers the lifecycle of assets and activities under different operating conditions (normal, abnormal and emergency).
The risk64 associated with each environmental aspect/impact is assessed based on the technical and managerial barriers in place at the site.

This assessment process is periodic, monitored and updated in order to ensure and improve the quality and effectiveness of the identification, analysis and risk assessment process, as well as to periodically verify its consistency, adequacy and the effectiveness of the mitigation measures implemented.

All entities classified as significant risk are ISO 14001 and ISO 45001 certified or have planned to obtain such certifications (at the end of 2025, 94% of these had already achieved ISO 45001 certification and 93% ISO 14001); entities classified as limited risk and required to develop an HSE management system have also already implemented it (90% in 2025) or have planned its implementation.

In addition to third‑party audits required to maintain certifications, further internal audits are carried out on a semi‑annual basis to verify the adequacy of the HSE management system and regulatory compliance.
During the implementation phase of operational activities, the objective is to prevent, reduce and manage identified direct and indirect environmental risks and impacts.

These impacts may arise both from the specific activities of production units/organisational structures and from the various processes of design, (64) In this context, the term risk is not intended in terms of financial materiality risk; rather, it refers to the combination of the likelihood that a given event may occur within a specific period or under specific circumstances and the consequences that may arise.
The approach adopted takes into account all stages of the lifecycle in order to ensure comprehensive management of environmental aspects.

In this phase, appropriate processes for the selection and management of suppliers and contractors are also ensured, in accordance with Eni’s HSE requirements, including specific criteria and controls throughout the entire process – from qualification to contract execution.
Environmental impact assessment procedures are shared with local stakeholders through public consultations, where required by applicable regulations and, in some cases, also on a voluntary basis.

Inclusiveness and stakeholder engagement represent reference principles for Eni, which promotes preventive, free and informed consultations, considering their concerns with respect to activities, projects and development initiatives.

In general, the needs and expectations of interested parties are assessed by Eni sites within the context analysis in accordance with ISO 14001:2015, and the handling of grievances is ensured through the Grievance Mechanism and the whistleblowing process (for further details see Human Rights for Eni).

The monitoring, including through HSE reviews65 and reporting, play a strategic role in maintaining the efficiency of the organisational system, supporting the decision‑making process and identifying areas for improvement and actions to be implemented in order to achieve the defined objectives.
Analysis of site level information enables the identification of the most critical situations and the planning of specific interventions and related priorities.
For the implementation of environmental activities, financial resources required to meet identified commitments are defined within Eni’s Strategic Plan and detailed in the relevant thematic chapters.

Internal Regulatory System • Principles for the operational management of environmental impacts/risks: (i) optimization of water cycle management; (ii) control and monitoring of water discharges; (iii) prevention, monitoring and control of pollutant emissions; (iv) prevention and monitoring of spills; (v) management of soil, subsoil, surface water and groundwater contamination and the relevant emergency safety and reclamation actions; (vi) emergency management.

• Prevention, protection and mitigation for the conservation and safeguarding of the environment from the release of pollutants, through effective monitoring and control mechanisms that are periodically verified, based on the results of assessments conducted to identify environmental impacts.

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; E2-3] Eni adopts an Internal Regulatory System and an HSE Management System that ensures the definition of operational guidelines for all business areas, based on an understanding of the operating context, applicable legislative obligations, environmental compliance requirements and stakeholder expectations.

Within this framework, Eni, while not setting quantitative targets, is consistently engaged in implementing actions aimed at safeguarding water resources, air quality and soils through an approach focused on the prevention and minimisation of risks and impacts related to emissions affecting these environmental matrices.

The Actions required to implement the management system are reviewed on a semi‑annual basis, together with the analysis of Metrics useful for the detailed monitoring of performance, enabling an intervention in case of deviations.

Furthermore, Eni ensures compliance with legislative requirements also through HSE management systems certified in accordance with international standards and, as further detailed in the chapter dedicated to water resources, the adoption of quantitative targets on pollution is under evaluation for future Strategic Plans.

In fact, the commitment defined in terms of water positivity ( Water Resources), in line with the principles of Positive Water Impact that Eni follows, intrinsically also considers the water quality dimension and therefore, where necessary, also aims at objectives for reducing water pollution.
(65) The HSE Review is aimed at assessing the management of HSE risks and verifying the suitability, adequacy and effectiveness of the HSE management system adopted.

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 173 MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3] The material impacts and risks related to the own activities66 on pollution topic are attributable to the release of substances into air, water or soil associated with industrial activities in the exploration, development and production of hydrocarbons, refining and transportation of fuels and other flammable products, petrochemical production, as well as potential malfunctions in water treatment systems within remediation activities.

Although Eni operates in compliance with national and local regulations and is subject to oversight by the competent authorities, these activities are inherently exposed to operational risks that may result in impacts on the environment and on people within Eni, as well as on contractors, suppliers and business partners, and local communities.

Material impacts Air emissions from the Oil & Gas sector are a source of potential environmental impacts, particularly at local level, in terms of air quality, odour disturbance, photochemical smog and the phenomenon of acid rain (acidification).

The main emissions, mostly related to processes at Upstream industrial facilities and at power generation, chemicals and refining plants, concern nitrogen oxides (NOx) and sulphur oxides (SOx), particulate matter (PM) and non‑methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs).

The same activities are also a source of potential impacts related to water discharges, for which Eni closely monitors the presence of hydrocarbons in Upstream produced water and in industrial wastewater.

With regard to releases to soil, Eni’s activities do not involve operational discharges; however, unintentional releases of oil and other chemical products may occur as a result of accidental loss of containment, mainly associated with Upstream and refining activities, or unlawful acts (theft and sabotage).

Material risks and opportunities Although effective preventive systems and good management practices are adopted, the operational risks inherent in Eni’s business activities may lead to the occurrence of: (i) process incidents, such as fires, explosions, spills, releases of hazardous substances, and asset integrity incidents; (ii) other non-process-related incidents (such as those occurring in road, rail, and maritime transport, service stations, and gas distribution networks); (iii) blowouts resulting from uncontrolled hydrocarbon flow from within the well (see Risk factors and uncertainty).
No material opportunities related to the topic of pollution have been identified.

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; E2-2] Eni, across the various geographical contexts in which it operates, is committed to preventing and minimising environmental impacts by promoting the adoption of international good practices and Best Available Technologies (BAT)67, both technical and managerial.

The operational management of pollution‑related impacts and risks focuses on managing pollutant emissions to air and water, preventing spills, and monitoring the effectiveness of the actions undertaken.

Industrial facilities operate in compliance with environmental regulations and permit conditions and with the principles of prevention, protection and mitigation of environmental impacts, implementing actions aimed at the continuous improvement of environmental performance.

Eni has adopted a multi‑level responsibility model and an Internal Regulatory System (see Health & Safety) which provides, among other things, the adoption of management system, in higher‑HSE risk sites, certified in accordance with ISO 14001 and ISO 50001 standards (as well as ISO 45001 for health and safety matters).
The technical audit plan and the internal control system adopted to prevent and minimise operational risks enable continuous verification of activities against Eni’s regulatory principles.

Below are the actions implemented by Eni on the basis of the material impacts, risks and opportunities identified, grouped into: (i) Air emissions; (ii) Water emissions; (iii) Soil emissions in relation to accidental releases (Spills).
It should be noted that the actions implemented by Eni and described below refer to accidental releases into the environment, both onshore and offshore.

Air emissions The operational model adopted by Eni, in addition to ensuring regulatory compliance, provides an approach aimed at preventing and reducing the risks associated with air pollution that emissions may cause, as well as their potential effects on local air quality.

To this end, Eni systematically and continuously defines and implements actions related to: • monitoring and control emissions, taking into account the territorial, environmental and regulatory context in order to ensure best performance in terms of limiting releases to the atmosphere.
Particular attention is paid to potential atmospheric effects and odour impacts, through continuous oversight and direct control of individual emission sources.

For activities subject to the European Industrial Emissions Directive (IED), Eni operates to ensure compliance with the requirements expressly set out in the Monitoring and Control Plan, as required by applicable regulations and in line with the application of specific BAT according to the different types of emissions (channelled, diffuse, fugitive and (66) For any other impacts on the value chain, see the chapter Value Chain and main impacts.
(67) By way of reference, consideration is given to the documents issued by the European Commission (BREF ‑ BAT reference documents).

It should be noted that the ongoing transformation projects related to decarbonisation and energy efficiency, further detailed in the Climate Change chapter, generally also have positive effects on air pollutant emissions.

Water emissions Eni implements continuous actions during both project development and operational phases, including: • systematic prevention, monitoring and control in the management of water discharges emissions, aimed at safeguarding not only the use of the resource but also the quality of the aquatic environment.

With regard to the monitoring of discharge quality, Eni adopts internal pre‑alert thresholds for specific pollutants, in order to promptly initiate any corrective actions, where necessary; • development of water-treatment technologies and application of the BATs; • monitoring of total oil in produced water discharged, related to Upstream operations.

It should be noted that projects aimed at optimising water cycles and minimising water withdrawals, described in the Water Resources chapter, also entail benefits in terms of reducing pollutant releases into the aquatic environment.
In 2025 the monitoring system was installed in Côte d’Ivoire on a section of an offshore field.

In Italy, a programme for the implementation of the remote monitoring system (TPI system, Third Party Interference technology) is being carried out on all pipeline sections most vulnerable to breaches, damage from third parties or ground movements.
For example, it is currently being implemented on the Gaeta‑Pomezia line, in Italy.
This system is subject to technological updates of both hardware and software to continuously improve the detection of third‑party interference and prevent breaches.

To prevent spills, in particular from storage tanks, Eni invests in a proprietary technology (e‑siam® ‑ Eni Structural Integrity Acoustic Monitoring) to detect and locate corrosion phenomena and leaks from tanks and pipelines, supported by ongoing testing and trials.
In addition, verification, monitoring and replacement activities of onshore and offshore pipelines are carried out at frequencies specific for each asset.

For storage tanks, dedicated cleaning and inspection programmes enable repairs and, where necessary, tank replacement or installation of double bottoms; • preparedness and response, following the analysis of possible accidental scenarios and the development of the most appropriate response procedures.
In 2025, for example, remediation with certification was completed for several service areas.
In 2025, remediation activities were completed and certified for several service stations, including the one in Vercelli (Italy).

The decrease in SOX emissions (‑7% compared to 2024) is linked to the reduction of contributions from the Italian refineries of Sannazzaro, Taranto (due to planned plant shutdowns during the period) and Livorno (due to plant reconfiguration related to the ongoing conversion to a biorefinery) and to the lower consumption of fuel oil from Steam Cracking at the Dunkirk petrochemical plant.

The reduction in NOX emissions (‑16% compared to 2024) and NMVOC (‑26% compared to 2024) was influenced by the exit from the Upstream portfolio, at the end of 2024, of assets in Nigeria, by the already mentioned conversion activities of the Livorno refinery and by the shutdowns of some petrochemical plants (Brindisi, Priolo, Ragusa and Grangemounth).

As regards PM emissions (‑33% compared to 2024), in addition to the portfolio operations in Nigeria, further reductions in activities recorded in the Upstream sector contributed to the downward trend (in particular the decrease of emissions from non‑routine flaring in Ghana and the divestment of the M’Boundi field in Congo), and by those of the Sannazzaro refinery due to the introduction of new calibration procedures for the measurement of emissions.
The content of total hydrocarbons in discharged waters was equal to about 53 tonnes, showing a significant decrease (‑50%) compared to 2024.

The trend is mainly attributable to the lower contribution of the Venice biorefinery, where it was recorded a reduction in the average hydrocarbons concentration in the effluent due to targeted cleaning interventions of the sewer network and a decrease in discharged water flows, as a result of water optimization and saving measures implemented during the period.

In 2025 the volumes spilled following operational oil spill recorded a further decrease (217 barrels vs. 675 in 2024, ‑68%), with a reduction also in the number of cases (9 vs. 18 in 2024) and improving performance both in the Upstream business and at the refineries.

The events recorded in 2025 involved three Countries: Germany, for 88% of the spilled volumes, Italy for 11% and Tunisia for the remaining 1%; among the recorded events, the most significant event (157 barrels) occurred at a service station in Germany.

As regards the recovery activities of the spilled volumes following the occurred events, it is specified that in some cases they are still in the phase of design and sharing with the competent authorities.
In 2025 no oil spill events due to acts of sabotage were recorded, also following the divestment of assets in Nigeria where historically such events were concentrated.
As regards chemical spills, they also recorded a decreasing trend: overall 4 events are counted in Upstream, for a total of 0.3 barrels spilled (compared to the 70 barrels of 2024).

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 179 WATER RESOURCES [ESRS E3] POLICIES [ESRS 2 MDR-P; E3-1] POLICY KEY CONTENTS: Code of Ethics Eni’s position on water • Commitment to the fight against climate change, including innovative solutions aimed at reducing the impact of Eni’s operations, also through the protection of water resources.

• Commitment to setting targets to minimise freshwater withdrawals in water‑stressed areas(a), by seeking improved solutions or by reducing consumption through efficiency activities and by looking for solutions outside the Company and its operational boundary.

• Commitment to periodically carry out a water risk assessment, analysing the specific areas where its operational assets are located, adopting an integrated and cross‑cutting approach that involves specialist functions and business lines.

• Commitment to identifying partnerships for optimal water management in line with local needs, to promote social development and safeguard ecosystems, providing for the active involvement of stakeholders in its procedures.

Internal Regulatory System • Water resource management model defining the procedures for: (i) identifying water‑stressed areas; (ii) managing water withdrawals, use and discharges; (iii) identifying priority sites and related interventions; (iv) reporting and communication activities.
These tools aim to identify withdrawals and consumption across all business sectors, to assess and minimise potential impacts on ecosystems and communities.
The treatment, disposal or reinjection of water is governed by sector‑specific best practices.
• Actions to be implemented through the application of water risk mitigation criteria: avoid, replace, reduce, recycle, replenish.

• Procedures to inform and engage stakeholders, promoting preventive, free and informed consultation, in order to take their views into account regarding activities, new projects and development initiatives.

(a) Water stressed areas are identified using Aqueduct, a tool developed by the World Resources Institute, and are monitored annually through an internal analysis carried out down to the level of the individual operational site.
Areas are classified as water‑stressed when their Baseline water stress value exceeds 40% (that is, when withdrawals of renewable water are greater than 40% of the basin’s recharge capacity).
Over the years, this journey has included its endorsement of the CEO Water Mandate and the publication of Eni’s position on water.
In 2024, Eni announced its ambition to achieve water positivity74 by 2050 in its operated sites.
This ambition is framed within an approach that also considers measures at river basin level and is inspired by the Positive Water Impact (PWI) principles set out by the CEO Water Mandate.

Actions to safeguard water will focus on the aspects identified as most critical for each territory in terms of water ‘availability’, ‘quality’ and ‘accessibility’, which represent the three dimensions to be considered under the approach proposed by the CEO Water Mandate.
Interventions will therefore be tailored to the identified needs and to the relevance of each operational site, giving priority to those located in highly water‑stressed basins.

As an intermediate milestone along the pathway towards the 2050 ambition, Eni is committed to achieving water positivity by 2035 in at least 30% of its sites that withdraw more than 0.5 Mm³/year of freshwater in areas identified as water‑stressed, based on 2023 data (priority sites)75.
This commitment therefore entails identifying actions to safeguard water resources, whose implementation and benefits must be monitored over time.
During 2024, Eni conducted a pilot study at one of its operated sites to assess the applicability of the PWI approach to the Oil & Gas sector.
In 2025, three priority sites were analysed, identifying impacts and opportunities in line with PWI principles, for which feasibility assessments will now be carried out.

Eni applies an Internal Regulatory System and an HSE management system that, based on contextual knowledge, legal requirements and stakeholder expectations, ensures the definition of operational guidelines and the related actions required for their implementation across all business areas.

Progress is monitored every six months through the HSE review process and by using a set of metrics (both those reported in the Sustainability Statement and additional indicators monitored internally), ensuring timely corrective measures in the event of deviations from expected performance.

(74) Ensuring that, at river basin level, water stewardship initiatives generate greater benefits than the impacts associated with the presence of an operational site, such as those related to the withdrawals required for industrial processes or to the quality of water returned to the environment, in line with the Guidance issued by the (75) Priority sites (with withdrawals above 0.5 Mm³ in 2023), which account for over 90% of Eni’s high‑quality freshwater operated withdrawals in waterstressed areas in 2023 (baseline).

Across industrial operations, around 86% of the water used is seawater, while the remaining 14% is freshwater, a resource that is difficult to replace in many applications and whose withdrawal may affect local availability for people, fauna and flora.

Freshwater withdrawals, mainly used for cooling, for producing demineralised water and for generating steam as an energy carrier, are attributable for approximately 80% to petrochemical and refining activities.
Seawater, on the other hand, is primarily used for cooling and, in Upstream operations, to increase the amount of crude oil that can be extracted from reservoirs (Enhanced Oil Recovery ‑ EOR).
It is also used for drilling activities, for which surface or underground brackish water can likewise be used.
Material risks and opportunities The Process and results of the double materiality assessment did not identify any material risks or opportunities at Group level.

The assessment of exposure to water risks at asset level, as well as of related opportunities, is carried out on an annual basis as part of the responsible water management system (see Actions taken on material IROs), taking into account the dependence of certain assets on water resources (see Biodiversity).

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; E3-2] Eni recognises the importance of responsible water management and therefore closely monitors water withdrawals, discharges and consumption across all its operations, also considering the interest that all stakeholder categories76 have in this resource.

Eni’s water resource management model is based on identifying, assessing and minimising impacts on water resources, preventing adverse and/or unlawful environmental events and supporting the protection and enhancement of ecosystems.
This process is an integral part of Eni’s broader environmental management approach across its operational contexts, in which all relevant projects are subject to ESHIA77.

Each year, Eni carries out – particularly with regard to freshwater – an analysis to assess the level exposure to water risk78 of its assets and to identify opportunities to improve water management through the planning of interventions, prioritised in line with each business activity.

Eni also conducts regular supplier assessments through continuous monitoring of their ESG performance, including aspects related to water management, promoting the adoption of management systems aligned with leading international standards.
Finally, Eni’s commitment to increasing the share of produced water79 reinjected into reservoirs helps reduce withdrawals of saline or brackish water and enhances hydrocarbon recovery.

Based on the results of water risk analyses, the main improvement actions are directed and planned at the most significant sites in terms of freshwater withdrawals in water‑stressed areas, namely downstream industrial sites located in central and southern Italy and Upstream assets in North Africa.

Eni implements actions to safeguard water resources through two main strategies: on the one hand, improving efficiency through internal recycling of freshwater; on the other, replacing high‑quality80 freshwater sources with lower‑quality alternatives.

The actions implemented by Eni on the basis of the material impacts identified for freshwater consumption are presented below, divided into: (i) water stress areas; and (ii) areas not at water stress.
Water stress areas Eni implements actions to safeguard water resources in water‑stresse areas.

Below are some examples of measures aimed at replacing high‑quality freshwater with the following types of water: • wastewater, consisting of a combination of civil and industrial discharges as well as rainfall collected and drained through sewer networks or drainage systems.

Some examples of actions carried out, in Italian sites, during the year include: (i) Ravenna petrochemical hub, with a wastewater reuse plant, operational from 2026; (ii) Brindisi petrochemical site, with a plant for the reuse of approximately 0.4 Mm³ of wastewater per year, expected to be operational by 202681; (iii) Gela biorefinery, where the reuse of urban wastewater for industrial purposes has been increased since August 2024; (76) For further information on community engagement, see (77) Environmental, Social and Health Impact Assessments.

Once appropriately treated, this water can be reused for operational purposes, helping to reduce freshwater withdrawals.

Areas not at water stress Eni is committed to safeguarding water resources also beyond water‑stressed areas, in line with its principles.

By way of example, some of the actions implemented at sites in Italy not located in particularly water stressed areas are reported as follows: (i) at the Enipower plant in Ferrera Erbognone, where an innovative water filtration system was tested, improving water use efficiency; (ii) Versalis petrochemical plant in Mantua, where the internal recycling of freshwater for cooling has increased; (iii) Ferrara, where Versalis and Enipower signed a Memorandum of Understanding with local stakeholders (May 2024), defining priority actions to reduce withdrawals from the Po River.

The financial resources used for water resource management include: (i) monitoring and treatment of wastewater; (ii) injection and reinjection facilities for produced water; (iii) supply, desalination and cooling facilities.
More than half of the total expenditure for water resource management in 2025 is allocated to interventions at sites located in water‑stressed areas.
For the next five‑year period, Eni has allocated €0.7 billion for water resource management.

In 2025, water withdrawals decreased compared to 2024 (‑341 Mm³), driven by reductions in both seawater and freshwater withdrawals.

Seawater withdrawals, amounting to 707 Mm³ (86% of total water withdrawals), fell by 326 Mm³ year on year, mainly due to shutdowns at the Italian petrochemical plants in Brindisi and Priolo (which together account for more than 60% of the reduction) and to lower production at the Enipower Brindisi power plant (where withdrawals decreased by more than 111 Mm³).

The downward trend was further supported by the end of Upstream activities in Eni UK during the first months of 2025 (‑18 Mm³) and the shutdown of the Taranto refinery (‑10 Mm³) in the South of Italy.
These effects were partly offset by higher withdrawals in Côte d’Ivoire, linked to the growth of offshore operations.
Freshwater withdrawals in 2025, representing 14% of total water withdrawals and attributable for approximately 80% to petrochemical and refining activities, fell by more than 10% compared with 2024.

This reduction is mainly due to Versalis, totalling ‑11 Mm³, of which 8 Mm³ relate to the Mantua site, in Italy (thanks to optimised water consumption enabled by operation at reduced loads) and 1.3 Mm³ to Porto Marghera (Italy), following changes in the production setup.

Further contributions to the reduction in freshwater withdrawals were provided by the Sannazzaro refinery (more than ‑0.6 Mm³), in Italy, as a result of the stable operation of the Water Reuse plant, the divestment of onshore sites in Nigeria at the end of 2024 and the increased internal reuse of treated groundwater (at Eni Rewind sites).
The volumes of recycled freshwater, more than 72% of which are attributable to Versalis, decreased by 5% compared to 2024, mainly due to the general shutdown of the Ragusa plant (Italy).
The percentage of Eni’s freshwater reuse in 2025 remained stable at 90%.
In 2025, total water consumption in water stress areas accounted for 40% of Eni’s overall consumption.

It is specified that high‑quality freshwater withdrawals – sourced from surface water, groundwater and municipal water supply – carried out in these areas represented 2% of Eni’s total water withdrawals.

The percentage of produced water reinjection increased to 56% in 2025 (51% in 2024), reflecting both higher reinjected volumes in Turkmenistan, Mexico and Italy, and lower the overall volumes of produced water generated during the period.
This decrease is linked to the divestment of assets in Nigeria and Alaska in the second half of 2024 and to the end of Upstream activities in Eni UK in the early months of 2025.

Eni Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services (BES) Policy • Technical guidelines for biodiversity risk assessment and management across global activities and decision‑making processes throughout the project lifecycle(a), considering potential impacts on local communities, where relevant.
• Adoption of the mitigation hierarchy(b) to manage identified impacts, prioritising preventive over corrective measures to avoid net biodiversity loss or, where feasible, achieving a net gain.
• Commitments adopted over time for the protection of areas of ecological significance.
Eni Position on Water • Close link between water resources, biodiversity and ecosystem services protection, and forest conservation.

Eni No-Go Commitment Eni Position on Biomass • Formal commitment not to perform Oil & Gas exploration and development activities within the boundaries of Natural Sites included in the UNESCO World Heritage List(c).

• General principles to ensure the sustainability of agricultural practices, sourcing and consumption of raw materials, including: traceability and transparency along the supply chain; selection of suppliers meeting ESG criteria; and the inclusion of contractual clauses to ensure the sourcing of certified biomass(d) only, not originated from land converted from areas of high biodiversity value or from ecosystems providing essential ecological services, such as carbon capture and storage.
Internal Regulatory System • Processes for identifying, prioritising, managing, and monitoring biodiversity impacts and actions, in accordance with the HSE Integrated Management System.
(a) In joint ventures in which Eni is not the operator, it is committed to promoting with its partners the development and adoption of management best practices in line with the BES Policy.

(b) The mitigation hierarchy is an internationally recognised best practice for managing environmental risks and potential impacts, based on a sequence of actions: (i) anticipate and avoid impacts; (ii) minimize impacts where avoidance is not possible; (iii) restore; and (iv) offset.
(d) Sustainability certification schemes recognised at the European or international level.

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; E4-4] While Eni has not established consolidated quantitative targets, it remains committed to implementing actions aimed at safeguarding biodiversity and ecosystem services by focusing on the prevention and minimisation of risks and impacts.
Biodiversity is site‑specific and characterised by unique features that vary significantly across geographical areas, environmental conditions of ecosystems, and ecological interactions.
The lack of a universally recognised metric for measuring global biodiversity makes it complex to define aggregated targets at the Group level.

For this reason, Eni applies a ‘site‑specific’ management approach, implementing, where necessary, environmental management plans that identify targeted measures and local indicators, as described below.
This approach allows the specific features of each environmental context to be addressed more effectively, ensuring that actions are concrete, measurable, and tailored to impacts on the territory.

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS and OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3] Impacts identification process The process of identification and assessment of dependencies and impacts on biodiversity and ecosystem services (BES) related to Eni’s activities82 is integrated into the HSE Management System.
Each site, in line with ISO 14001:2015 certification, conducts studies to identify and evaluate environmental impacts (EIA ‑ Environmental Impact Assessment).

In addition, Eni annually maps its sites and concessions to verify proximity to legally protected areas and Key Biodiversity Areas (KBAs), and to assess the potential presence of priority species from the IUCN Red List83, in order to ensure more effective management of impacts in priority conservation areas.

The screening identifies the sites with the (82) For any other impacts on the value chain, see (83) The IUCN Red List is the global database developed by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) that classifies the conservation status of species according to internationally recognized scientific criteria.

The geographic distribution (range) of species is available through IBAT (Integrated Biodiversity Assessment Tool), a platform that integrates global data on species, protected areas and Key Biodiversity Areas.

184 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX highest risk of biodiversity loss, especially those overlapping with at least one important biodiversity area, where further in‑depth studies (BES Assessments) are carried out, following a risk‑based prioritization, to verify the possible presence of significant unmanaged residual impacts.

During the impact assessment process, related to EIA studies or BES assessments, Eni evaluates the possible interactions between company activities and the environment, considering the main global drivers of biodiversity loss, such as land and sea‑use change, overexploitation of natural resources, the global phenomenon of climate change, pollution, and the introduction of invasive species.
This analysis defines the operational and environmental context, identifies sensitive BES receptors, and assesses main dependencies and both direct84 and indirect85 impacts.

The significance of biodiversity impacts is determined by combining the magnitude of the impact (e.g., pressures generated by the project on environmental matrices) with the sensitivity of the BES receptor involved (e.g., presence of species at risk of extinction), and assigning a significance category.
If significant residual impacts are identified, they are managed through specific measures included in the site environmental management plans or through dedicated Biodiversity Action Plans (BAPs).

Local stakeholders are engaged from the earliest stages of a new project and throughout the project lifecycle, through required authorization processes and, where appropriate, voluntary initiatives, such as consultations or dedicated workshops.

Consultation and collaboration with communities, indigenous peoples, and other local stakeholders help to understand expectations and concerns, determine how ecosystem services and biodiversity are used, and develop management options that address local needs.

Material impacts In the absence of actions targeted at mitigation, Eni’s activities can cause significant negative impacts in terms of degradation or loss of biodiversity (habitats, ecosystems, and species) and of ecosystem services.
The extent of these impacts depends on project complexity, the value of the natural environment, and the social context of reference.

Among the most common impacts are those associated with changes in land (or sea) use and with the physical presence of plants and infrastructure, which can result in habitat removal, degradation, or fragmentation, with consequences for the distribution and abundance of species.

In Oil & Gas Upstream activities and in the large‑ scale development of plants for renewable energy generation, such impacts are particularly relevant when they involve natural or semi‑ natural areas86.
Moreover, wind plants can have negative impacts on vulnerable species, such as birds of prey, due to the risk of collision with turbines, wind blades, and distribution lines.

Downstream activities instead develop predominantly in contexts already industrialized; therefore, the contribution to changes in land use is generally limited and does not represent to date a material impact in terms of loss of biodiversity.

Material risks and opportunities [E4-1] The Process and results of the double materiality assessment did not identify material risks (including systemic risks)87 or currently realised opportunities related to biodiversity.

Nevertheless, Eni continues to monitor potential risks and opportunities by consulting publicly available scenarios, used to understand how changes in the state of nature may translate into physical or transition risks, as well as into opportunities, including those associated with nature‑based solutions.
Physical risks are associated with ecosystem degradation and potential reduction of natural resources, which may affect the operability and profitability of assets.

Transition risks, instead, derive from the evolution of the regulatory framework, environmental protection policies, and stakeholder expectations, which may result in limitations on access to natural resources or land use in certain geographical areas.
Within this scope are also included reputational risks, connected to the perception of the energy sector, which could affect litigation, reputation and access to financing.

In 2024, Eni conducted an internal analysis based on the qualitative approach outlined in the TNFD88 ‘Guidance on scenario analysis’ to assess the resilience of Eni’s strategy with respect to such risks.
The analysis was aimed in particular at exploring transition risks linked to key dependencies on nature.

Regarding physical risks, water represents the main dependency for some assets, as it is essential in production processes; the metrics on water withdrawals and use are reported in the Water resources chapter, while resilience to physical risks associated with reduced water availability (water stress, drought) is analysed as part of the assessment of asset resilience to climate‑related physical risks, in Climate Change chapter.

The relevance of (84) For the identification of direct impacts, any change (potential or actual) in the state of nature caused by an Eni activity with a direct causal link (due to the physical presence of plants and infrastructure and related activities such as emissions, discharges, and waste) is considered.

(85) For indirect impacts, an indirect causal link is considered (for example, greater colonization of the areas surrounding operational sites may lead to increased access to natural areas previously inaccessible through the opening of service roads).
(86) It is an ecosystem with most processes and biodiversity intact, albeit altered by human activity (IPBES glossary).

(87) Risks arising from the collapse of the system as a whole rather than from the malfunctioning of individual components; characterised by the indirect combination of minor tipping points that generate severe disruptions through cascading interactions between physical and transition risks.

(88) The TNFD (Taskforce on Nature‑related Financial Disclosures) is an international initiative born to help companies and financial institutions manage risks and opportunities linked to nature and biodiversity.

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 185 other dependencies on natural resources, analysed in terms of transition risks, varies by business type (for example, biomass sourcing is particularly significant for biofuel production).

In this context, the resilience analysis, conducted in 2024, focused on Eni’s direct operations and made reference to a publicly available scenario developed by ‘Inevitable Policy Response’ (Forecast Policy Scenario ‑ FPS89), to explore possible future trajectories.

The scenario, developed over the 2020‑2050 time horizon, highlights a global context characterized by a growing awareness of the importance of safeguarding biodiversity and ecosystem services, which could lead, through new regulations, to constraints on land availability for bioenergy crops in certain areas or for renewable energy installations.
Eni’s resilience to such risks is based on a strategy that integrates global portfolio diversification, the development of new technologies, and the adoption of circular business models.

In the Agribusiness context, for example, the company has implemented an approach that diversifies the type of agri‑feedstock used and sourcing regions, and it valorises waste and residues to reduce reliance on virgin raw materials.

Under the new approach, wind turbines are modelled as curvilinear surfaces based on rotor diameter.
The wind turbines have been grouped for adjacency analysis with protected areas.

This update enables a more accurate representation of the area actually occupied by the turbines, replacing the previous modelling based on broader wind farm area boundaries; this resulted in a downward revision of surface values compared to the previous reporting period.

For the sites overlapping protected areas or KBAs, and for Upstream concessions overlapping with operational activities within the overlap area, Eni initiates BES Assessment studies, according to a risk‑based prioritisation approach and, where necessary, defines a Biodiversity Action Plan (BAP) to manage significant residual impacts on such areas.
BAPs can also be developed for sites not overlapping with protected areas or KBAs, if required by EIA studies, financing or permitting conditions, or other management needs.

The main impact driver identified for Eni’s activities relates to land use change resulting from Oil & Gas Upstream infrastructures (for example pipeline installation and well pad construction) and from Plenitude’s electricity generation sites.
Such changes may entail habitat loss or degradation, with potential disturbances to species.

To mitigate this impact, the BAPs focus on two priority areas of intervention: (i) the restoration of natural habitats that have been modified or degraded; and (ii) monitoring campaigns aimed at confirming the presence of species at risk and assessing potential impacts of activities on their conservation status at the local level.
