RESOURCE USE AND CIRCULAR ECONOMY [ESRS E5] POLICIES [ESRS 2 MDR-P; E5-1] POLICY KEY CONTENTS: Code of Ethics • Commitment to the promotion of development models based on the regenerative principles of the circular economy that minimize the use of virgin resources and reduce waste, maximizing the recovery and valorisation of waste and residues.

Internal Regulatory System • Implementing actions across its operations aimed to improve efficiency, reduce waste, maximise the recovery and valorisation of waste and by‑products, use secondary raw materials or renewable sources, extend the useful life of assets and innovate processes and products, with the broader goal of creating long‑term value for both the environment and society.

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; E5-3] Although Eni has not adopted quantitative targets, aside from the business target relating to biorefining capacity outlined in the Strategy section, the Company promotes the prevention of waste generation in line with the priority criteria established under EU legislation and ensures proper waste management in accordance with its Internal Regulatory System.
Eni is also consistently engaged in implementing projects with a strong circular footprint (see Actions taken on material IROs).

To guide these processes, the Company adopts an Internal Regulatory System and an HSE Management System that set out operational guidelines for all business areas, informed by the operating context, environmental compliance requirements, stakeholder expectations, and the assessment of impacts, risks and opportunities.

The system also provides for semi‑annual monitoring of the actions required to implement these guidelines, supported by the collection of specific KPIs enabling effective performance monitoring and prompt intervention in the event of deviations from expected trends.

By their nature, Eni’s operations entail the production of waste, whose negative effects include: (i) the potential contamination of environmental matrices (soil, water and air) in cases of inadequate management, with possible consequences for local communities; (ii) impacts associated with the transport and treatment of waste at destination facilities; and (iii) land use associated to such facilities.
Waste generated by Eni originates both from production activities and from the remediation of contaminated soil and groundwater.

Remediation waste mainly consists of excavated soil and rock, demolition materials and groundwater; the latter, which in 2025 accounted for around 60% of total remediation waste, is treated in specific Groundwater Treatment Plants and is usually contaminated by hydrocarbons, benzene and dichloroethane.
The production of remediation waste is also linked to assets acquired by Eni from other companies, where Eni has never directly operated.

With regard to waste from production activities, produced water from Upstream operations, when not managed as discharge, represents the most significant contribution (approximately one‑third of total waste from production activities).
Produced water typically has very high salinity and variable composition, with residual components such as hydrocarbons and additives resulting from the separation process.

At the same time, the materiality assessment has also identified a positive environmental impact associated with circular economy actions, including the conversion and redevelopment of decommissioned assets and sites, as well as the use of renewable and recycled raw materials in production processes.
Material risks and opportunities The materiality assessment did not identify any specific material risks related to the circular economy.

Instead, it confirmed the presence of a material opportunity, linked to the Group’s ability to leverage its industrial expertise, technological solutions and internally developed know‑how to meet the growing demand for services and products promoting a more efficient use of resources.

In this context, on the one hand, this includes the remediation and waste‑management activities carried out by Eni Rewind, including services provided to third parties, and on the other hand, circular initiatives developed in other industrial areas, such as Enilive’s production of biofuels and Versalis’ projects for the recycling of plastics and other materials.
Circular economy initiatives are implemented across the Group’s business lines, generating market opportunities along the value chain and contributing to the evolution of its industrial models.

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; E5-2] The actions implemented by Eni on the basis of the material impacts, risks and opportunities identified are presented below and organised into two areas: (i) circular economy, for which the actions are described according to the different types of circularity interventions (extension of useful asset life, asset conversion and redevelopment; use of circular raw materials and alternative feedstocks; recycling and recovery technologies); (ii) waste.

Circular Economy Eni integrates circular economy principles into its business model, applying them both to the development of new product value chains and to existing ones, with the aim of improving efficiency in the use of resources and extending the lifecycle of assets and products.
This approach is implemented along the value chain and across the Group’s various industrial contexts, adapting to the operational specificities of each business.
Below is an overview of the main actions, organised by type of circular economy intervention.

The main resources allocated during the year to activities related to biorefineries and bio‑feedstock production amounted to €0.8 billion, while those dedicated to other circular economy initiatives totalled €0.1 billion.
For the next five‑year period, Eni has allocated €7 billion to biorefining/bio‑feedstock activities and €0.8 billion to other circular economy initiatives.

To support its initiatives, Eni has developed, together with the Scuola Superiore Sant’Anna, a model and tool for measuring circularity, incorporating the requirements of the UNI 11820 and ISO 59020 standards.

In line with its commitment to transparency and accountability, Eni has also obtained third‑party verification of its level of circularity under UNI 11820, both for support functions (business‑support processes and services provided at headquarters and local offices in Italy) and for the Gela biorefinery (in Sicily), thereby initiating a structured path of continuous analysis and improvement of its circularity performance.

EXTENSION OF USEFUL ASSET LIFE, ASSET CONVERSION AND REDEVELOPMENT Extending asset useful life, converting and redeveloping existing infrastructure represents a key lever of Eni’s circular economy approach, aimed at maximising the value of existing facilities throughout their lifecycle while reducing the consumption of new resources.
Within this framework, the transformation of traditional refineries into biorefineries plays a central role.

The conversion of the Livorno site forms part of the Group’s broader plan to expand its biorefining capacity from the current 1.65 million tonnes per year to more than 5 million tonnes per year by 2030.

Relating to extending the useful life of facilities, Plenitude contributes through maintenance and targeted component replacements, repowering projects, and the enhancement of existing infrastructure and synergies.

For example, in collaboration with other companies of the Group, remediated industrial areas have been redeveloped as sites for renewable energy production, combining environmental remediation with new energy generation capacity within a single territorial‑revitalisation strategy.

In this context, from 2025 a new Plenitude photovoltaic plant in Porto Marghera has become operational, complementing similar initiatives already implemented in Italy, in Porto Marghera, Assemini, Gela, Porto Torres, Priolo and Ponticelle.

In the Upstream business, circular economy principles are also being applied to the valorisation of mature assets, including assessments of the feasibility of converting offshore platforms in the Adriatic and Mediterranean Seas into infrastructure for subsea data centres, or converting Italian industrial sites into areas dedicated to renewable energy, logistics or data centres.

USE OF CIRCULAR RAW MATERIALS AND ALTERNATIVE FEEDSTOCKS The use of circular raw materials and alternative feedstocks plays a key role in reducing reliance on virgin resources and enabling materials to re‑enter production cycles, thereby contributing to the closure of their lifecycle.

This area encompasses biorefining initiatives, the production of advanced biofuels and the valorisation of residues and by‑products, as well as the development of industrial solutions aimed at expanding the use of circular materials across a wide range of applications.

Key initiatives include the production of advanced biofuels obtained mainly from residual feedstocks, such as used cooking oil, and the production of biomethane derived from organic residues, including agricultural and agro‑industrial by‑products, livestock effluents and organic waste.

For biofuels production, a residual share is sourced from vegetable oils derived from the cultivation of oilseed crops through certified supply chains which include programmes in various Countries in Africa, South‑East Asia and Central Asia, aimed at restoring degraded land and ensuring crop rotation in line with the EU’s RED Directive.
By‑products are also valorised and converted into animal feed and fertilisers, strengthening the circular value chain approach.

In the chemicals sector, Versalis aims to diversify feedstock sources using raw materials from renewable sources, such as biomass, and through the development of products derived from secondary raw materials from recycling94.

Additional circular solutions currently under development include residual materials for road‑infrastructure applications, recycled plastics or alternative raw materials in furniture production, and the use of recovered post‑industrial residues.

RECYCLING AND RECOVERY TECHNOLOGIES Initiatives in this area focus on promoting material recovery and the reintegration of waste into production cycles through the adoption of innovative recycling and valorisation technologies.

In the chemicals sector, investments are being directed towards the development of recycled materials and a range of technologies for the recycling of plastic and rubber, including through collaborations with partners across the value chain.
New facilities have been launched to convert plastic waste into secondary raw materials that can be reused in production processes.

At the same time, agreements have been established with other operators to strengthen an integrated recycling system, enabling the recovery and valorisation of different types of plastic waste and their transformation into new materials or products.
In the remediation sector, Eni Rewind is developing solutions aimed at the recovery and reuse of waste and contaminated soils.
In this context, new platforms dedicated to waste treatment and soil recovery are currently under development at the Ponticelle site (in Italy), with start‑up expected in 2026.

Further technologies capable of converting hard‑to‑recycle waste into new products, such as fuels, are also being assessed, and research projects are under way to further enhance recycling solutions in other sectors, including renewable energy.

Waste Across all its operational contexts, in addition to complying with applicable waste‑management regulations, Eni is committed to reducing the environmental impacts associated with the various phases of the waste‑management process.

The main actions in this area are based on the following principles set out in Eni’s Internal Regulatory System: • Sustainable operational waste management, inspired by the waste‑hierarchy model established by European legislation.
This model aims to prevent waste generation and prioritises recovery solutions over disposal.

In Italy, for example, through Eni Rewind’s brokerage activities, technically feasible solutions are selected based on the characteristics of the waste, giving priority to: (i) recovery operations; (ii) treatments that reduce the quantities sent for disposal; and (iii) the selection of suitable plants as close as possible to the production site; • Verification of third‑party reliability, ensured through targeted checks on the parties involved along the value chain and through periodic supplier assessments carried out by specialist technical units (e.g.
audits and operational controls).

In particular, in Italy and wherever required by current legislation, Eni relies exclusively on authorised operators; (94) Secondary raw materials are recycled materials that can be used in production processes either in place of, or alongside, virgin raw materials.

In 2025, more than 4 million tonnes of waste were generated at Eni, of which 1.5 million tonnes from production activities and 3.1 million tonnes from remediation activities, showing an overall increasing trend compared to 2024.
Waste from production activities increased during the year for both hazardous and non‑hazardous waste.

This trend was mainly driven by the increase in produced water at Eni Mediterranea Idrocarburi sites, in Sicily, the disposal of wastewater as waste at the Calenzano fuel depot (following the shutdown of the site water treatment plant due to the accidental event of December 2024), the volumes generated at the Livorno refinery in connection with the conversion of the site into a biorefinery, and those generated by construction works at the Porto Marghera biorefinery related to the construction of new plants (Steam Reforming Unit, Biojet section and Ecofining Deoxygenation section).
In 2025, almost 370 thousand tonnes of waste from production activities were sent for recovery and recycling, an increase compared to 2024.
Waste from remediation activities during the same period amounted to 3.1 million tonnes (of which more than 2.5 million tonnes were attributable to Eni Rewind), a figure decreasing compared to 2024.

The downward trend is mainly attributable: (i) the start‑up of the groundwater treatment plant at the Pomezia fuel depot, in Italy, which allowed the management of extracted groundwater as waste to be eliminated; (ii) the completion of remediation activities at the Eni Rewind sites of Avenza and Brindisi, in Italy; (iii) the reduction of activities at the Eni Rewind site of Manfredonia, in Italy, with a consequent reduction in the volumes of groundwater treated and managed as waste.
In terms of composition, the majority of remediation waste consists of groundwater treated in Groundwater Treatment Plants (almost 60% in 2025), partly reused and partly returned to the environment.
In 2025, more than 638 thousand tonnes of remediation waste were sent for recovery.

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 193 EU Taxonomy Regulation EU 852/2020 of the European Parliament and of the Council enacted in 2020 has established a classification system of economic activities based on criteria of environmental sustainability for the purposes of channeling productive investments.

An economic activity is environmentally sustainable where that economic activity: i) contributes substantially to one or more of the environmental objectives of the EU; ii) does not significantly harm any of the environmental objectives; iii) is carried out in compliance with the minimum safeguards foreseen by the Regulation, which are procedures implemented by an undertaking that is carrying out an economic activity to ensure a responsible business conduct.

As the next step, the Group evaluated the degree of alignment of its economic activities with the objectives of the Taxonomy through the verification of compliance with the ‘Technical Screening Criteria - TSC,’ which are the performance conditions for an economic activity to make a substantial contribution to the objective and respect the ‘do no significant harm’ principle to other objectives.
Furthermore, for each activity, compliance with the safeguard clause was verified.
The Group’s economic activities capable of making a substantial contribution to the climate change mitigation objective were identified.

The Group does not produce products or services for climate change adaptation, while activities contributing to environmental objectives, in consideration of the limited number of eligible activities and the selectivity of the TSC, are minimal, in the Eni consolidated financial statements.

Based on the reporting criteria established by the Commission through Delegated Act EU 2021/2178, the key performance indicators (KPIs) of Eni’s activities aligned with the Taxonomy for 2025 and the corresponding comparison period were calculated.

REPORTING OBLIGATIONS AND BASIS OF PRESENTATION With Delegated Regulation (EU) 2021/2178, the Commission defined the content and the presentation methods for the three performance indicators (‘KPIs’) related to the share of revenues, operating costs (‘opex’), and investments (‘capex’) associated with economic activities aligned with the total of these three items at the consolidated financial statements level, as well as the commentary information and the reporting templates.

POLICIES [TAXO; ESRS 2 MDR-P; S1-1; S2-1; S3-1; S4-1] POLICY KEY CONTENTS: Code of Ethics • Commitment to respecting human dignity and Human Rights, requiring the same commitment from all partners.
• Commitment to ensuring an inclusive work environment that values uniqueness and diversity as fundamental resources for the development of humanity.

ECG Policy on Respect for Human Rights in Eni(a) • Human rights due diligence process, reflecting the relevant international standards(b), which sets out Eni’s priority commitment to the Salient Human Rights Issues.

• Commitment to respecting and applying the principles set out in the ILO Tripartite Declaration of Principles concerning Multinational Enterprises and Social Policy, which includes the fundamental rights on work, as well as the rights set out in the international labour instruments for the promotion of decent working conditions.

• In cases of potential divergence between local and international standards, commitment to develop measures that allow conduct to be aligned with international standards while taking account of local principles, ensuring that risks relating to possible violations are duly analysed and assessed, so as to monitor the associated level of risk and verify the effectiveness of the management measures identified.

• Stakeholder engagement across all stages of the human rights due diligence process, involving active collaboration, together with grievance mechanisms and other reporting channels available both at central level and operating site level.

• Contractual safeguards (‘responsible contracting’), concerning human-rights compliance, which are consistent with applicable regulatory provisions, including specific ones relating to respect for workers’ rights.
• Commitment to promoting respect for human rights in activities carried out by or with partners and across interactions with stakeholders.

• Commitment to respecting the minimum age access to employment and the measures laid down in the applicable international and national norms on child labour and on the employment of minors and rejection of any form of forced or compulsory labour as well as any exploitative labour practices, including human trafficking, restrictions on freedom of movement and seizure of identity documents.

Supplier Code of Conduct • Requirement for suppliers to respect human rights, in line with the principles set out in the Eni Code of Ethics and ECG Policy on Respect for Human Rights in Eni, and to promote their adoption among their employees and the throughout the supply chain.
Internal Regulatory System • Clear definition of roles and responsibilities within the human rights management model.
(a) The principles reported in this section apply to the four stakeholder categories required under the ESRS: own workforce, workers in the value chain, affected communities, consumers and end-users.
For this reason, they have been addressed here in a cross-cutting manner.

ENI’S APPROACH TO HUMAN RIGHTS [TAXO] Human rights are a focus area for Eni’s Board of Directors, which in 2023 approved both the ECG Policy on Respect for Human Rights in Eni and the ECG Policy on Zero Tholerance against violence and harassment at work.
The topic is also regularly overseen by the Sustainability and Scenarios Committee, which each year receives updates on the human rights management system and the activities carried out.
In 2025, Eni continued to link management incentives to human rights performance, setting specific objectives for the various managerial levels.

The Company also strengthened awareness-raising and training efforts through courses for Eni personnel, targeted sessions on specific topics delivered also to its subsidiaries, and practical workshops for suppliers on safety and human rights.

HUMAN RIGHTS DUE DILIGENCE [TAXO; S1-4; S2-4; S3-4; S4-4] The journey undertaken in recent years to strengthen and embed a culture of respect for human rights has reinforced Eni’s human rights due diligence activities, as outlined in the ECG Policy Respect for Human Rights in Eni.

It has also established a shared responsibility across several functions — including Sustainability, Integrated Compliance, Human Resources, Procurement and Security — for managing the processes most relevant to human rights risks.

Human rights due diligence is a continuous process that considers the full spectrum of potential implications Eni’s activities may have on people, focusing on the so called ‘Salient Human Rights Issues95’.

Eni’s human rights management model adopts a multidisciplinary, multi-level and fully integrated approach within business processes, built on a risk-based logic to identify, prevent, mitigate and report adverse human rights impacts.

GOVERNANCE AND COMMITMENT DUE DILIGENCE ACCESS TO REMEDY Human rights have been incorporated into governance policies and processes, including through the structuring of appropriate training frameworks.
Eni has adopted a management system which includes a set of processes and tools to assess the most relevant issues, risks and impacts related to the respect for human rights.
Eni ensures adequate management of complaints through the ‘Grievance Mechanism’ and the whistleblowing process.

Eni’s human rights management model is grounded in the commitment of senior leadership and all company functions to uphold and embed human rights principles across governance instruments at every level.

A key pillar of the model is stakeholder engagement, supported by continuous and appropriate access to grievance mechanisms and reporting channels, as well as dedicated safeguards designed to ensure that any human rights violation is promptly identified, assessed and addressed, with appropriate remediation measures applied whenever violations are confirmed.
An integral part of the model is the communication of results, including through the Eni for sustainability report and the dedicated Eni for-Human Rights publication.

Eni’s human rights approach has been recognised as particularly solid and well structured by the World Benchmarking Alliance, which ranked the Company first in the Corporate Human Rights Benchmark99 (CHRB).

(95) A company’s salient human rights issues are those that stand out because they are at risk of suffering the most severe negative impact, taking into account the activities carried out, the contexts in which it operates, or the company’s business relationships.

(96) The Compliance Risk Assessment consists of the identification and evaluation of compliance risks, for the purpose of prioritizing them and optimizing the related mitigation measures (see (97) Risks related to the potential violation of human rights are assessed from a dual perspective: (i) the risk of causing (or contributing to causing) actual or potential adverse impacts, with reference to the UNGPs and the OECD Guidelines; (ii) the risk of incurring sanctions, significant financial losses, or reputational damage (so-called compliance risk).

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 197 Salient Human Rights Issues Eni’s commitment, management model and human rights activities focus on the issues considered most material for the Company, considering the nature of its business, the operational contexts in which it operates, and the perspectives of both local and international stakeholders.

The list of Salient Human Rights Issues, first identified in 2017, was updated in 2024 through dedicated workshops involving more than one hundred employees from different corporate functions, as well as meetings with a number of authoritative stakeholders — including institutions, specialised think tanks, industry associations, civil society organisations and NGOs.

The analysis led to the identification of 13 priority topics relating to workers, communities and consumers, along with an additional 5 emerging issues to be monitored due to their relevance to specific business segments or operational contexts.

These models help gather insights on the operating context, identify potential risk factors based on the specific activities and business processes involved, and implement appropriate prevention and mitigation measures in line with the level of risk.

Access to remedial measures, whistleblowing process and grievance mechanisms [TAXO; S1-3; S1-17; S2-3; S3-3; S4-3] Eni is committed to adopting — also in collaboration with third parties — appropriate remediation measures in response to any negative impacts it has caused or contributed to causing, and to making every possible effort to ensure remediation where an impact is directly linked to its activities, products or services.
To this end, Eni also seeks to use its leverage with third parties to help ensure that any negative impacts directly connected to their activities are effectively addressed.

In line with this commitment and with international standards, Eni’s human rights management model relies on mechanisms for receiving complaints, alleged human rights violations and concerns raised by stakeholders, individuals, communities or associations, with particular attention to vulnerable groups.
These tools enable the Company to identify, assess and address potential issues and, where impacts are confirmed, to implement appropriate remediation measures in a timely manner.

Stakeholders have access to two specific channels in cases of alleged human rights violations: (i) the Grievance Mechanism, which manages the submission, handling and resolution of concerns, with a dedicated review process for human rights-related grievances classified as ‘significant’ (see Local Communities); and (ii) the Whistleblowing system, which allows anyone — employees or third parties — to report, confidentially or anonymously, suspected violations of the Internal Control System or the Code of Ethics.

Regarding Whistleblowing Reports100, during the year the review of 40 human rights related whistleblowing files was completed, primarily involving potential impacts on workers’ rights and occupational health and safety.

In total, 68 assertions were examined, of which 16 were at least partially substantiated, leading to corrective actions to mitigate and/or minimise the impacts, including: (i) actions within the Internal Control and Risk Management System, such as implementing or strengthening existing controls; (ii) awareness-raising activities on the Code of Ethics and the ECG Policy on Zero Tolerance against Violence and Harassment at Work; and (iii) employee related measures, including disciplinary actions in accordance with the collective labour agreement and applicable national regulations.

Disputes and non-judicial remedy mechanisms [TAXO; S1-17; S2-4; S3-4; S4-4] Eni cooperates with other non judicial remediation mechanisms, such as those established under the OECD Guidelines and managed by the OECD National Contact Points operating in various Countries.
Eni does not in any way hinder access to judicial or non judicial mechanisms, as well as institutional mechanisms.

Eni actively supports and facilitates the use of judicial, non judicial and institutional mechanisms, consistently adopting a proactive approach and providing all necessary information and channels to ensure their effective operation.

In 2025, Eni and its subsidiaries did not record any severe human rights incidents101, nor did they receive any final convictions for breaches of laws, regulations or other regulatory provisions relating to human rights.

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; S1-5] Eni’s human capital targets and commitments are defined based on the evolution of its workforce structure and in line with the Company’s medium to long-term energy transition strategy.
They take into account historical trends and forecasts on employment dynamics, with ongoing monitoring and regular updates of both results and future objectives.

Monitoring activities are conducted quarterly through standard reporting shared with HR managers across the various business areas, enabling the definition and implementation of corrective actions where needed.

Key workforce data and related trends are also presented to employee representatives during the European Works Council and the Global Framework Agreement meetings to illustrate developments and discuss progress.

Additionally, on a quarterly basis, the Company reviews and monitors the indicators underlying the targets, to ensure that performance trends remain aligned with development plans, to identify any emerging issues, and to define corrective actions where needed.

200 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3; S1 SBM-3] Material impacts Eni places human capital102 at the heart103 of its strategy, supporting the development of employees’ skills to foster professional growth and promoting workforce well-being through a range of welfare initiatives.

At the same time, to safeguard against potential negative impacts that its activities may have on workers, Eni maintains a strong focus on consistently upholding human rights in the workplace (such as working hours, fair wages, freedom of association and collective bargaining, and job security).

Particular emphasis is also placed on the principle of non-discrimination, equal treatment and equal opportunities (in hiring, training, career development and career progression) as well as on preventing physical, psychological or verbal violence or harassment, including gender-based forms.
The energy sector is often characterized by complex working conditions, including night shifts and extended work schedules required to ensure operational continuity.
Exploration, development and production activities frequently take place far from populated areas, requiring rotational work arrangements over several days or weeks.

Moreover, although workers in the sector are traditionally represented by trade unions and covered by collective bargaining agreements, some employees operate in Countries where these rights are limited and therefore face a higher risk of intimidation or unfair treatment.

Working conditions, work environments, skills requirements and the nature of roles across the sector, combined with the predominance of certain workforce groups (such as male-dominated roles), may also lead to potential discrimination on the basis of ethnicity, gender, sexual orientation, disability, religion, nationality or employment status.

Finally, potential impacts on the workforce may also arise from the industrial restructuring required to advance the decarbonisation pathway, which will entail the transformation of certain production sites, such as basic chemicals and traditional refining.

Material Risks and opportunities The expected evolution of Eni’s business activities and of the labour market, together with new strategic directions and the challenges posed by technological change, require a significant and ongoing commitment to strengthening human capital.

This includes upskilling and reskilling initiatives aimed at enriching or redirecting employees’ competencies, as well as attracting new talent by leveraging the opportunities offered by emerging skill sets in the market to support the development of new technologies and businesses.

Material risks related to Eni’s own workforce are detailed in the Health & Safety chapter, while potential risks concerning the loss of confidentiality of sensitive information, including those linked to Cybersecurity, are addressed in the Cybersecurity chapter.

EMPLOYEE ENGAGEMENT104 [S1 SBM-2; S1-2; S1-3; S1-8] Industrial relations Eni’s industrial relations model, managed by a dedicated function, plays a central role in fostering constructive relationships with workers and safeguarding their rights.
At international level, Eni established its European Works Council (EWC)106 in 1995.

Within the scope of the European Economic Area, the EWC primarily focuses on information concerning activity programmes, investments, acquisitions or disposals of businesses, employment prospects, occupational health and safety, the energy transition pathway and sustainability.
The EWC includes representatives of Italian and European Eni workers, representatives of Italian trade union organisations, and a representative of the European trade union IndustriAll Europe.

An additional European instrument is the European Observatory for Workers’ Health, Safety and Environment, which serves as a platform for sharing data and analytical, management and monitoring tools relating to occupational accidents, incidents and illnesses, Stakeholder Engagement chapter.
(102) It includes all direct employees operating in Italy and abroad.
Contractors are excluded from this boundary and are instead considered as value chain workers.

It also aims to contribute, in collaboration with stakeholders, to the development of a clear and stable regulatory framework that is conducive to investment and capable of balancing economic and financial sustainability with environmental and social sustainability.

One annual meeting of both the EWC and the European Observatory for Workers’ Health, Safety and Environment is envisaged, together with at least three annual meetings of the EWC Select Committee involving the relevant Eni functions.
Finally, in January 2026, Eni signed the new Global Framework Agreement on International Industrial Relations, Corporate Social Responsibility and a Just Transition (GFA).

The GFA provides for annual engagement with Eni’s international and European workers’ delegates, representatives of Italian trade union organisations, and a representative of the global trade union IndustriALL Global Union.

The agreement forms part of Eni’s concrete commitment to guiding sustainability orientations, defining strategies based on the principles of integrity and transparency, promoting the fight against corruption, respecting human and labour rights, ensuring health and safety, and protecting the environment and sustainable development.
For each meeting, detailed documentation is shared in advance and formal minutes are subsequently prepared and signed by both parties, documenting the matters discussed and the agreements reached.

Whistleblowing mechanisms and remediation processes In line with the commitments undertaken and in accordance with international standards, Eni has established mechanisms for receiving complaints and reports that can be used in the event of an alleged violations107 of human rights and consequently trigger a remediation process.
These mechanisms are further described in the chapter Human Rights for Eni.

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; S1-4] The actions implemented by Eni on the basis of the material impacts, risks and opportunities identified are presented below, organised into the following areas: (i) Human rights management model; (ii) Pay Equity; (iii) Work-life balance and welfare; (iv) Diversity & Inclusion; (v) People development and feedback; (vi) Training and skills development.
Human Rights Management Model Since 2020, Eni has introduced a risk-based model for assessing the safeguarding of human rights in the workplace.

The model is designed to segment Group companies on the basis of quantitative and qualitative parameters that capture the characteristics and specific risks of the Country and operating context, and that are linked to human resources management processes (including the prevention of all forms of discrimination, gender equality, working conditions, and freedom of association and collective bargaining).
This approach enables the identification of potential risks and areas for improvement, for which specific actions are defined and monitored over time.
The model is regularly updated to reflect changes in the relevant external context and in Eni’s Internal Regulatory System, with a view to continuous improvement.
The mitigation actions identified following the application of the model are therefore communicated to all Eni companies.

With regard to impacts, in 2025, following confirmed cases of physical, psychological or verbal violence and harassment (identified through the whistleblowing channel), Eni implemented disciplinary measures modulated in relation to behaviours up to dismissal and suspensions from work against the perpetrators of harassment in the most serious cases.
In addition, it has implemented awareness-raising initiatives on the Code of Ethics and the Zero Tolerance Policy.
In addition, actions were taken within the Internal Control and Risk Management System to support the implementation and strengthening of existing controls.

Pay Equity Eni is committed to promoting fair and inclusive working conditions, in line with United Nations objectives on wage improvement, the reduction of income inequalities, the promotion of decent work, gender, generational and ethnic equality, in accordance with the principle of ‘equal pay for equal work’.

To this end, Eni applies a globally integrated remuneration system to all its employees, aligned with reference markets and linked to both company and individual performance, while fully complying with applicable local legislation.
The system sets salary levels using the market median as a benchmark, ensuring fair and competitive remuneration that is commensurate with roles and acquired competencies.
Remuneration levels are designed to ensure a decent standard of living and consistently exceed subsistence thresholds, statutory or contractual minimum levels, and minimum market benchmarks.
As part of its remuneration policies, Eni is also implementing a Broad-Based Share Ownership Plan for the 2024-2026 period, open to all Eni employees in Italy and abroad.

The plan provides for the free allocation of shares in 2024 and 2025 and a co-investment mechanism in 2026, with the objective of strengthening employees’ sense of belonging and their participation in the creation of long-term corporate value.

Finally, in connection with the implementation of EU Directive 2023/970 on pay transparency, Eni launched a dedicated project in 2025 to review and further enhance its policies and processes, providing additional assurance of gender equality as well as pay equity and transparency.
(107) Any cases of confirmed human rights violations are further addressed in the chapters remediation actions undertaken are described.
The model also includes a broad range of welfare measures supporting parenthood, disability, and the health of employees and their cohabiting family members.
The SW model has been progressively extended to other Countries, in compliance with local legislation.

As of June 2025, a revised SW model has been introduced, providing additional flexibility and inclusiveness and promoting improved work-life balance through, among other measures, an enhanced welfare package, the possibility to perform SW from abroad, and further extensions to address parenthood and health related needs; • parenthood support initiatives: in all Countries where Eni operates, employees are granted conditions no less favourable than those established under international standards for maternity and paternity.
In addition, Eni guarantees: at least 14 weeks of leave for the primary carer, as per ILO Conventions, with 100% salary coverage, and 20 fully paid working days of leave for the second parent.

Further measures to support parenthood are also promoted; in 2025, Eni strengthened the range of parenthood services introduced through the ‘Protocollo Noi’, signed with trade unions in 2024; these include income support measures such as reimbursement for nursery and babysitting expenses.

In 2025, a new digital platform was launched to provide employees with access to a wide range of discounted agreements; • corporate volunteering initiatives: employees at Eni and its subsidiaries may use up to two working days per year to engage in volunteering activities either by joining Eni promoted initiatives carried out through partnerships with third sector organisations or by independently choosing an organisation where they wish to volunteer.

Diversity & Inclusion Eni’s approach to Diversity & Inclusion (D&I) is grounded in the principles of non-discrimination, equal opportunities and the inclusion of all forms of diversity, as well as integration and work-life balance.
All D&I initiatives are coordinated by a dedicated corporate function.

Given Eni’s global footprint, valuing people’s uniqueness is not only an ethical commitment but also a strategic driver for growth and innovation in support of the Company’s evolving development model.

In 2025, Eni began a review of its D&I framework to adapt its initiatives to the current context: starting with an internal assessment involving top management listening sessions and external benchmarking, Eni identified the priority focus areas that will shape D&I initiatives from 2026 onwards.

During 2025, D&I actions focused on four main pillars: • listening: listening initiatives addressed to all Eni employees represent a core element of the D&I strategy and are carried out annually across international locations and Group companies.
In 2025, design thinking methodologies were applied to explore issues related to disability and intergenerational dynamics.

In addition, the first Employee Resource Groups were established at the end of the year, dedicated to gender equality and interculturality, enabling the development of initiatives also at local level; • training: D&I training offerings were enriched through dedicated programmes on leadership and inclusive language, updates to training for employees on international assignments, and a course focused on parenthood.

The latter included a self-assessment tool aimed at enhancing and recognising competencies for female employees returning from maternity leave; • communication and awareness-raising: throughout the year, communication initiatives addressed different dimensions of diversity through parallels with sport.

In international locations, dedicated D&I workshops were organised to engage local employees on country-specific issues, with a particular focus on interculturality and women; • women’s empowerment: Eni continued to advance women’s empowerment through multiple initiatives.

In 2025, the Company implemented a gender equality management system in accordance with UNI/PdR 125:2022 and obtained the related certification for Eni SpA Italy, confirming its commitment to an inclusive organisational model aligned with Eni’s strategic priorities.

This process enabled a more structured management of gender equality issues and the identification of improvement actions, which were included in a plan approved by the Gender Equality Committee established during the year.
MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 203 orientation programmes promoting STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics) disciplines and careers in the energy sector.
Collaboration with ‘Valore D’108 continued, as did cooperation — within procurement — with Open-es, to promote D&I principles along the supply chain, with a specific focus on SMEs.
In the first half of 2025, Eni designed and implemented the WIP (Women in Power) project, aimed at fostering women’s empowerment.
The initiative involved 37 female employees, in addition to the 17 participants from the 2024 edition, together with their managers and the relevant HR functions.
The programme combined dedicated training with the development of a tailored action plan for each participant.
The partnership with ‘Woman X Impact’, the annual summit focused on gender parity, women’s leadership and self-branding through female networking, was also renewed.
In parallel, Eni strengthened its commitment to preventing and combating gender-based violence.
In 2024, the Company defined a dedicated strategy and established a cross-functional working group involving multiple corporate functions and affiliated companies.
The Group promotes prevention and response initiatives both within and outside the workplace, targeting employees and local communities.

Starting from the end of January 2025, Eni made available psychological, economic and organisational support measures for female employees who are victims of violence outside the workplace, alongside awareness-raising initiatives addressed to the entire workforce.

People development and feedback Eni’s business model is based on internal competencies, which represent a strategic asset in which the Company continues to invest to ensure alignment with business needs and long-term strategic objectives.

To increase the value of its human capital over time, Eni prioritises reskilling and upskilling initiatives and people development plans, with the objective of supporting the redeployment of employees into new or transformed activities.

With respect to workforce impacts arising from the industrial conversion process, Eni is committed to: (i) renewing and evolving skills to support the Company’s transformation in line with decarbonisation objectives; (ii) developing the satellite operating model; (iii) improving organisational efficiency within transversal support functions; and (iv) restructuring traditional business sectors, including through initiatives aimed at enhancing available internal skills via targeted training and internal mobility programmes.

In parallel, Eni remains strongly committed to attracting high-quality talent with distinctive profiles aligned with the evolving needs of its business lines, in line with strategic developments and the requirements expressed by the various business areas.

In 2025, key actions in this area focused on: • skills development (soft and hard skills): through initiatives aimed at embedding a new model of capabilities and behaviours within the corporate culture to effectively manage the transition.
This included the review of professional models to promote the development of more integrated and comprehensive profiles, supporting a continuous upskilling approach.
Training initiatives covered topics such as the circular economy, decarbonisation and renewable energy.

In addition, in 2025 Eni designed and delivered a company-wide training programme on the responsible use of artificial intelligence, raising awareness of regulatory, ethical and governance aspects, corporate applications, data literacy and the personal skills required for conscious AI use.

Additional initiatives were aimed at preparing pools of employees capable of effectively representing Eni’s strategy and businesses in various brand exposure contexts (Global Ambassador Programme); • career-orientation programmes: aimed in particular at younger generations, supporting more informed educational and professional pathways; • communication and Employer Branding: reinforced through recruitment campaigns across major media channels — digital and traditional — to strengthen Eni’s positioning as an employer of choice.
Furthermore, through its internal procedures, Eni promotes and values the continuous and widespread use of feedback as a key enabler of its corporate culture and values.

Timely, objective, constructive feedback, recorded through corporate systems, supports the development and engagement of Eni people not only within formal processes (such as performance evaluations), but also on an ongoing basis whenever dialogue and mutual listening between managers and employees are required.

Performance evaluation is founded on objectives aligned with (108) A non-profit business association that promotes gender balance, the recognition and enhancement of diversity, and the development of an inclusive organisational culture.

For executives and senior managers, business and behavioural goals are included; for all other employees, qualitative and quantitative objectives consistent with their responsibilities are set alongside behavioural objectives, which may be updated during the year based on discussion between manager and employee.

Training and skills development Eni’s training initiatives address the full spectrum of professional growth, encompassing technical-professional, transversal and personal development through upskilling and reskilling programmes, delivered through a balanced mix of in-person and remote learning.
Training represents a key enabler for building the skills required for the future, in line with the strategic priorities and objectives of the Company.
The effectiveness of training programmes is systematically assessed through final questionnaires completed by participants to verify the achievement of learning objectives.

Depending on the type of programme, assessment methods typically include: (i) for technical and specialist training paths, a final learning test; (ii) for mandatory health and safety training, practical and/or theoretical tests required to pass the course; (iii) for language courses, final tests to certify the achievement of the expected proficiency level; (iv) for behavioural training programmes, self-assessment questionnaires on the competencies acquired.

With reference to significant workforce-related expenditures incurred by Eni in 2025 (excluding labour costs reported in Note 30 ‘Costs’ of the consolidated financial statements), total training expenses amounted to €32.1 million (of which €0.27 million for D&I activities).
Over the next five-year period, such expenses are expected to amount to €176.6 million, of which €2 million for D&I initiatives.
For other significant workforce-related expenditures, see the Health & Safety chapter.

206 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX Employment and Diversity & Inclusion In 2025, Eni’s total workforce remained broadly stable compared to 2024 (-146 employees).

This trend reflects the combined effect of limited M&A transactions (primarily the deconsolidation of certain overseas Natural Resources assets, including in Tunisia and Congo, and companies operating in the CCS sector in the United Kingdom), as well as the transfer of Overseas personnel to joint ventures, largely offset by a slight positive balance between hires and terminations confirming Eni’s commitment towards the renewal of skills necessary to support the energy transition.
During the year, Eni carried out 2,790 hires (-6.4% ca.
Of these, 2,486 were permanent hires (-5% compared to 2024) with a proportion of female personnel equal to approx.
Employees aged 30 or under accounted for around 46% of permanent recruitments.
A total of 2,703 employment terminations were recorded (700 in Italy and 2,003 abroad).
Of these, 2,378 involved employees on permanent contracts (-15% ca.
vs. 2024), with female employees representing approximately 39% of terminations.
Among permanent employees whose employment ended in 2025, 74% were under the age of 50.
The turnover rate stood at 7.5%, down by 1.3 percentage points compared to 2024, mainly reflecting a reduction in terminations.
The share of local personnel employed abroad remained broadly stable, averaging around 85% over the last three years.
The average age of Eni’s global workforce increased slightly to 45.3 years (46 years in Italy and 43.6 years abroad), compared to 44.9 years in 2024, mainly due to the lower turnover rate.
The number of non-employee workers varies according to business needs and operational flexibility requirements, as well as potential conversions into permanent contracts.
Compared to 2024, the number of non-employee workers decreased slightly, mainly as a result of M&A transactions.
Industrial relations In Italy, all employees are covered by collective bargaining agreements in accordance with applicable legislation.
Abroad, coverage by collective bargaining agreements amounts to 48.32%, reflecting the different regulatory frameworks in place across the Countries where Eni operates.

In contexts where employees are not covered by collective bargaining agreements, Eni nevertheless ensures full compliance with applicable international and local labour legislation, as well as the application of higher protection standards guaranteed across the Group through Eni’s worldwide corporate policies.
People development and feedback In 2025, performance evaluations covered 92% of Eni’s executives, middle managers and young graduates.

Full coverage was maintained for the executive population, while coverage for middle managers and young graduates remained broadly in line with the previous year, supported by the consolidated adoption of the new performance evaluation form for non-managerial employees.

The slight decrease in coverage compared to the previous year was mainly due to specific circumstances, including corporate transactions, hires completed in the final months of the year, and a limited number of cases involving prolonged absences.
Training and skills development In 2025, a slight increase was recorded across the various indicators compared to the previous year.
In particular, total training hours increased by 3%, while average total hours per employee rose by 4.4%.
Total training expenditure and average expenditure per employee also increased, by 2.5% and 4.1% respectively.

During the year, more than one million training hours were delivered, 77% of which were completed by men and 23% by women, consistent with the previous year, with a 1.5% increase in average hours per woman.

Remuneration and adequate wages In 2025, the ratio between the remuneration of the CEO/Managing Director and the median employee remuneration (Total Remuneration Ratio) is equal to 138, decreased compared to 2024, with a significant change mainly due to the lower value of the long-term share-based incentive.

The global Gender Pay Gap amounted to 10%, increasing compared to the previous year following the integration of newly acquired companies in Countries characterised by heterogeneous workforce distribution structures and remuneration levels compared to other Countries.

As calculated in accordance with the applicable standard, the indicator may be influenced by objective, non-discriminatory factors, including role and professional category, seniority, working hours and conditions (such as shift work and related allowances), individual performance, and the size and distribution of the female workforce relative to the male workforce.

To ensure a robust assessment, Eni performs additional analyses, based on the same objective factors mentioned above, in order to identify any unjustified gaps and implements appropriate corrective measures.
In 2025, analyses conducted at comparable role and seniority levels identified an average global pay gap of 2.3%, which was not statistically significant and in line with 2024.

To ensure adequate and decent wages, Eni applies remuneration benchmarks in each Country of operation, that are significantly higher than applicable statutory or contractual minimum wages and above the 1° decile of the local remuneration market.
The Company annually reviews the remuneration positioning of its workforce and adopts corrective actions where necessary.

Benchmarks include statutory or contractual minimum wages and the minimum remuneration levels of medium-to-large local companies, which are well above the poverty thresholds defined by Eurostat for the European Union and by Wage Indicator for other Countries.
Further information on pay gap indicators, minimum wage benchmarks and Eni’s remuneration policies is provided in the Report on the 2026 Remuneration Policy and remuneration paid 2025.

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 207 HEALTH & SAFETY [ESRS S1; ESRS S2; ENTITY-SPECIFIC] POLICIES [ESRS 2 MDR-P; S1-1; S2-1] POLICY KEY CONTENTS: Code of Ethics • Principle of promoting health and safety in compliance with the highest international standards and the specific laws and regulations of all Countries in which we operate, to ensure a management based on the principles of precaution, prevention, protection, and risk management.

• Commitment to providing suitable tools for prevention and protection from any culpable or malicious behaviour, including from third parties, which could cause direct or indirect damage to Eni People and/or to the company’s tangible or intangible resources.

• Commitment to clarity and transparency of information flows towards Eni People, the community and our partners about the necessary preventive and protective measures to be implemented in order to eliminate (and when not possible, mitigate) the risks and critical issues of the processes and activities in which they are involved.
• Commitment to spread a culture of health and safety and avoid any engagement in illegal or dangerous behaviour.

Supplier Code of Conduct • Commitment required of suppliers to identify and assess health and safety risks in advance, in order to be able to manage and prevent them, and contribute to the preparedness and effective management of emergencies, and to pursue continuous improvement by taking part in KPI monitoring and control, proactively adopting good practices and sharing the lessons learned from all accident events.

ECG Policy on Respect for Human Rights in Eni • Commitment to ensuring a safe and healthy work environment for all workers, with a view to continuous improvement and empowerment of all levels in the company.
• Safeguarding the physical, psychological, and social well-being of its people, with a special focus on situations of greater vulnerability and promoting access to adequate healthcare services.

Internal Regulatory System • Operational procedures to ensure health surveillance and prevent the onset of occupational illnesses; health risk-based clustering and related requirements; procedures for managing health emergencies.
• Management of health emergency procedures.
• Operational procedures for developing effective prevention and protection measures to safeguard personnel, suppliers, and owned assets, in line with the HSE regulatory system.

• Principles relating to occupational safety and industrial hygiene, as well as process safety, aimed at preventing significant accident risks through the application of high managerial and technical standards, product safety, emergency management, and the promotion of a safety culture.

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; S1-5] The targets and commitments on health and safety topics, in line with last year, are linked to the principles set out in the Policies and specifically refer to activities aimed at safeguarding workers’ physical and psychological health with reference to the work environment and situations of vulnerability, as well as to people’s safety.

These targets are agreed with the organisational units responsible for achieving them, and the safety topic (using a dedicated indicator defined by Eni based on TRIR) forms part of the variable incentive scheme for the CEO and management.

Monitoring of targets and commitments is carried out on a semi-annual basis through the Health, Safety, and Environment review processes and, more frequently, through the use of specific metrics to ensure appropriate actions in cases of misalignment with expected trends.
The target’s reference scope applies exclusively to Eni-operated entities; the target confirms the commitment to continuous improvement of performance in coherence with Eni’s new strategic plan.

208 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3; S1 SBM-3; S2 SBM-3; ENTITY-SPECIFIC] Material impacts The presence, often in remote locations, of assets and facilities for oil, gas, and energy production makes effective risk management necessary to protect the safety of people, communities, and operations in relation to potential occupational injuries and incidents involving assets and infrastructure, which may also generate negative environmental impacts.
The primary focus is on identifying and mitigating potential hazards that could result in adverse impacts.

Alongside safety, workers’ health and well-being also represent an invaluable asset for the Company, which is protected and promoted through health promotion initiatives aimed at both its own workers and those in the value chain.

However, the performance of work activities may lead to potential occupational illnesses arising from prolonged exposure to risk agents present in the working environment, affecting both the company’s own workers and those in the value chain.
The impact may be caused either by the work performed or by the environment in which the work itself takes place.
The main occupational illnesses may result from exposure to chemical, biological, or physical agents or may be linked to ergonomic or psychosocial factors.

Material risks and opportunities In reference to material risks, Eni identifies the following: (i) major process incidents (for example fires/explosions, spills or releases of hazardous substances) and asset integrity incidents; (ii) incidents associated with other ‘non-process’ activities, such as road and rail transport services, maritime transport, service stations, gas distribution networks and LPG pipeline networks; (iii) blowouts resulting from the occurrence of an uncontrolled flow of hydrocarbons within wells.
In addition, the global security risk109 has been identified as material.

This risk is linked to the possible occurrence of adverse scenarios and/or potential threats in areas of strategic interest for Eni arising from intentional or negligent actions or events of a criminal or political nature, with possible impacts on Eni people, in particular on groups of workers operating in such contexts.
Finally, among the material risks for Eni there is also the biological risk, related to the possible spread of epidemics and pandemics.
To this end, Eni constantly analyses and monitors local epidemiological contexts for better prevention and management of eventual outbreaks and emerging pandemics.
No material opportunities were identified.

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; S1- 4] To confirm that employees’ health and safety are an essential and non-negotiable value for Eni and fundamental value for the working conditions of each individual, the highest level of supervision is ensured with the goal of achieving safe operations.
In this regard, all entities with significant risk are covered by ISO 9001, 14001, 45001 and 50001 certifications or have planned to obtain them.
For the mitigation and management of health and safety risks, a risk-based management system has been established.
Within this framework, all incident events, including near misses and unsafe conditions/acts, are reported, analysed and monitored with the necessary corrective and preventive actions.
This system is continuously improved, taking into account events occurring in our operations and across the industry.

Below are the actions implemented by Eni based on the identified material impacts, risks and opportunities, divided into: (i) People safety; (ii) Process safety; (iii) Asset integrity; (iv) People’s health; (v) Health and safety in the value chain.

People safety Eni continuously invests in implementing the necessary actions to ensure the safety of people in the workplace, particularly through the development of models and tools for risk assessment and management, as well as the promotion of a safety culture, in order to pursue its commitment to zero injuries.

To prevent injuries, in addition to the continuous updating of management documents and operating instructions, the main actions implemented in 2025 focused on: • initiatives to raise awareness and engage workers on safety topics (Safety Golden Rules and Principles, training programme on Safety Leadership, Technical and Behavioural Safety Coaching Program, promotion of Stop Work Authority110); • use of an artificial intelligence tool (Safety Presence), solution capable of predicting recurring hazardous situations based on weak signals, recorded in safety databases.

Since its deployment, this tool has generated about 800 alerts that have led to the implementation of targeted preventive actions; • implementation of new digital technologies to support operational safety and HSE processes; • application of the behavioural and human reliability analysis model (THEME methodology) to identify intervention strategies to strengthen human barriers.

(109) For further details on the link between risks and Eni’s strategy and business model, please refer to the chapter and the chapters (110) With Stop Work Authority, every worker, at any Eni site, has the authority to stop an activity when they detect a hazardous behaviour or condition.
Integrated Risk Management and Risk factors and uncertainty.

In terms of people and asset safety, Eni has allocated resources of €1.8 billion for the next five years, of which, in particular, for facilities, equipment, and fire protection management (€0.5 billion), plants and vehicles safety (€0.4 billion), maintenance of facilities and safety equipment (€0.4 billion), and controls, supervision, inspections, and testing (€0.4 billion).

Asset integrity Eni applies the Asset Integrity process to its facility development and management activities, in order to ensure the highest level of integrity in design and construction, as well as the utmost rigour in operations, up to decommissioning, thus managing risks in compliance with people safety and the environment protection.
In the context of asset integrity, Eni assigns a central role to technical monitoring activities.

These activities make it possible to verify, in a structured and independent manner, the adequacy of operational safeguards, the effectiveness of integrity management processes and the correct implementation of regulatory and corporate requirements, while at the same time disseminating lessons learned and best practices.
Within this framework, there is a dedicated function that oversees, on a cross-cutting basis, the technical and operational assessment process of Eni’s strategic industrial assets.

The function operates in close integration with cross-functional working groups dedicated to asset integrity in both the upstream and downstream areas, fostering a coordinated and shared approach to the planning and execution of verification activities.

During 2025, in support of technical monitoring in the area of asset integrity, two key tools were developed: (i) a software tool (AIPE Tool) providing a single and integrated environment for the collection and management of Asset Integrity verification data, the reduction of information redundancies and structured monitoring through dashboards and standardised KPIs; (ii) a processing tool based on artificial intelligence techniques supporting the dedicated function in the systematic analysis of assessments.
Taken together, the two tools strengthen the overall effectiveness of the technical monitoring system, enabling an integrated, data driven and prevention-oriented approach to emerging risks.

The application of the health management system, understood as a set of actions aimed at continuous improvement, ensures a constant commitment to mitigating negative impacts and its implementation is periodically monitored also through audit activities.

The health management system relies both on internal resources, healthcare and managerial professionals, and on a network of specialised external providers, such as universities and centres of healthcare excellence.
With regard to future resources, investments planned for health-related activities for the 2026-2030 five-year period amount to approximately €357 million.

Health and Safety in the value chain [S2-4] Eni is committed to improving the safety of contracted works and to the provision of specialist training and coaching services, as well as to providing operational HSE support to the business.

In this context, the main actions carried out in 2025 concerned: • proactive oversight and support in the supplier improvement process, promoting management models characterised by an increasingly preventive culture of safety and environmental protection, promptly addressing situations identified as below standard and enhancing the innovative good practices identified, ensuring their sharing among contractors.

The Safety and Environment Pacts (voluntary agreements with companies) active in 2025 covered 65 sites in Italy and 22 abroad and in 2026 will be extended not only to major energy transition projects (Sannazzaro de Burgondi and Priolo biorefineries, as well as the Brindisi Giga Factory), but also to additional entities abroad of Versalis, Enilive and to certain companies/JVs in the Global Natural Resources sector; • training and awareness-raising activities on occupational health and safety with strategic suppliers to promote the key principles of safety culture.

The programme focused on the active involvement of management, on the strengthening and monitoring of competencies, on the application of the ‘Stop Work Authority’ principle and on the adoption of all measures necessary to ensure safety.

A webinar was also delivered to contractors, with the aim of promoting proper management of the skills of personnel employed in contracted works and the correct organisation of operating teams and involved subcontractors, while at the same time fostering the sharing of sector best practices.

Within the framework of a global partnership with the International Labour Organization (ILO), Eni implements a structured programme aimed at strengthening occupational health and safety and expanding access to social protection along agribusiness value chains.
In Kenya, initiatives have reached an advanced stage of implementation, translating analytical assessments into concrete actions at operational level.
In Côte d’Ivoire, specific assessments made it possible to identify priority areas for improving health and safety practices and social protection coverage.

Building on these experiences, the partnership was extended to the Republic of the Congo, where activities were launched at the end of 2025 and are currently focused on programme set-up and stakeholder engagement.

The programme involves small and large farmers, aggregators, cooperatives and producers along the value chain, contributing to improved working conditions and alignment with international labour standards.
METRICS [ESRS 2 MDR-M; S1-14] People safety Eni’s internal HSE regulatory system requires all entities with employees to adopt an HSE management system.

For all entities with more than 250 employees or carrying out industrial activities, in addition to developing a management system, certification in accordance with ISO 45001 and ISO 14001 standards is required.

Regarding Eni’s health management system, all employees and contractors are covered, also through specific internal procedures applied in compliance with the regulations in force in the Countries in which the Company operates.

In 2025, the Total Recordable Injury Rate (TRIR) improved compared to 2024, both for contractors and employees; for both categories, a significant reduction in the number of events was recorded (44 injuries in 2025 compared to 69 for contractors in 2024 and 34 compared to 42 in 2024 for employees).
In addition, during the year there were neither fatal events nor events resulting in permanent disability.
The Fatality Index was equal to zero for both employees and contractors.

The contractor/subcontractor has its own HSE management system to ensure that the personnel for whom it is responsible are qualified and fit for the job, and that the processes, equipment, materials and tools provided are suitable, properly maintained and fit for the purpose of the contract.
This approach requires the reporting of HSE performance.

Mode 2: The contractor/subcontractor or Technology Partner provides personnel, processes, equipment, materials and tools for contract execution under its own HSE management system, providing the necessary instructions and supervision and verifying the proper functioning of its system.
This approach requires interfaces or bridging arrangements with Eni’s management system and the reporting of HSE performance.

Eni is responsible for verifying the overall effectiveness of the contractor’s HSE management controls, including interfaces with subcontractors, and for ensuring compatibility between Eni’s and the contractor’s HSE management systems.

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3; S2 SBM-3] Workers in the value chain Within Eni’s classification of value chain workers most closely monitored, the following groups are primarily considered: (i) workers employed by Eni’s suppliers, particularly those working at Eni’s operational sites; and (ii) workers employed by Eni’s business partners112, including those involved in joint ventures.

In certain higher-risk geographical contexts, some workers may also be considered more vulnerable, such as migrant workers, workers operating in remote areas, or individuals belonging to minority groups, and therefore more exposed to potential risks of forced labour, modern slavery, or child labour113.
