214 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX Material impacts Although Eni’s activities contribute to increasing employment rate along its entire value chain, they may also be associated with negative impacts affecting stakeholders such as contractors, suppliers’ employees, and business partners.

The presence of joint ventures or other business relationships in certain Countries and contexts, together with the complexity of the Group’s activities, entails the involvement114 of a large number of suppliers and business partners.

These actors vary in nature and size and operate in Countries characterised by different socio economic and cultural contexts, as well as in activities and sectors that may be identified as having a higher risk of labour related human rights violations, including potential impacts in terms of forced labour and modern slavery.

Furthermore, the outsourcing of production related activities may generate negative impacts in relation to workers’ health, workplace discrimination and lack of fairness in treatment and opportunities, employment guarantees, adequacy of wages, non application of collective agreements, and obstacles to freedom of association and trade union membership affecting workers in the value chain.
Impacts related to sexual harassment in the workplace are more likely to occur in sectors with a significant male presence and in remote work locations.

According to the risk- based model adopted by Eni (see Human Rights for Eni), both industrial activities — such as maintenance, construction, assembly — and general services — such as cleaning, catering and security — have been classified as activities at high-risk of human rights violations.

The adoption of structured due diligence in managing supplier relationships is therefore essential to prevent and mitigate potential negative impacts ( Actions taken on material IROs – Safeguards in the procurement process).

No widespread or systemic negative impacts related to procurement activities or to business relationships maintained by Eni have been identified; therefore, where they occur, impacts are generally linked to specific individual events.
With regard to positive impacts related to the supply chain, please refer to the chapter Sustainable supply chain management.

Material risks and opportunities At Group level, no material risks or opportunities have been identified in relation to workers in the value chain (see Process and results of the double materiality assessment).
The only material aspect concerns the potential risk of loss of confidentiality of sensitive information linked to Cybersecurity, which is addressed in the dedicated Cybersecurity chapter.

ENGAGEMENT OF THE WORKERS IN THE VALUE CHAIN [S2 SBM-2; S2-2; S2-3] Engagement activities involving workers in the value chain take place primarily through the supplier, as the legal entity, and are embedded throughout all phases of interaction with Eni — from supplier qualification to sourcing and contract execution.

These activities, coordinated by the central procurement function, with the support of business procurement units and requesting units, include: (i) workshops and training on human rights and other social matters; (ii) workshops on health and safety-related topics (see Health & Safety); (iii) training activities on sustainability and the energy transition (see Sustainable supply chain management); (iv) training on anti-corruption matters (see Business Conduct); (v) training on responsible supply chain management.

The effectiveness of engagement activities on these topics is assessed through periodic evaluations of suppliers’ performance, conducted through audits and reviews, followed by monitoring the implementation of jointly agreed action plans to address any identified gaps.

For companies considered to be more exposed to potential negative impacts according to the risk-based model (see Actions taken on material IROs), onsite audits are carried out, including interviews with the workforce on human rights-related aspects and business conduct.

Whistleblowing mechanisms and remediation processes [S2-3] In line with its commitments and in compliance with international standards, Eni has established mechanisms for receiving complaints and reports, which are also accessible to suppliers’ workers.
These mechanisms may be used in cases of alleged human rights violations115 and can trigger a remediation process, including through collaboration with third parties.
These mechanisms are further described in the Human Rights for Eni chapter.
Eni also expects its suppliers to provide workers and communities with their own reporting and remedy mechanism, which should also be accessible on an anonymous basis.
ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; S2-4] Eni defines a set of control and mitigation measures aimed at managing the risk of potential human rights violations.

In order to ensure that actions are targeted in line with the material impacts identified, these measures are structured around several key areas: (i) safeguards in the procurement process; (ii) training and awareness-raising activities; (iii) health and safety of workers in the supply chain detailed in (114) For further details on how impacts related to workers in the value chain are taken into account in defining the Company’s strategy and business model, see the chapter Stakeholder Engagement.
(115) Any confirmed cases of human rights violations are addressed in the remediation actions undertaken are described.

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 215 the section Action taken on Material IROs in the Health & Safety chapter; (iv) the definition of a governance oversight model of the other business partners.

All actions undertaken are part of the broader support provided to suppliers in meeting ESG requirements, by offering tools to support their sustainable development journey and, more generally, the competitiveness of their businesses (for actions and related metrics, see the Sustainable supply chain management chapter).
The main actions under each area are outlined below.

Safeguards in the procurement process Eni’s commitment to engaging the entire production system in a sustainable development pathway translates into concrete solutions and a systemic strategy characterised by openness to the market, a collaborative approach, and a strong focus on people and innovation.

The focus on people means that particular attention to human rights and safety is not limited to direct contractual relationships, but also extends to the workforce of subcontractors and potential suppliers.

This approach is reflected in a procurement process that includes: (i) the adoption by the Procurement function of transparent, impartial, consistent and non-discriminatory conduct in supplier selection, bid evaluation and the verification of contractually required activities; and (ii) the assessment of aspects related to individuals working for suppliers through the application of a dedicated model.

This model is applied throughout all stages of the procurement process, from qualification to contract execution, and involves different safeguards and actions by all units engaged in relationships with third parties (central procurement function, procuring units and contract management units).

It enables suppliers to be subject to a process of continuous monitoring, aimed at periodically assessing the effectiveness of the control measures adopted and updating the evaluations relating to each supplier.
The model adopts a risk- based approach that allows suppliers to be analysed and classified according to their potential level of risk of generating negative impacts.

This assessment is based on: (i) the supplier’s Country risk, derived from data provider information (Maplecroft), in order to consider the likelihood of human rights violations; and (ii) the risk of the activities performed, assessed by considering vulnerabilities related to specific conditions, such as labour intensity, the level of training and skills required for tasks, and health, safety, and environmental risks.

Based on this risk mapping, the model provides for differentiated control measures inspired by international standards such as SA8000: the higher the potential risk of negative impacts associated with a supplier, the more detailed the assessment and the actions undertaken.

The model also includes the adoption of specific prevention and mitigation measures, as well as monitoring plans aimed at supporting suppliers in adopting and developing a culture of respect for human rights.
As part of human rights due diligence, annual expenditure is incurred (relating to the functions and personnel involved, as well as the costs of on-site audits carried out by third parties).
These costs are fully integrated into ordinary management and operational activities and form part of the broader structural and cross-functional costs incurred by Eni.
In order to obtain or maintain supplier status, all companies are required to sign the Supplier Code of Conduct.

When a contract is awarded, specific clauses are included to guarantee respect for human rights and, in higher-risk cases, a clause allowing Eni to carry out verification activities at the supplier’s premises.

Contractual mechanisms are also included to safeguard Eni’s interests, enabling the Company to suspend and/or terminate the contract in the event that the contractor fails to comply with the declarations, guarantees and contractual obligations relating to human rights116.

Regardless of the risk level associated with a supplier, due diligence checks are carried out to verify whether the supplier has been involved in cases of human rights violations, through the use of open sources.
Periodic qualification checks are also conducted, based on performance indicators, document reviews, on-site audits and dedicated questionnaires, in order to minimise the likelihood of violations.
During the tender phase, suppliers are required to meet and demonstrate minimum human rights requirements, particularly where the activities involved may present significant risks.

During contract execution, Eni evaluates and monitors suppliers and subcontractors through feedback mechanisms and documentary checks, with the aim of preventing impacts related to modern slavery and forced labour, child labour, wage discrimination, irregularities in social security contributions, and other issues that could negatively affect workers.
Where critical issues are identified, improvement plans focused on human rights are established, requiring the implementation of specific corrective actions.

Where minimum acceptability requirements are not met, suppliers may be prevented from participating in Eni tenders; in the most serious cases of non-compliance, the relationship with the supplier is terminated and the supplier is removed from Eni’s vendor list.

As part of a continuous improvement approach, the Company aims to make its model increasingly accessible and replicable by the suppliers, promoting stronger accountability among its direct business partners and encouraging them to systematically carry out due diligence on their own third parties, in order to safeguard human rights throughout the supply chain.
In line with this approach, during 2025 more than 1,000 human rights- related checks were carried out on contractors and subcontractors, including both document-based reviews and on-site inspections.
These activities also took into account reports received through the whistleblowing channel.
Where shortcomings were identified, suppliers had their participation in Eni tenders restricted and corrective action plans were agreed to ensure alignment with human rights standards.

A notable example of monitoring activities concerns a case identified at Eni Algeria, where in 2025 a delay in the payment of wages by a contractor (116) Eni has developed a set of standard human rights clauses to be included, on a risk-based approach, in the main types of contracts.
It also provides support to the business in their negotiation.

Eni intervened promptly to ensure that contractual obligations were fulfilled, introducing a number of measures including closer and more frequent monitoring of the contractor’s performance in coordination with the contract manager.
These actions also aimed at strengthening the supplier’s awareness and capabilities in this area.
The supplier was subsequently included in a watch list and subject to conditional engagement.
These measures enabled the outstanding wages to be paid.
As a precautionary step, Eni also launched a new tender process for the service, from which the contractor will be excluded due to the shortcomings identified.

The model for overseeing potential impacts within the procurement process enables such impacts to be identified from the qualification phase through to contract award and throughout contract execution and provides for improvement or remediation actions where actual impacts are identified.

Eni is committed to strengthening internal checks on subcontractors and on all entities with which it maintains business relationships, with particular attention to critical or high-risk contexts, adopting an increasingly rigorous approach.

This effort aims to enhance the Company’s ability to identify, prevent and mitigate risks, while reinforcing transparency and shared responsibility across the supply chain in the short and medium-term.

Training and awareness-raising activities Eni organises workshops, training and awareness-raising initiatives that give suppliers the opportunity to engage with experts on ESG topics, including issues related to respect for human rights across the supply chain.

The Company also promotes awareness of its human rights safeguards through internal training programmes and workshops for professionals involved in supplier management, including those working in foreign subsidiaries.
Within the platform, through an assessment process, companies receive also feedback on their positioning, together with insights and practical suggestions on actions to be undertaken for improvement.

Health and safety of workers in the value chain Eni requires its suppliers to identify and assess risks related to the health and safety of their workers, by implementing appropriate prevention and protection measures to avoid behaviours that could cause harm to people, assets or the environment.
Suppliers are expected to regularly update their operating procedures and use the best available technologies, in a perspective of continuous improvement.
Top management commitment to managing workers’ health and safety is also required, including ensuring adequate training and raising awareness on the adoption of safe working practices and behaviours.
These requirements are not limited to the qualification stage but are also requested and verified periodically throughout the commercial relationship.

When activities are carried out on Eni sites, suppliers are required to ensure cooperation with Eni and other suppliers, including through the proactive implementation of good operational practices, reporting of hazardous conditions or actions, and investigation and sharing of lessons learned from all incidents.
In addition, personnel working for supplier companies on Eni operational sites are involved in specific training and awareness-raising activities related to HSE topics.
For activities, metrics and measures aimed at protecting the health and safety of workers in the supply chain, please refer to the Health & Safety section.

Safeguard model for other business partners Eni also considers potential human rights impacts arising from the activities of companies in which it holds a minority interest or is not primarily involved in operational management (so-called ‘non-operated’ entities), as well as, more generally, from the activities of third parties.
This includes promoting measures to prevent, mitigate, and address such impacts.

Eni expects these entities to commit to and comply with the principles set out in Eni’s Code of Ethics and in the ECG Policy on Respect for Human Rights in Eni, including through the inclusion of specific compliance obligations in relevant contracts and the exercise of leverage activities (for example, through the role of Eni representatives in joint ventures).

Such contractual compliance provisions are defined according to a risk-based approach and typically require potential counterparties or partners to fulfil their obligations in accordance with key international human rights standards and guidelines, including the United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises, and the ILO Declaration on Fundamental Principles and Rights at Work.

Where necessary, these clauses may also require the counterparty to include commitments on human rights in contracts with subcontractors and/or sub-suppliers engaged in performing the activities under the contract with Eni.
These commitments must be equivalent to those required of the counterparty itself (‘contractual cascading’).
Human rights are also integrated into due diligence processes for specific types of transactions, such as M&A deals and joint ventures, always following a risk-based approach.
If these reviews identify human rights-related concerns, Eni considers the adoption of mitigation measures and the monitoring of their implementation.
• Promotion of continuous and transparent consultation to ensure that local communities are duly informed and that their expectations are taken into consideration in Eni’s activities.
• Respect for the rights of communities by recognizing and enhancing their culture, lifestyles, institutions, ties with the land of origin and development models.

ECG Policy on Respect for Human Rights in Eni • Respect for the rights of local communities, with particular reference to biodiversity, environmental protection (including so-called ‘culturally sensitive’ areas), the rights to ownership and use of land and natural resources (‘Land Grabbing’ is not tolerated), the right to water and the right to the enjoyment of the highest attainable standard of physical and mental health.

• Special attention to the rights of vulnerable groups, with a particular focus on national or ethnic minorities, religious and language minorities, people with disabilities, migrant workers and their families, children, women and girls.
• Respect for the rights of indigenous peoples.
• Involvement of communities through free, prior, and informed consultations, with particular attention to the presence of vulnerable groups.

• Commitment to avoiding the resettlement of communities and, where this cannot be avoided, to carrying out consultations to reach joint agreements, fair compensation and improvements to living conditions.

• Commitment to respecting human rights within security activities aimed at protecting its people and assets from any threat arising from third parties’ criminal conduct that could cause direct or indirect damage.

Internal Regulatory System • Model for supporting local development structured into various sub-processes: understanding the context, integrating sustainability and health into the business, identifying needs and expectations and developing initiatives, monitoring, evaluation, and reporting.
• Commitment and operational procedures for health impact assessments and for community health projects.
• Department responsible for local development aspects, engagement with local communities and stakeholders, and the assessment and management of social impacts.
TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; S3-5] The targets and commitments relating to local communities are grounded in the principles set out in the relevant Policies.

They are defined through a bottom up approach, bringing together individual initiatives and grouping them by specific indicators for each area of intervention, in line with the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals.
Monitoring is carried out internally on a quarterly basis, tracking the progress of initiatives through the Stakeholder Management System117.
Annual results are published in Eni’s sustainability reporting, including at the local level where available.
Both external and internal evaluations are carried out halfway through and at the end of each cycle.
Their purpose is to assess the efficiency and effectiveness of the initiatives, identify best practices and lessons learned, involving the key stakeholders in the process.

The targets listed below, grouped by Eni’s priority areas of intervention (access to energy, economic diversification, education and vocational training, access to water and sanitation services, health, and life on land) were defined through direct engagement with stakeholders.
For initiatives related to healthcare services, local health authorities were also involved in the process.

(b) The initiatives include support activities for community waste management, the rehabilitation of disposal sites, remediation actions aimed at restoring native vegetation, tree planting, biodiversity conservation, and awareness-raising campaigns on the risks associated with pollution from oil spills and on the importance of biodiversity protection.

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3; S3 SBM-3] Local communities of reference The communities potentially affected by material impacts are identified before any business activity begins in which Eni acts as operator.
The same approach applies to certain joint ventures where Eni plays a significant role in managing local stakeholders.
This assessment considers both communities located within Eni’s operational areas and those designated by national governments, for example in offshore development zones.
These communities may also be identified outside the defined area of influence118, taking into account agreements with the host Country and the priorities set out in National Development Plans.

Particular attention is given to vulnerable groups — including children, women, national and ethnic minorities, migrant workers and indigenous peoples — for whom specific assessments are carried out through inclusive consultation processes.

Material impacts Eni promotes the development of local communities, with particular attention to the local entrepreneurship and to community health, and is committed to creating long-term value by transferring its know-how and expertise to local partners (an approach known as ‘Dual Flag’ model).

This is also achieved by developing local supply chains to enhance the competitiveness of local enterprises, engaging local workers and transferring skills, knowledge and development programmes that support economic growth and diversification.
At the same time, hydrocarbon exploration and production activities can, by their nature, generate potential negative impacts on the human rights of local communities.
(118) Scope of analysis for impact assessment studies carried out during the preliminary phases of business activities.

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 219 Material risks and opportunities Eni’s activities entail a number of operational risks that may generate potential impacts not only on its workforce and infrastructure, but also on local communities, both directly and indirectly.

Material risks119 include major process and asset integrity incidents, such as fires or explosions, including blowouts, spills, releases of hazardous substances and other industrial events, as well as incidents not strictly related to process activities (for example transportation services, fuel stations or gas distribution networks), all of which may negatively affect communities and ultimately have reputational and economic repercussions for Eni.
Also relevant for business operations is the risk of a potential negative perception of Eni among local stakeholders.

Continuous dialogue and engagement with local stakeholders, together with collaboration with civil society organizations and public institutions, enable Eni to appropriately manage these risks and to seize opportunities to access new business activities in synergy with the territory.

COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT [S3 SBM-2; S3-2; S3-3] When operating in diverse socio economic contexts, it is essential to understand stakeholders’ expectations and share key decisions in order to build relationships based on mutual trust, identify actual, potential or perceived impacts, and determine the most effective forms of engagement.

Understanding the broader context, including cultural aspects, makes it possible to design appropriate access channels and adopt the most suitable approaches for dialogue, information sharing and the management of any conflicts.
Eni and its subsidiaries carry out engagement with local communities120, including indigenous peoples and vulnerable groups, through free, prior and informed consultations.
Responsibility for these consultations lies with the local Managing Director, supported by the Sustainability unit at both local and central level.
In some contexts, dedicated liaison figures are also appointed to maintain ongoing relationships, including through periodic consultations throughout the various phases of business activities.

In the event of economic or physical resettlement, specific meetings are held to provide transparent and comprehensive information to the communities concerned, with particular attention to people in vulnerable conditions.

For every new business development initiative, engagement takes place through public hearings open to local communities (where not prohibited by national regulations), ensuring the active participation of authorities (including indigenous authorities) and local representatives.
This process ensures that communities are properly informed about business developments and able to provide feedback.
Engagement does not end with the permitting phase but continues throughout the entire project lifecycle, including construction and operations.

Depending on the operating context, consultations are carried out through information sessions, focus groups, sharing of reports and updates, with periodic communication on project progress and awareness campaigns on health-related issues.
Where relevant, Eni also identifies women’s associations active in the areas where it operates, involving them in consultations or proposing cooperation within project activities.

Whistleblowing mechanisms and remediation processes – Grievance Mechanism In line with its commitments and in accordance with international standards, Eni has established mechanisms for receiving complaints and whistleblowing reports that can be used in cases of alleged human rights violations121, thereby activating an appropriate remedy process.
These mechanisms are further detailed in the chapter Human Rights for Eni.
Among the various channels available, Eni has defined and applies guiding principles for the management of Grievance Mechanisms, for which operational responsibility lies with all subsidiaries.
These entities analyse the grievances and agree the resolution with the complainants, whether individuals or communities.

Any request or complaint received is handled and monitored until closure through agreements with the parties concerned and a response is provided even when the issue is not directly related to Eni’s activities.

Grievances may be submitted through online channels (including a dedicated e mail address and the institutional websites of subsidiaries), or physically at administrative/operational offices or via collection boxes located in areas affected by the project.
Eni prohibits and acts to prevent any form of retaliation against workers and other stakeholders who raise concerns.

As set out in the ECG Policy on Respect for Human Rights in Eni, Eni does not tolerate or encourage threats, intimidation, retaliations or attacks (whether physical or legal) against human rights defenders and other Stakeholders and actively seeks dialogue and engagement with them.
All grievances received, assessed and managed by subsidiaries are tracked in the Stakeholder Management System, the tool used to map relationships with stakeholders.
Grievances are classified by topic and relevance, and the rate of resolution is monitored.

The system also tracks timeliness of management, trends in recurring issues and potential escalation towards disputes, as well as any critical stakeholder concerns that may require adjustments in the engagement strategy.

220 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX safeguarded through measures designed to protect the complainant and the identity of the reported individuals, without prejudice to any party.

To ensure the mechanism is effective and robust, the access modalities by complainants are assessed in each context, including any language-related implications and the possible need for support when submitting a grievance.
The review also considers how the mechanism is communicated and whether stakeholders receive clear, adequate information on how it works.

Furthermore, once the proposed resolution has been approved, Eni ensures that it is communicated to and discussed with the complainant, inviting observations or alternative solutions and ensuring that all steps are duly recorded and archived.
If the complainant is not satisfied, Eni evaluates the reasons and, where necessary, activates the examination and response process, including the potential involvement of third parties.
In relevant Countries, Eni conducts quarterly reviews on the status of grievances, monitoring specific indicators.

To strengthen confidence in the mechanism, the Eni assesses: (i) the most effective forms of communication; (ii) how to enhance stakeholders’ understanding of how to access and use the mechanism; (iii) the need for support when submitting a grievance, including through regular dialogue with local communities; (iv) whether and how the results of these indicators should be made accessible to communities.

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; S3-4] Starting from the analysis of the local socio-economic context, including the global Multidimensional Poverty Index122, Eni adopts tools and methodologies to identify and manage potential impacts, both negative and positive, direct and indirect, including those related to human rights.

This assessment begins in the earliest stages of project design, with the objective of preventing, mitigating or remedying negative impacts, and of promoting the development of local communities in the case of positive impacts.

Below are the actions implemented by Eni based on the material impacts, risks and opportunities identified, grouped as follows: (i) environmental, social and health impact assessment (ESHIA) studies; (ii) Eni’s management system for human rights; (iii) management of any resettlement and related compensation measures; (iv) local development projects; (v) security activities.

Environmental Social and Health Impact Assessment In addition to meeting the mandatory requirements for environmental authorization in the Countries where it operates, Eni conducts Environmental, Social and Health Impact Assessments (ESHIA)123 and Health Impact Assessments (HIA)124.

These assessments ensure compliance with recognized international standards125 and secure stakeholder engagement in order to protect their interests, identify critical issues, assess potential impacts and implement appropriate mitigation measures.

In 2025, with the objective of evaluating potential health impacts on the communities involved, Eni completed 11 studies: 7 integrated into ESHIA processes in Oman, Indonesia and Cyprus, and 4 specific health studies, of which 3 in Italy and 1 in Côte d’Ivoire.
For each ESHIA, an Environmental and Social Management Plan is prepared.
The Plan incorporates elements related to the respect for human rights and sets out the measures to mitigate such impacts throughout the project lifecycle.
It is shared with the relevant authorities to support progress monitoring.

At the end of the construction phase, compliance with project documentation, including environmental and social aspects, is verified and any deviations lead to the identification of corrective actions.
Health impact assessments are either integrated into the ESHIA or conducted separately through HIA/VIS.
If potential health impacts resulting from operational activities are identified, the outcome is communicated to the relevant stakeholders in accordance with applicable local legislation.
A Mitigation and Monitoring Plan is then developed to ensure the proper management of any significant impacts and to provide for periodic monitoring of progress.
In some Countries, such as Australia and Kenya, Eni operates in areas inhabited by indigenous peoples or tribal groups.

In these contexts, Eni has adopted specific policies and procedures to protect their rights, tangible and intangible cultural heritage, traditions, lifestyles, institutions, ties to ancestral lands and development models, in line with international standards.
These measures also promote their free, prior and informed consultation.

(122) The Global Multidimensional Poverty Index, developed in 2010 by the Human Development Report Office of UNDP, is an international measure of acute poverty, covering over 100 developing Countries and integrating traditional monetary poverty measures with three other fundamental dimensions: health, education, and living standards.

(123) Through ESHIA studies, conducted before the start of business activities, the following are defined: the Area of Influence, i.e., the area within which project activities may potentially affect resources/receptors and where potential impacts (both direct and indirect) must be assessed, and the Study Area, which must be analysed to properly understand and characterize the reference scenario.
These studies take into account the specific characteristics of each business activity.
As part of the process, communities living or working near the operations, as well as those located within the Areas of Influence, are mapped and identified.
(124) The Health Impact Assessment (HIA) is a decision-support tool used to evaluate the potential health effects of industrial projects on the affected populations.
It is applied, where relevant, to national initiatives, in line with the reference methodological framework and in integration with environmental studies.

The analysis is based on available epidemiological evidence and is conducted with reference to the area affected by the project activities and the potentially involved communities, using available institutional and local data and information.
(125) Examples of references include UNGPs, OECD Guidelines, IFC Performance Standards, and methodologies defined by IPIECA.

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 221 Eni’s management system for human rights Eni’s commitment to preventing potential negative impact on human rights arising from industrial projects is implemented through a structured due-diligence approach (see Human Rights for Eni) and the application of a risk-based model.

This model draws on contextual elements, such as risk indices provided by Verisk Maplecroft, and on project-specific characteristics, in order to classify business activities according to their potential human rights risk and to identify the appropriate management measures.

Projects with higher risk levels undergo dedicated Human Rights Impact Assessments (HRIA) to identify and evaluate potential impacts, including through engagement with right holders, and to develop recommendations that are then translated into prevention and management measures within Action Plans.

During the year, implementation and monitoring activities continued for the Action Plans (available on Eni’s website) associated with HRIA or Human Rights Risk Analyses (HRRA) conducted in previous years.

Moreover, in 2025 Eni advanced two projects launched in 2024 following the update of the Salient Issues: one focused on biomass trading activities and one on agri-feedstock initiatives for the production of vegetable oils intended for biofuel manufacturing.

Although both business areas offer significant opportunities for agricultural development, given their reliance on the cultivation of biomass, they are also exposed to potential negative impacts, particularly regarding the working conditions of farmers in the supply chain and the implications for communities related to land use.
With regard to biomass trading, in 2025 Eni implemented an Action Plan aimed at strengthening certain responsible-sourcing safeguards126.

For agri-feedstock initiatives, a dedicated framework was established to govern this new area of activity through a set of cross-cutting measures — such as conducting specific impact assessments covering the entire supply chain, providing training and awareness-raising initiatives for Eni’s counterparties and supply-chain actors, and reinforcing the criteria for screening and selecting commercial counterparties.
The framework is designed to be applied in all Countries where these projects are launched.
Throughout the year, Eni supported its subsidiaries in the initial implementation of the framework, particularly in Kenya, the Republic of the Congo, Angola and Mozambique.

This first application will offer valuable insights into which aspects of the framework may need to be refined or strengthened depending on contextual factors and the specificities of the Countries involved.
Management of any resettlement and related compensation procedures The development of projects for the use of natural resources may require acquiring and/or using land from local communities.

For all individuals who live or carry out activities within Eni’s operational areas, the adoption of fair, transparent and sustainable compensation measures is ensured (applying the international standard on involuntary resettlement)127, with the aim of minimising any loss of assets that could compromise income sources or livelihoods, even when local legislation does not provide adequate remedies for the impacted communities (Project Affected People).
In 2025, no cases of economic or physical displacement occurred128.
It is also specified that each action plan includes a monitoring plan, followed by an intermediate and a final evaluation to measure the effectiveness of the actions.
It should also be noted that each action plan includes a monitoring plan, followed by an interim and a final evaluation to measure the effectiveness of the actions.

These collaborations help identify the most relevant interventions for addressing community needs and supporting their development.

Specific tools and actions are used to identify potential impacts, enhancing positive ones and preventing negative ones, supported by dedicated training for local sustainability teams.

Security activities [ENTITY-SPECIFIC] Eni manages its security operations in compliance with international principles, including those set out by the Voluntary Principles on Security and Human Rights promoted by the Voluntary Principles Initiative132 (VPI), and proactively collaborates with its Business Partners to ensure that such activities, carried out in synergy with and/or in the interest of Eni, are managed in full respect of human rights and fundamental freedoms of individuals.

Eni has been a ‘full member’ of the VPI since 2022 and, in 2025, within the framework of human rights, carried out a series of actions aimed both at confirming its commitment and at increasing awareness and sensitivity in managing potential impacts on the communities where it is the operator.
Since 2009, Eni has promoted a training programme for public and private security personnel in host Countries to disseminate corporate best practices in line with international principles.

In 2025, the ‘Security & Human Rights’ Workshop was held in Congo, in Pointe Noire, with the participation of Eni’s Head of Global Security, the Italian Ambassador to Congo and the Congolese Minister of Justice.

The initiative was aimed at raising awareness among Congolese public and private Security Forces not only on security and human rights issues, but also on violence against women, their rights and the behaviours expected of Security Forces.
The workshop was a valuable opportunity for discussion among local authorities, Eni personnel, Congolese institutional security representatives, and the trainer.

During 2025, commitment also continued in the implementation of the ‘In-Country Workshops on Security & Human Rights’ project, dedicated to the delivery, by subsidiary Security Managers, of training workshops on security and human rights to local private security forces.
These workshops were held in Ghana, Indonesia, Algeria, Egypt, Iraq, Kenya, and Libya.
In 2025, the new Train the Trainers Project was also implemented, involving the Group’s subsidiary in Mozambique (Eni Rovuma Basin).

The initiative aims to train public security forces on Security & Human Rights, through the involvement of an external consultant, so that they in turn acquire the necessary know-how to train other security forces in this area.
This project represented the first application of a Training Module that can be replicated in other Eni operations worldwide.
The initiative was carried out in collaboration with the Ministry of Justice and the National Human Rights Authority of Mozambique.
The training lasted five days and involved 21 trainer candidates (including six women) from the Ministries of Justice, Interior and Defence, Penitentiary Police, and Immigration Police.
The number of Countries in which armed guards are deployed to protect sites is 15.

224 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX CUSTOMERS AND CONSUMERS [ESRS S4] This chapter focuses on B2C customers of Plenitude - Benefit Corporation (Società Benefit), with a particular focus on the 10 million retail customers to whom Eni supplies energy, energy efficiency solutions and electric mobility services.
Attention is focused on this customer segment due to the presence of an ongoing contractual relationship, which differentiates these customers from those of Eni’s other business lines.

POLICIES [ESRS 2 MDR-P; S4-1] POLICY KEY CONTENTS: Code of Ethics • Principle of transparent management of relationships with customers and consumers, inspired by best practices and the principle of professional loyalty to customers and consumers.
• Building commercial relationships focused on customer needs, enabling customers to choose freely and consciously.

ECG Policy on Respect for • Engagement of third parties in the prevention and mitigation of any adverse human rights impacts that their operations, Human Rights in Eni products or services might cause or contribute to causing.

ECG Policy on Privacy and data protection • Definition of roles and responsibilities for the collection, use, retention and deletion of data, including appropriate technical organizational measures for data protection.
• Definition of operational procedures for the processing of personal data in compliance with applicable regulations, ensuring transparency, security and fairness in data processing activities.

• Management of data subjects’ requests, procedures for reporting of any violation and control and periodic update activities for the continuous and systematic protection of the personal information processed.

ECG Policy on Consumer Protection & Green Claims • Compliance with rules and principles on consumer protection and on accurate environmental and sustainability communication (Green Claims and Sustainability Claims).
• Tools aimed at preventing the risk of breaches, including ‘inadvertent’ breaches, of Consumer Protection Regulation.

• Dissemination of a compliance culture in the area of consumer protection, contributing to fostering the identification and reporting by Eni people to identify and report any actions/behaviours that may constitute violations, in line with the company’s Internal Regulatory System.

Internal Regulatory System • Commitment to comply with all regulations aimed at safeguarding fair competition and consumers’ right to receive clear, accurate and comprehensive information on the products and services offered.

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; S4-5] The defined targets, in line with the applicable policies, play a central role in the strategic choices and the commitments to build customer-centric commercial relationships, enabling customers to make free and informed choices, also through correct commercial communication.

To this end, Plenitude has established a dedicated organisational function responsible for verifying compliance with consumer protection regulations across all its business initiatives and customer communications, with the aim of avoiding unfair commercial practices and providing clear, complete, accurate and non-misleading information.

Customer service performance is monitored on a weekly basis through business review processes, using a set of metrics (both reported in the Sustainability Statement and other indicators monitored internally) tracking alignment with the defined targets.

Meetings are also organised with national and local representatives of Consumer Associations on an annual basis to present corporate strategies and, periodically, for in-depth discussions on specific topics of interest to end consumers.
Scope: e-mobility business area.
Relative target defined on the basis of the introduction of new technologies and a customer service remuneration model focused on service quality.

Absolute target defined on the basis of the plan for the progressive digitalisation of contract subscriptions through physical sales channels and the implementation of new digital acquisition channels.
Scope: new electricity and gas supply contracts signed by B2C customers.
(a) The installation plan for charging points has been updated compared to the disclosures set out in the 2024 Sustainability Statement, in response to market trends.
(b) The reference value is not reported as it is market-sensitive and not comparable across companies due to the use of different methodologies.

(c) Performance, which has steadily improved over the past four years, reached 86% in 2025 (vs. a 90% target), mainly due to the different levels of digital maturity across the European Countries considered and to the different mix of customer acquisition channels.

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3; S4 SBM-3] Material impacts Eni broadens its operational scope to reach end markets by offering gas, electricity and energy solutions to retail and business customers, as well as providing electric mobility services.
The main material customer-related impacts primarily concern Plenitude, due to the existence of an ongoing contractual relationship.
Eni is committed to enhancing customer satisfaction while simultaneously promoting a culture of responsible energy use, by offering appropriate and quality products and services.
This commitment is supported by effective customer dialogue channels and by the adoption of transparent and fair commercial practices.

At the same time, potential negative impacts may arise from accidental events, such as unclear advertising campaigns or misleading or aggressive commercial practices, which could mislead customers or unduly influence their purchasing decisions.

Material risks and opportunities No material risks have been identified at Group level in relation to consumers (see Process and results of the double materiality assessment), except for the potential loss of confidentiality of sensitive information related to cybersecurity risk, which is addressed in the Cybersecurity section.
In line with the identified target, the development of electric vehicle charging points134 represents a business opportunity for the expansion of services supporting electric mobility.

CUSTOMER ENGAGEMENT [S4 SBM-2; S4-2; S4-3] Within Plenitude, there is a dedicated team responsible for market analysis and customer listening135, aimed at identifying needs and areas for improvement, consumption trends, socio-economic developments and main concerns.

Each year, a wide range of qualitative and quantitative market research initiatives is carried out through multiple channels, including online, telephone and in-person surveys, with the support of specialised research institutes and companies operating in Italy and abroad, in line with recognised industry quality standards.
In 2025, more than 120 research projects were conducted, involving over 190,000 current and potential customers.
In addition, an initiative aimed at listening to calls made to the toll-free customer service number continued throughout the year.
Customer satisfaction is monitored on a continuous basis and is measured as the percentage of customers assigning an overall satisfaction score above 7 out of 10 to Plenitude as an energy supplier.

To evaluate the effectiveness of customer engagement channels, additional KPIs are monitored, including the Net Promoter Score (NPS), which measures the share of customers willing to recommend Plenitude and the complaint rate.
Ongoing dialogue with Consumer Associations is also actively promoted to enhance customer satisfaction and service quality.
(134) This opportunity is further analysed in the (135) For further details on expectations and stakeholder engagement, please refer to the chapter Climate Change section.

226 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX This dialogue is supported by dedicated channels, such as the Protocollo di Conciliazione Paritetica (Joint Conciliation Protocol), an out-of-court dispute resolution mechanism between the Company and customers, in line with Alternative Dispute Resolution procedure.
Consumer Associations are also enabled to report potential service shortcomings and product malfunctions on behalf of customers through a dedicated telephone service and a dedicated web area.

Plenitude further engages with relevant Authorities and institutions at both national and local level, through consultations and hearings, including on matters related to the protection of vulnerable customers (for further details see Actions).

Operational responsibility for ensuring effective customer engagement and for integrating its outcomes into the Company’s approach lies with the Director of Italian retail market, in collaboration with the support functions.
Reporting mechanisms and remediation processess Plenitude has implemented consumer protection mechanisms that also extend to intermediaries carrying out commercial activities on its behalf.

All sales operators are subject to a preliminary assessment aimed at verifying their financial soundness, sector-specific experience, the adequacy of their commercial structure and their technical and commercial training requirements.

Following a positive outcome of this assessment, personnel of the operators authorised to operate on behalf of Plenitude are required to complete a mandatory training programme, designed to ensure compliance with the Company’s processes and quality standards.
Monitoring of the activities carried out by commercial partners is also envisaged, based on continuous performance analysis and qualitative KPIs.
These results are reviewed by management in periodic sessions, focused on customer protection, ensuring proactive and systematic oversight of the entire process.
In Italy, Plenitude manages customer reports136 in compliance with the regulations of the Authority for Energy, Networks and Environment (ARERA).
These reports are analysed both qualitatively and quantitatively to understand customer issues and to activate corrective actions.
On a quarterly basis, the multidisciplinary customer protection Committee reviews indicators related to the quality of partners’ commercial performance and defines the related action plans.

Specific sanctioning procedures apply to complaints concerning commercial partners, including penalties for unsolicited activations and preliminary investigations into contractual breaches under the mandate, which are assessed by the Penalties Committee.

Customers may submit reports through multiple channels, including remote channels (website, customer area/app, chat, call centre, e-mail and postal service), a dedicated assistance service in Italian Sign Language, as well as direct and indirect physical channels across the territory.
Information on available contact channels is provided on eniplenitude.com, on bills and within commercial and contractual documentation.
Regardless of the channel used, Plenitude ensures the receipt, analysis and handling of reports, including anonymous submissions, while guaranteeing the highest level of confidentiality.

Plenitude also trains and informs its service providers, including customer care operators and call centres, by sharing internal procedures and delivering training sessions supported by quality-monitoring surveys.

To ensure alignment with Plenitude’s standards and expectations, controls are carried out on call handling and customer management, including sample call listening and the sharing of structured feedback with partners.
Complaints and information requests are monitored daily through a dedicated dashboard and managed by a combination of internal resources and specialised external providers.
Control systems are in place to verify the quality of practices and to identify training gaps and opportunities for improvement.

The Customer Feedback programme relies on a dedicated platform to deliver surveys, measure customer satisfaction KPIs and interact with customers, integrating their feedback into the Company’s systems.
The effectiveness of these tools is monitored in line with the commercial quality standards set by ARERA.
In relation to information requests and complaints, response times, underlying reasons and any recurrence are analysed to identify root causes and define improvement actions.

Finally, Plenitude monitors both the percentage of reports resolved at first contact (First Call Resolution) and the share of operations carried out autonomously by customers out of the total number of requested operations.
ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; S4-4] Plenitude provides for the implementation of several actions to remedy potential negative impacts on customers and consumers.

The actions implemented on the basis of the material impacts, risks and opportunities identified are outlined below and are grouped as follows: (i) actions for customer management; (ii) customer protection and fraud management; (iii) initiatives for vulnerable customers; (iv) customer engagement in the energy transition.

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 227 Actions for customer management To manage and oversee material customer-related impacts, Plenitude relies on dedicated teams with specific expertise within both the commercial functions (e.g.
Customer Operations, Value Stream Customer Experience Management) and the legal functions (e.g.
Data Protection, Corporate Liability Compliance and Ethics Code Values, Consumer & Brand Identity Protection).

Dedicated budgets are defined to support these activities and are fully integrated into day-to-day business management and operations, covering customer contact services, back-office activities, Customer Relationship Management (CRM), billing and metering processes, and credit management.
Future financial resources are planned and allocated in line with the evolution of the customer base and with the services required to support business development.

In relation to actions aimed at strengthening positive impacts on customers, Plenitude continues its commitment to the ongoing updating of the CRM system to improve the overall customer and user experience by simplifying activities for both operators and customers, as well as expanding customer oriented functionalities, while ensuring multichannel availability.
In addition, Plenitude has adopted internal procedures to mitigate the effects of any service disruptions attributable to the Company that may occur in the management of individual services (e.g.
contractual operations such as switching, activations, terminations and product changes) or in the supply service (e.g.
billing regularity137, payment recording, etc.
), in addition to the compensation already provided for under sector regulation.
Such procedures provide, for example, for compensation measures proportionate to the type and duration of the service disruption, as well as rebates or refunds of costs incurred by customers.
Each case is assessed on an individual basis and, where appropriate, addressed directly with the customer to identify a mutually agreed solution, with the aim of preventing the issue from persisting.

Customer protection and fraud management With regard to actions related to negative impacts, Plenitude has entered into the ‘unsolicited activations’ protocol with the consumer associations affiliated with the National Council of Consumers and Users, with the aim of strengthening consumer protection measures and, more broadly, addressing conduct attributable to unfair commercial practices.
Moreover, a joint Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) mechanism is also in place, providing a rapid, simple and out-of-court solution to disputes between consumers and companies.
Plenitude is committed to protecting its customers against potential unfair commercial practices by third parties, such as unauthorised activations.
To ensure continuous oversight of service quality, the trend in the activation of supply contracts, as well as other products (e.g.
boilers, air conditioning systems) and services (e.g.
insurance policies) is monitored, with particular focus on cases where activations do not occur.
The trend in the activation of supply point contracts is reported, and any issues that may arise after the customer signs the contract preventing its effective activation are closely monitored.

In the area of data protection, Plenitude adopts an integrated and interdisciplinary approach to the management of personal data and confidential information, ensuring that all processing activities are carried out in full compliance with the principles of lawfulness, fairness, transparency and data minimisation set out in Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR).

The Company promotes a widespread culture of privacy, supported by specific training and awareness-raising initiatives for personnel, as well as by the adoption of procedures and tools aimed at strengthening data security and protection throughout the entire information lifecycle.

This commitment is reflected in the continuous monitoring of processes and the regular updating of technical and organisational measures, in line with regulatory and technological developments in the sector.
Plenitude has also appointed a Data Protection Officer, who performs this role also for the subsidiaries.

Finally, in relation to fraud prevention, Plenitude has implemented a range of initiatives to support customers affected by attempted scams, by providing them with specific tools to protect themselves and to verify the identity of individuals who contact them.

These include informational alerts on fraud attempts, a dedicated toll-free number to handle reports on suspicious calls, as well as a verification service allowing customers to check whether the phone number from which they are contacted is attributable to a Plenitude operator.

This service, active since 2020, received 1,150 reports in 2025, all relating to phone numbers not registered in the Single Register of Call Centre Operators and therefore in breach of the applicable legal framework and potentially fraudulent.

(137) During 2025, Eni Plenitude France received a fine of € 275,000 from the Directorate-General for Competition, Consumer Affairs and Fraud Control (DGCCRF) for billing errors affecting more than 77,000 customers between 2022 and 2023.
During the investigation, in order to protect its customers, Plenitude voluntarily adopted compensatory measures in favour of the customers involved.
Following the agreement reached with the DGCCRF, the Company accepted payment of the fine without contesting the decision.

228 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX Initiatives for vulnerable customers138 Following the end of the regulated gas market in Italy in December 2023, Plenitude also confirmed for 2025 a range of offers with characteristics and price levels similar to those of the former regulated regime.

Accordingly, these offers continued to be available not only to vulnerable customers, as defined by ARERA, but also to all other customers previously served under the regulated market who had not yet opted for an offer in the free market.
Plenitude has also implemented specific measures to protect blind and visually impaired customers, hearing-impaired customers and financially vulnerable customers.
All app functionalities have been made fully accessible to blind and visually impaired users.

For hearing-impaired customers, in addition to chat and other digital contact channels, the TELLIS customer service has been available since 2022, enabling communication via Italian Sign Language through qualified remote interpreters.
Furthermore, Plenitude recognises young people among financially vulnerable customers.

The Company is among the first 50 partner organisations of the National Youth Card, an initiative promoted by the Italian Department for Youth Policies and Universal Civil Service, aimed at European young people aged between 18 and 35, residing in Italy.
The initiative provides benefits on the supply of gas and electricity from renewable sources covered by Guarantees of Origin, as well as a promotional offer on boilers and air conditioning systems.

Customer engagement in the energy transition With regard to activities related to efficient energy usage, Plenitude is committed to guiding customers towards energy awareness by providing personalised advice based on their energy profile, through a dedicated web area and app reserved to customers.

To this end, also in 2025, Plenitude made available to its customers a tool enabling them to estimate the electricity production of a residential photovoltaic system, ensuring, at the offer evaluation stage, the possibility to estimate potential savings based on customers’ consumption habits.

The ‘Plenitude Insieme’ loyalty programme, in addition to building a lasting and valuable relationship with customers, offers initiatives aimed at increasing awareness and knowledge of energy efficiency.
In 2025, the participation rate of registered users remained high (over 80%), confirming the continued engagement of customers in the energy transition journey.

In 2025, in fact, 83% of enrolled customers interacted with the programme at least once, and more than 390,000 people deepened their knowledge of energy efficiency (an increase of 95% compared to 2024).

In 2025, Plenitude launched the ‘Adopt a Panel’ service, enabling residential customers to virtually consume solar energy produced in real time by photovoltaic panels located at Plenitude-owned plants, without the need to install a domestic system.
The service allows customers to benefit from a 100% discount on electricity charges for the energy produced by the panel and consumed by the customer at the same time.
It also enables customers to monitor energy production and receive energy efficiency advice through the Plenitude app or their personal web area.

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T] Eni has defined specific commitments on business conduct matters, shared with its stakeholders, relating to the maintenance of ISO 37001:2016 and ISO 37301:2021 certifications, the continuous improvement of its Anti-Corruption Compliance Programme and training on the Programme for personnel exposed to medium and high levels of risk.
Corruption, by its very nature, represents an obstacle to sustainable development; it can distort competition and undermine trust in the economic system and in institutions.
The economic repercussions include financial losses, reduced business competitiveness and lower investment levels.
Moreover, corruption can erode economic efficiency and the fair allocation of resources, leading to slower economic development.

The occurrence of these events, also considering their significant reputational impact, may generate adverse consequences not only for employees but also for various categories of stakeholders who maintain economic, contractual and institutional relationships with the Company.

In doing so, the Company reaffirms the importance of conducting its business with loyalty, fairness, transparency, honesty and integrity and in compliance with applicable laws, regulations and other binding requirements, as well as with international standards and guidelines, both national and foreign, to which it is subject.

INSTITUTIONAL ENGAGEMENT ACTIVITIES As part of its partnerships and advocacy initiatives, Eni engages in dialogue with policymakers and national, European and international institutions, as well as with advocacy organisations, industry associations and business federations, highlighting its commitment to the energy transition in both its traditional activities and its new businesses.

In this context, Eni contributes its experience as an international energy company to the development of policies and regulations aimed at supporting the Net Zero transition, taking into account the social, economic and environmental dimensions of the contexts in which it operates.
Material risks and opportunities No material risks or opportunities have been identified at Group level (see Process and results of the double materiality assessment).

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; G1-1; G1-3; G1-4] Business Conduct, Corporate Culture and Corruption Prevention Eni strongly believes in promoting, at all levels of the organisation, a culture grounded in legality, compliance with applicable rules, integrity values and behavioural and control principles adopted by the Company.

The Code of Ethics reaffirms integrity and transparency among the values that characterise the commitment of Eni people and all third parties working with the Company (see Code of Ethics and Policies chapter).

In this context, to prevent the potential material impacts identified, Eni develops appropriate ethical programmes and safeguards and adopts suitable measures to meet the expectations of different stakeholders.

The actions implemented focus on: (i) the Anti‑Corruption Compliance Programme; (ii) training and communication activities; (iii) anti‑corruption initiatives across Eni’s value chain; (iv) the role of the Internal Audit function.

THE ANTI-CORRUPTION COMPLIANCE PROGRAMME Since 2009, Eni has adopted an Anti-Corruption Compliance Programme: a structured and continuously updated system of rules, controls and organisational safeguards designed to actively prevent corruption and money-laundering offences.
The Programme has been developed in accordance with national and international regulations and is aligned with applicable best practices and guidance.

From a regulatory perspective, the Anti-Corruption Compliance Programme is based on the ECG Policy on Anti-Corruption, supplemented by a set of detailed regulatory instruments made available to Eni people, which govern activities at risk and define the relevant controls.
These instruments govern activities exposed to corruption risk and define the related control measures.
Eni requires its subsidiaries, both in Italy and abroad, to formally adopt these instruments through a resolution of their Board of Directors (or equivalent governing body).

For companies in which Eni holds a non-controlling interest, the Company encourages compliance with the standards it has defined through the adoption and maintenance of an internal control system consistent with applicable legal requirements.

Eni has also established a centralized organizational function that provides specialized support on anti-corruption and anti-money laundering matters to the Company and its subsidiaries, with particular reference to the assessment of the reliability of higher-risk third parties (so-called ‘anti-corruption and anti-money laundering due diligence’), the management of potential issues or red flags, and the definition of related mitigation measures.
These include the formulation of contractual compliance safeguards and, for individually assessed higher-risk cases, requesting counterparties to adopt an anti-corruption compliance program.

In 2025, neither the Company nor members of senior management were involved in criminal proceedings resulting in final convictions for violations of anti-corruption regulations (for further details on Group litigation, see Note 28 ‘Guarantees, commitments and risks’ of the consolidated financial statements).
and their relative exposure to corruption-risk activities, as well as the Country risk and the specific risk of the Company.

Finally, compliance risk assessment activities and Compliance Monitoring interventions are planned according to a risk-based approach.

During 2025, Compliance Monitoring activities focused on the risk areas of ‘Joint Ventures’, ‘Sponsorships and Non-Profit Initiatives’, and ‘Transactions for the acquisition, disposal and management of exploratory mineral rights’; the outcomes of the analysis confirmed the expected level of risk, as well as the overall adequacy and effectiveness of Eni’s anti-corruption compliance model.
As in 2024, in 2025 the number of substantiated corruption cases140 relating to Eni SpA was 0 and, consequently, no dismissals related to such cases occurred.

For information on ongoing proceedings and on the total number of significant cases of non-compliance with laws and regulations (including any anti-competitive behaviour and violations of antitrust and monopoly regulations), reference should be made to the Legal proceedings section.
TRAINING AND COMMUNICATION ACTIVITIES [TAXO; G1 GOV-1] Anti-corruption awareness-raising and training initiatives are identified based on the needs defined for the different target groups.

In particular, the Management and control bodies of Eni SpA receive a semi-annual report, which forms an integral part of the Integrated Compliance Report, focusing on the key activities carried out under the Anti-Corruption Compliance Program and on the planning of future activities141.

Members of the Board of Directors and the Board of Statutory Auditors, during meetings of the Board Committees, receive in-depth sessions on strategic topics such as sustainability, human rights and the fight against corruption.

Eni personnel are also required to be informed about applicable laws, the principles set out in the Code of Ethics, and other relevant internal regulations, in order to understand the different risks, the potential consequences of violations and the actions to be taken to prevent corruption and money laundering.

To enhance the effectiveness of training, Eni segments its personnel according to risk level and assigns each employee a specific training programme (online or classroom-based), with the aim of covering 100% of at-risk resources142.
Training courses illustrate the applicable regulations, the tools to identify risks and the procedures for reporting violations.

In line with the principle of top-level commitment, members of the top management of Eni SpA, business heads/directors and the Chief Executive Officers (or equivalent roles) of subsidiaries also participate in training activities.
These roles generally take part in classroom workshops, highlighting the importance of - and the strong synergy required between - compliance and business.

In addition, senior executives of subsidiaries in Italy and abroad attend a three-day training event aimed at supporting the development and consolidation of their role, with a specific focus on compliance and risk mitigation.

In 2025, training activities included the continuation of the online courses ‘Code of Ethics, Anti-Corruption and Corporate Administrative Liability’, addressed to all Eni personnel in Italy and abroad, as well as the course on the Anti-Corruption Compliance Program for medium and high-risk personnel.
Classroom-based training activities were also delivered in accordance with a risk-based methodology.

These include, among other things, a commitment to review the Code of Ethics, the 231 Model and the ECG Policy on Anti- Corruption (available on the company’s website) and to comply with their principles.

As part of the qualification process for potential suppliers, described below, their ethical and reputational profile is assessed.
In cases involving higher corruption risk, the adoption by such suppliers of an Anti‑Corruption Compliance Program is also evaluated.

In all cases, the relevant contracts provide for the inclusion of compliance clauses which, in addition to the above‑mentioned commitments, include contractual remedies in the event of violations and, for higher‑risk cases, audit rights in favour of Eni.
