THE ROLE OF THE INTERNAL AUDIT FUNCTION Eni’s Internal Audit function plays a primary role in ensuring compliance with business conduct (including the management of reports received regarding alleged violations), within its broader mandate of reviewing and assessing the Internal Control and Risk Management System (ICRMS).
This role is carried out through the verification of the overall effectiveness and adequacy of the system and of its operational implementation.
In order to provide specialized support to top management and senior leadership on the Eni Risk and Internal Control Holistic Framework143, audit activities are defined within an Audit Plan.

The Plan is developed on the basis of relevance criteria and coverage of the main risks and is approved at least annually by the BoD, following the opinion of the Control and Risk Committee (CRC) and after consultation with the Chair of the Board, the Chief Executive Officer and Eni’s Board of Statutory Auditors.

The Head of the Internal Audit function may also initiate additional audit activities not included in the Audit Plan, at the request of the management, control and supervisory bodies, as well as of top management and senior leadership.
During 2025, a total of 29 audit engagements144 were carried out in 16 Countries as part of overall audit activities.

These included anti‑corruption audits to verify compliance with the provisions of the Anti‑Corruption Compliance Program and 15 supervisory interventions on the 231 Model and compliance systems of Italian and foreign subsidiaries.
The appointment and removal of the Head of the Internal Audit function are governed by specific governance rules designed to ensure full independence.

In addition to the recommendations set out in the Corporate Governance Code, the Head of Internal Audit is appointed by the Board of Directors, following the opinion of the Control and Risk Committee and the Nomination Committee and after consultation with the Board of Statutory Auditors, upon proposal by the Chair of the Board in agreement with the Chief Executive Officer.
The Head reports directly to the Chair of the Board and also reports to the Board of Statutory Auditors, including in its capacity as ‘Audit Committee’ under U.S. legislation.

Furthermore, in order to promote the evolution and progressive strengthening of risk management and control systems along the entire value chain, in 2025 Eni launched the initiative ‘G•row – Evolving through Risk & Control Governance’, with the support of several corporate functions.

The initiative, which implements the principle underlying the SDG 17 of the United Nations 2030 Agenda, aims to foster the growth of the corporate ecosystem and the progressive development of distinctive skills in the field of Risk & Control Governance through a collaborative approach involving partners, suppliers and stakeholders.

G•row takes shape through a digital platform that provides tools and resources for self‑assessment, benchmarking against comparable organizations and the start of an organic and continuous improvement journey (for more information see G•row).

WHISTLEBLOWING MECHANISMS AND VERIFICATION PROCESS FOR VIOLATIONS OF THE CODE OF ETHICS, ANTI-CORRUPTION RULES AND OTHER REGULATIONS [TAXO] Since 2006, Eni has adopted an internal regulatory instrument, Management of Whistleblowing Reports received by Eni SpA and by its Subsidiaries, which has been updated over time and, most recently, in November 2025, aligned with national and international best practices and with EU Directive 2019/1937 governing the process for receiving, analysing and handling whistleblowing reports.

The regulation, available on Eni’s website and intranet together with a concise operational guide, enables Eni people, as well as anyone who works or has worked in (143) The Internal Control and Risk Management System reflects the Company’s culture and values.

It consists of a coordinated set of tools, rules, procedures, organizational structures, data, systems, information flows and behaviours aimed at the effective and efficient identification, measurement, management and monitoring of the main risks.

In doing so, it contributes to the Company’s sustainable success and to the enhancement of business opportunities, as set out in the (144) Audit engagements that also cover reviews of activities exposed to corruption risk, as defined under Eni’s Internal Regulatory System.
ECG Policy on Eni Risk and Internal Control Holistic framework.
MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 233 Italy or abroad in the name, on behalf, or in the interest of the Company, to report alleged violations arising in the work environment.

To allow an effective and appropriate follow-up, whistleblowing reports should be substantiated and include, where possible, a detailed description of the facts, events or circumstances underlying the alleged violation, providing sufficient detail to enable the competent corporate functions to verify the accuracy of the reported elements.

Activities following receipt of a whistleblowing report are managed by the ‘Whistleblowing Team’145, which operates in accordance with the principles of objectivity, competence (also through periodic training) and professional diligence, and ensures that the whistleblower receives appropriate feedback.

After a preliminary analysis, the Team conducts in-depth reviews, analyses and assessments to establish whether the reported facts are substantiated, and monitors any corrective actions implemented in the relevant corporate areas and processes.
The Whistleblowing Team, through its members or other individuals appointed within the relevant unit, also prepares the Quarterly Whistleblowing Report.

This document is submitted to Eni’s Board of Statutory Auditors and, for the relevant whistleblowing files, subsequently forwarded to the Supervisory Bodies (national or international, depending on the subsidiary) and to the Boards of Statutory Auditors of the relevant companies, where applicable.
Statistical information on whistleblowing files handled over the past five years is published on Eni’s website.

All functions involved in the whistleblowing files management process, including those dealing with anti‑corruption issues, ensure independence, absence of conflicts of interest, objectivity, competence and professional diligence.
These principles are consistent with international standards and with the provisions of the Code of Ethics.

To facilitate the receipt of whistleblowing reports, both in written and oral form, Eni makes available a dedicated platform (https://whistleblowing.eni.com), operated by an external service provider, which guarantees the confidentiality of the identity of the whistleblower, the reported person and the contents of the communication.

The platform, widely publicised on companies’ websites, provides separate channels for Eni SpA and for EU subsidiaries (so-called proximity channels), in line with and pursuant to national legislation implementing EU Directive 2019/1937.

Regardless of the nature of the whistleblowing report or the Eni company involved, it is always possible to submit whistleblowing reports through the Eni SpA channel, which are managed in compliance with Italian whistleblowing legislation.

Alternative whistleblowing reporting tools are also in place (such as dedicated mailboxes, where necessary to ensure accessibility in specific contexts, and a voicemail service integrated into the platform).
In 2025, the investigation of 79 whistleblowing files relating to procurement and human‑resources matters was completed; for full details, see the dedicated page on Eni’s website.
Whistleblowing reports received during 2025, both from Eni employees and from the value chain, demonstrate awareness and effective use of the dedicated whistleblowing tool.

All Eni personnel who receive or handle reports are required to ensure the maximum confidentiality regarding the identity of the whistleblower, the persons involved and any other mentioned individuals, as well as the content and related documentation.

This obligation is based on the ‘need-to-know’ principle, requiring communication methods that protect the identity and reputation of the individuals concerned and ensure maximum confidentiality of the data processed.

The identity of the whistleblower cannot be disclosed without their explicit consent, except where required by law, and any retaliatory or discriminatory actions, direct or indirect, against the whistleblower are strictly prohibited.
Whistleblower are protected against any form of dismissal, demotion, suspension, threat, harassment or discrimination.
Violations of this prohibition result in disciplinary action and the adoption of appropriate measures to support the parties concerned.
Whistleblowers retain, in all cases, the right to contact the competent authorities and institutions to report any retaliation.

Eni engages in dialogue with policymakers both directly and indirectly through trade associations, without providing political contributions.

In particular, Eni contributes to the development of strategies and regulations aimed at accelerating the transition to Net Zero, clearly and transparently conveying its positioning on climate change and related strategic topics.
Eni recognizes the value of active participation in business associations’ work to develop and share best practices and advocacy positions aimed at promoting the energy transition.

In this regard, Eni proactively commits to periodically addressing the positions of each association, particularly those whose positions diverge from Eni’s Climate Advocacy Principles, towards an approach consistent with the need to act effectively to tackle climate change.

Activities and commitments related to Eni’s engagement with institutional stakeholders, including lobbying activities, are the responsibility of the Public Affairs Director (reporting directly to Eni’s Chief Executive Officer), who participates in the Management Committee and the Risk Committee meetings and regularly reports to the CEO on relevant matters.

POLITICAL CONTRIBUTIONS As provided by the Code of Ethics, Eni does not use corporate resources for electoral contributions or political advocacy activities, nor for activities directed toward non-governmental organizations, except for internal costs related to the activities of the Public Affairs Department and any expenses to third parties for intermediation activities with European Union institutions.

Furthermore, Eni does not make donations to political parties but supports a range of scientific, cultural and social initiatives worldwide: every request from such programs is subject to rigorous due diligence to ensure that Eni’s contribution is not misused and/or misinterpreted.
Eni is also registered in the EU Transparency Register146 and adheres to its Code of Conduct, which governs its relationship with EU institutions.

Through the Register, Eni provides extensive information on its activities, including the organization’s objectives, membership in trade and business associations, and expenses related to activities covered by the Register in the previous year.
In Italy, Eni is listed in the registers established at the Ministry of Enterprises and Made in Italy (formerly Ministry of Economic Development) and at the Chamber of Deputies.
Expenses related to lobbying activities in Italy are reported in the transparency register of the Ministry of Enterprises and Made in Italy.
The Chamber of Deputies publishes annual reports on the activities of registered companies.
In the United States, all activities and expenses falling under the Lobbying Disclosure Act are reported quarterly and made publicly available.

Furthermore, every public position submitted to stakeholders and USG regulatory bodies (e.g., SEC, BOEM - Bureau of Ocean Energy Management) is published on the relevant websites of those stakeholders and regulatory bodies.
TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T; S2-5] In line with its medium-to-long-term sustainable supply chain strategy, Eni has established specific targets for supplier management and procurement.
These targets aim to ensure that ESG-related requirements and safeguards for suppliers serve as a driver of sustainable development across the entire supply chain.
The Company monitors these indicators periodically, defining and implementing corrective actions as needed.

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM- 3; G1 IRO-1] Material impacts Eni’s procurement strategy is based on sharing values, commitments and objectives across the supply chain, adopting a systemic and inclusive approach.

This approach seeks to engage all levels of the supply chain in a path of sustainable growth through the promotion of sustainability principles, promoting environmental and social sustainability principles, to raise awareness and fostering more responsible business practices.
The positive impact of this strategy is reflected throughout the entire supply chain, enhancing its competitiveness, and Eni’s own operations.

In an industrial landscape increasingly focused on sustainability, Eni strengthens its leadership through the Open- es initiative, a digital platform and system alliance aimed at building a more resilient supply chain and supporting a modern, competitive entrepreneurial ecosystem aligned with sustainable transition objectives that constitute a central pillar of Eni’s strategy.
Material risks and opportunities No material risks were identified at Group level (see Process and results of the double materiality assessment).
236 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX a leadership role through the coordination of the system alliance and the Open-es digital platform.

By adopting a sustainable supply chain management strategy, Eni promotes more sustainable and responsible behaviours, transparency and innovation among suppliers, reducing potential inefficiencies and enhancing environmental, social and governance standards across the entire procurement cycle.

This approach generates tangible value for both Eni — through more competitive, resilient partners fully aligned with corporate objectives — and stakeholders, who benefit from a more robust, inclusive and energy-transition-oriented productive ecosystem.
Within this context, Eni’s leadership in Open- es serves as a key enabler.

Guiding this alliance allows the Company to accelerate the evolution of its supply chain toward more sustainable and higher-performing models while reinforcing its role as an innovator and catalyst for change in the market.

ACTIONS TAKEN ON MATERIAL IROs [ESRS 2 MDR-A; S2 SBM-3; S2-4; G1-2] The sustainable supply chain management strategy of Eni is based on the sharing of values, commitments and objectives with its supply chain and is articulated around three pillars, on which specific actions are implemented: • systemic and inclusive approach, involving the supply chain in a path of improvement and sustainable development, through common objectives and differentiated models depending on the ESG maturity of companies.

In this context, Eni promotes Open-es, launched with Boston Consulting Group and Google Cloud in 2021, to create a multi-stakeholder initiative among the industrial, financial and associative sectors to support companies in the process of measuring and improving their ESG performance.

To date, more than 30 partners have joined, including large industrial players, financial institutions and associations, and over 40,000 companies have registered, of which over 9,400 belong to Eni’s supply chain, both in Italy and abroad; • development and enhancement of best practices, supporting suppliers in meeting ESG requirements, providing tools for their sustainable development pathway and, more broadly, for competitiveness.

These include tools for: (i) measuring and improving ESG maturity through metrics aligned with the regulatory context and benchmarking against sector peers, with access to personalised development plans, solutions offered by specialised ESG providers and free events and training programmes; (ii) financial support through ‘Sustainable Supply Chain Finance’ (launched in 2023) to request early payment of invoices without impacts on credit lines, in order to incentivise a sustainable development pathway that integrates improved financial resource management with enhanced ESG performance.

In 2025, invoice advances were granted for a total amount of approximately €240 million; (iii) offering products and services under favourable conditions, such as solutions for energy efficiency and the use of HVOlution biofuel in transport; (iv) recognition of excellence through the Supply Chain Award, to share best practices and reward the most distinctive and innovative companies in the ESG field; (v) support for the competitiveness of the supply chain in the context of the energy transition.

In 2025, the ‘Energia di filiera (Value chain energy)’ programme was launched, a development initiative dedicated to Eni qualified suppliers, in particular SMEs, operating in the industrial areas most impacted by the transformation of Eni’s businesses, with the aim of supporting them in defining an improvement and development plan in a continuously evolving market context.

In 2025, the focus of the programme was the Versalis Transformation Plan, involving more than 200 SMEs, half of them contractors located in the Italian industrial districts of Brindisi, Priolo, and Ragusa; • pervasiveness of sustainability in the procurement process, represented by the integration of environmental protection, social growth and economic development principles at every stage.

Eni has adopted the ‘Sustainable Supply Chain Framework’, a governance mechanism that combines corporate objectives, legislative requirements, targets, and specific action plans that impact the procurement process and the supply chain more broadly.

This framework is implemented through a cross-cutting oversight of the different sustainability dimensions, with a focus on priority ESG topics periodically identified based on the corporate strategic plan and the evolution of the regulatory framework.

In 2025, for certain ESG dimensions identified as priorities for Eni — such as climate change, supply chain governance, human rights, cybersecurity and safety — verification and in-depth review activities continued, using specific minimum criteria for the evaluation of bids, in addition to dedicated standard contractual clauses.

(147) Assessment carried out based on information available from open sources and/or provided by the supplier and/or performance indicators and/or on-site audits, through at least one of the following processes: reputational due diligence, qualification process, performance evaluation feedback in HSE or compliance areas, feedback process, assessment on human rights issues (based on the SA8000 standard or similar).

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 237 Particular attention was devoted to topics identified as material in the value chain (for further details see Value chain and main impacts): (i) for suppliers with higher emissions, an engagement activity was launched to ensure the disclosure of their Scope 1 and 2 emissions.

The expenses related to the implementation of Eni’s Sustainable Supply Chain strategy are fully integrated into ordinary management and business operations and fall within the broader structural costs incurred.

In pursuing a more sustainable management of the supply chain Eni aims to strengthen all levels of the chain by providing tools that enable suppliers to adopt and replicate the Eni model, while maintaining a systemic and inclusive approach.

The Company also aims to promote greater accountability among direct commercial partners, particularly major market players, encouraging them to regularly conduct due diligence on their third parties and actively oversee environmental and social sustainability topics throughout the entire supply chain.

At the same time, Eni is committed to intensifying internal checks on subcontractors and all entities with which it maintains business relationships, with particular attention to critical or high-risk contexts, adopting a more rigorous approach.

This pathway is designed to enhance the Company’s ability to identify, prevent and mitigate risks, strengthening transparency and shared responsibility across the supply chain in the short and medium-term.

238 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX TAX STRATEGY AND PAYMENTS TRANSPARENCY [ENTITY-SPECIFIC; TAXO] POLICIES [ESRS 2 MDR-P] POLICY KEY CONTENTS: Code of Ethics • Commitment to carry out its activities in compliance with the applicable regulations on economic and financial sanctions as well as on trade controls, in particular by carrying out prior checks on all counterparties and requiring the same level of diligence from contractual partners.
Guidelines on Tax Matters (Tax strategy and tax control) • Contribute to the tax revenues of host Countries, supporting the economic and social development of the territories in which Eni operates.

• Commitment not to establish or locate the registered office of its subsidiaries in States that do not adopt international standards with regard to transparency and the exchange of tax information or in low-tax jurisdictions, except in cases of justified operational needs.

• Application of the regulations and instruments provided for by national legislation and by the ‘International Conventions for the avoidance of double taxation on income and capital and for the prevention of tax evasion and avoidance’, in line with the interpretative guidelines of the OECD or the United Nations.
• Maximisation of tax transparency through the publication of the Country by Country Report.
• Use of mechanisms aimed at avoiding or reducing tax disputes.
• Establishment of the Tax Control Framework (or tax risk management and control system) within the internal control system and definition of Tax Governance.

• Minimisation of tax risk, through specific controls to ensure the accuracy and timeliness of tax calculation and payment within a transparent and accurate compliance framework, in order to prevent potential disputes.

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T] Eni’s tax strategy, approved by the Board of Directors and published on the Company’s website, is based on the principles of transparency, honesty, fairness and good faith set out in the Code of Ethics and in the ‘OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises’.

Its primary objective is the timely and accurate fulfilment of tax obligations in the various Countries in which Eni operates, in the awareness that the Group makes a significant contribution to public revenues, supporting local economic and social development.

In this regard, while no quantitative targets have been defined, Eni is continuously committed to adopting conduct consistent with the principles of transparency, integrity, fairness and good faith set out in its Code of Ethics.

Eni’s tax strategy aims to ensure the correct determination of taxes in the different jurisdictions in which it operates, to mitigate the risk of juridical and economical double taxation of profits, and to prevent potential disputes with the tax and judicial authorities of the Countries of activities.
As part of its internal control system, Eni has implemented a Tax Control Framework (i.e.

a tax risk management and control system), the purpose of which is to provide reasonable assurance that business activities are managed in line with the principles and objectives of the tax strategy ( Actions taken on material IROs).

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3] Material impacts In carrying out its business activities, Eni works in close coordination with the governments of numerous Countries worldwide, which are often recipients of significant economic transactions.

Payments to governments represent socio‑economic support for host States and their proper and transparent management helps to prevent potential corrupt practices, which could have adverse impacts on local communities.

In addition to direct transactions with governments (such as, for example, production rights and royalties), Eni’s activities make a significant contribution to tax revenues, supporting the social and economic development and welfare of the Countries in which it operates151.
Given the importance of these financial flows for collective welfare, Eni adopts conduct consistent with the principles of transparency, integrity, fairness and good faith.

Transparency is a core value for Eni, which is committed to the voluntary disclosure of payments to governments and to combating all forms of corruption, while promoting the responsible use of financial resources in line with Sustainable Development Goal 16 of the United Nations 2030 Agenda.
Material risks and opportunities No material risks or opportunities were identified at Group level (see Process and results of the double materiality assessment).
(151) For further information, reference should also be made to the Report on Payments to Governments.
These actions are grouped into two main areas: (i) tax strategy; (ii) transparency initiatives.

TAX STRATEGY Eni’s Tax Strategy provides for the payment of taxes in the Countries where operations take place, in compliance with local tax regulations, and rejects aggressive tax policy choices, including locating legal entities in so-called tax havens.

Eni has implemented the Tax Control Framework, for which the Chief Transition & Financial Officer is responsible, structured in a three-phase process: (i) tax risk assessment (Risk Assessment); (ii) identification and implementation of controls designed to mitigate the identified risks; and (iii) verification of the effectiveness of controls and the related information flows (Reporting).

The control framework together with processes and procedures have been designed to reduce the risk of violations with significant financial or reputational impacts (tax risk) to a relatively low level.
In 2025, Eni Spa and its subsidiaries were not involved in any material disputes for violations of tax law or tax fraud that resulted in a final conviction.
For more information on the status of the Group’s tax litigation, please refer to the Notes of the consolidated financial statements, Legal proceedings section.
These disputes relate to the technical interpretation of local tax rules — often highly complex — and are managed with a conciliation approach.

As part of its tax risks management activities, Eni adopts a proactive approach of early engagement with tax authorities and maintains relationships based on transparency, dialogue and collaboration, participating — where appropriate — in cooperative compliance arrangements, such as the collaborative-compliance regime in Italy.

TRANSPARENCY INITIATIVES To promote the responsible use of resources and prevent corrupt practices, Eni supports the Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative (EITI) and has been a member since 2005, confirming its commitment to enhanced governance and transparency in the extractive sector.

Since 2023, Eni has served as an Alternate Member of the EITI Board, the initiative’s main decision-making body, responsible for setting strategic priorities and assessing Countries’ progress in implementing the EITI Standard.
The EITI framework establishes specific expectations for supporting companies which, since 2021, also constitute an assessment tool aimed at identifying good practices and areas for improvement.

In 2025, as part of the periodic assessment of compliance with the Expectations for EITI supporting companies, Eni fully met 8 expectations and partially met 1, out of a total of 9, increasing the number of fully met expectations from 7 to 8 compared to the previous year.

At local level, Eni actively participates in EITI initiatives in the Countries where it operates, both directly through the Multi-Stakeholder Groups established in EITI-implementing Countries and indirectly through industry associations.
In addition, in accordance with Italian Law No.

208/2015, Eni prepares the Country-by-Country Report (CbCR) required under Action 13 of the OECD/G20 Base Erosion and Profit Shifting (BEPS) project, aimed at enhancing transparency for tax administrations on the allocation of profits, the taxable base declared in each jurisdiction and the underlying economic activity, as well as the proportionality between taxes paid and value generated locally.
With the objective of further strengthening tax transparency for a wider range of stakeholders, Eni voluntarily publishes this report.
In 2024, Italy transposed EU Directive No.
2021/2101, which introduces mandatory public disclosure of selected CbCR information starting from the 2025 fiscal year.
The voluntary publication of the CbCR has been recognised by EITI as a best practice.

Consistently with its support for EITI, Eni has also published a position on contract transparency, encouraging governments to align with the new contract disclosure standard and expressing support for the mechanisms and initiatives adopted by Countries to promote transparency in this area.

Internal Regulatory System • Procedures and operating manuals relating to principles for the management of cybersecurity in ICT and Operational Technology (OT(a)) systems, such as vulnerability management, third party risk management, relevance assessment of applications, guidelines for the management of OT systems, identity and access management, management of the data sharing tools lifecycle and data security in test environments.
(a) Operational Technology (OT) refers to the set of hardware and software dedicated to the monitoring and direct control of physical processes, machinery and critical infrastructures.

Unlike IT, which manages data, OT operates in the physical world, covering sectors such as manufacturing, energy and transport, using systems and technologies to automate processes (for example PLC, SCADA, DCS).

TARGETS AND COMMITMENTS [ESRS 2 MDR-T] Eni ensures the protection of information systems, networks, data and digital infrastructures in terms of confidentiality, integrity and availability, against cyber threats and attacks.

Eni is committed to annually updating its cybersecurity strategy by capturing inputs both internal and external to the Company, such as emerging threats, the regulatory landscape and constantly evolving emerging technologies.

MATERIAL IMPACTS, RISKS AND OPPORTUNITIES (IROs) [ESRS 2 SBM-3] Material impacts In a digitally interconnected and technology-driven world, Eni’s activities are exposed, across the entire value chain, to the occurrence of potential cybersecurity incidents.

Such events may lead to the loss of data confidentiality and/or integrity following the disclosure or alteration of information relating to employees, customers or business partners and may harm the financial community, representing a threat to the security and privacy of the parties involved.
Similarly, the unavailability of information systems supporting the provision of services to customers and business partners could have significant impacts on these stakeholders.
Finally, the potential propagation of a cybersecurity incident to the information systems of Eni’s suppliers and partners could also result in serious impacts for those parties.

Material risks and opportunities Cybersecurity risk refers to the occurrence of cyberattacks capable of compromising information and communication technology (ICT) systems and industrial control systems (ICS), as well as facilitating the misappropriation of information sensitive to Eni.
For further information, see the section Risk factors and uncertainty/Legal, IT and financial risks.
No material opportunities were identified.

242 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX Reporting principles and criteria [ESRS 2 BP-1; BP-2] The Sustainability Statement, prepared on a consolidated Group basis, is approved by the Board of Director and subject to limited assurance by the same auditor of the consolidated financial statements.
To guarantee the reliability and traceability of the information, Eni relies on a dedicated IT tool that centralizes the collection of data sourced from the various company systems.
This system ensures a structured and verifiable management of the information flow and also traces the entire authorization process.

In parallel, to protect the integrity of such information, appropriate cybersecurity measures are adopted that integrate all the software applications with advanced protection systems against cyber threats, thus guaranteeing the security and availability of the data.

The structure of each thematic chapter of the Sustainability Statement follows the layout of the standards: Policies; Targets and commitments; Material Impacts, Risks and Opportunities (IROs); Actions related to the material IROs; Metrics.

By contrast, OpEx and CapEx associated with ordinary operational management (business as usual) are not included, as they fall within the broader structural or cross-cutting costs incurred by Eni regardless of the implementation of the actions described, such as those for the management of personnel, customers and suppliers.

244 MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX Code of Ethics and Policies [MDR-P] To enable the concrete implementation of what is stated in the mission and to guarantee integrity, transparency, fairness and effectiveness in its processes, Eni adopts specific rules for the performance of company activities and the exercise of powers, ensuring compliance with the general principles of traceability and segregation.

All Eni’s operational activities are attributable to a map of processes functional to the company’s activity and integrated with the needs and control principles set out in the compliance and governance models and based on the Articles of Association, the Code of Ethics and the Corporate Governance Code, on the 231 Model, on the principles of Eni’s control system on mandatory financial and sustainability reporting and on the CoSO Framework (Committee of Sponsoring Organizations of the Treadway Commission).

The Code of Ethics, renewed in 2020, expresses the company values: integrity, respect and protection of human rights, transparency, promotion of development, operational excellence, innovation, teamwork and collaboration.

Together with these values, the Code contains general principles158 and concrete rules of conduct, which provide practical guidance in company operations, addressed to the members of the management and control corporate bodies and to the employees of Eni and the subsidiaries, to all third parties, such as suppliers, commercial and industrial partners; the Code was drawn up taking into account their perspectives.

To ensure widespread understanding, the Code is disseminated and enhanced through various initiatives, including specific training activity and translation into the languages of the Countries in which Eni operates.
The updated version of the document can be consulted on the internet and intranet sites of Eni SpA and the subsidiaries.

The Code of Ethics was developed with the involvement of management and was approved by the BoD, on the proposal of the CEO in agreement with the Chairman, having heard the opinion of the Board of Statutory Auditors and the Control and Risks Committee.

The Code of Ethics constitutes a reference for Eni’s Internal Regulatory System159, and the main contents are recalled within the Policies section of the thematic chapters in which other regulatory documents160 are also mentioned, among which: • ECG Policies161: public documents (except for the Privacy and data protection Policy), applicable to Eni SpA and its subsidiaries, whose ‘Fundamental Guidelines’ are approved by the BoD, while the ‘Application Methods’ are approved by the Process Owner162, who is responsible for the design and its adequacy over time.

(158) The document underlines, among other things, Eni’s commitment to respect for human rights in its own activities and those of commercial partners, in line with the United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises, as well as in compliance with the Voluntary Principles on Security and Human Rights.
Other references considered are the SDGs and the Paris Pledge.

Consequently, the 2025 financial year represents the first ordinary fiscal year of application of the amended Regulation.

KPI related to turnover (turnover KPI) Eni Group’s consolidated financial statements are prepared in accordance with the International Financial Reporting Standards ‘IFRS’ as adopted by Commission Regulation (EC) 1126/2008.

In compliance with that, the Group turnover and the turnover relating to Taxonomy-aligned economic activities and to Taxonomy-eligible economic activities (not Taxonomy-Aligned activities) have been recognized pursuant to International Accounting Standard (IAS) 1.
The 6.6% share of eligible and aligned turnover is calculated as the part of turnover derived from eligible or aligned economic activities (numerator) divided by total turnover (denominator).
Eligible and aligned economic activities are described under paragraph 1.2.2.
The denominator comprises the Sales from operations (Revenues) line from the Consolidated Statement of Income.

A reconciliation is provided below: TURNOVER Revenues from contracts with customers (€ mln) Aligned activities Eligible activities Total Group 1,231 4,170 82,151 The proportion of turnover referred to in Article 8(2), point (a), of Regulation (EU) 2020/852 ‘turnover KPI’ is calculated as the part of the turnover derived from products or services associated with Taxonomy-aligned economic activities (numerator), divided by the Group total turnover (denominator).

The Group turnover and the turnover of eligible and aligned economic activities are recognized net of the effects of commodity derivatives activated to manage the Group’s exposure to movements in the prices of energy commodities, which qualify and are designated as cash flow hedges due to the efficacy of the relationship between the instrument and the hedged item, whereby a cash flow is either paid or received at the delivery of the underlying commodity.
The mark-to-market of cash flow hedges relating to a forecast transaction are taken to other comprehensive income.

Other commodity derivatives utilized by the Group to manage exposure to the commodity risks, which lack the requirements to be recognized in accordance with the own use exemption or to be qualified as hedges in accordance with IFRS, are marked to market with gains or losses recognized through profit and loss in a separate line item from revenues.
Such line item comprises the ineffective portion of cash flow hedges.

KPI related to capital expenditure (CapEx) (CapEx KPI) Capital expenditure ‘CapEx’ of the Eni Group’s and the ‘CapEx’ relating to eligible economic activities and to aligned economic activities cover costs that are accounted based on: a) IAS 16 Property, Plant and Equipment, paragraphs 73, e), point i) and point iii); b) IAS 38 Intangible Assets, paragraph 118, e), point i); c ) IFRS 16 Leases, paragraph 53, point h).
CapEx also covers additions to tangible and intangible assets resulting from business combinations.
The Group does not engage in economic activities that are recognized in accordance with IAS 40 and IAS 41.

The denominator comprises additions recognized in the financial year to the following line items of the Group’s assets reported in the Group statement of financial positions at December 31, 2025: ‘Property, plant and equipment’, ‘Intangible assets’ and ‘Right of Use’ as disclosed under footnotes no.

KPI related to operating expenditure (OpEx) (OpEx KPI) The 17.8% share of eligible and aligned operating expenditure ‘OpEx’ is calculated as the part of OpEx relating to eligible or aligned economic activities (numerator) divided by the Group total OpEx (denominator).
Eligible and aligned economic activities are described under paragraph 1.2.2.

Accounting policy Economic and financial data relating to Eni’s eligible and aligned economic activities for calculating the Taxonomy’s KPIs and proportion of eligible turnover, CapEx and OpEx, have been extracted from the Group accounting systems, the general ledger and the management accounting systems, which are used to prepare the separate financial statements of each consolidated subsidiary undertakings, mostly of which are in accordance with IFRS.
to calculate Eni’s Taxonomy KPIs and the eligible turnover, CapEx and OpEx proportion.

Therefore, data of turnover, OpEx and CapEx relating to Eni Group’s aligned and eligible economic activities utilized in calculating the Taxonomy KPIs and the proportion of eligible activities are the same the Group used in preparing the consolidated financial statements.

In the case of mono-business consolidated subsidiary undertakings performing a given eligible activity, relevant economic and financial data for the calculation of the Group eligible proportions have been extracted from the general ledger and the financial accounting to retrieve amounts of revenues, operating expenditures, additions to property, plant and equipment (PP&E) and intangible assets, additions to the right-of-use and additions to PP&E and intangibles resulting from business combinations.

In case of multi-business subsidiary undertakings, relevant data for calculating the Group eligible proportions have been derived also from the systems of managerial accounting that splits the accounts of the financial system and allocates revenues and cost amounts to different reporting objects: profit centers which correspond to business units, product lines which can share common costs, plants, capital projects, cost centers, etc., to support management’s understanding of the drivers of the financial performance and cost control.

Such structure of accounting flows, which is employed in preparing the Group consolidated financial statements, ensure that turnover, OpEx and CapEx are recognized by the economic activity where the underlying transactions occur, by this way avoiding double counting.
This is explained by evidence that amounts recognized or allocated by the managerial accounting system are reconciled with the accounting system and the general ledger.
Common costs are apportioned to different reporting objectives and economic activities based on disaggregation criteria that reflect how common inputs are absorbed.

For the purposes of reporting obligations, management has identified industrial fixed costs and non-capitalised R&D costs as the aggregate ‘OpEx’ operating expenses corresponding to the definition of the denominator adopted by the Delegated Regulation on reporting.

In line with the provisions, the OpEx incurred to purchase enabling products or in relation to enabling manufacturing processes have been claimed by the economic activities carried out by Eni in compliance with Art.
16 of the Taxonomy Regulation so that do not lead to a lock-in of assets that undermine long-term environmental goals, considering their economic lives.
In this context, the OpEx incurred by the E&P sector to increase energy efficiency/reduce CO2 emissions at oil & gas plants were excluded.
This principle has also been applied to CapEx.

MANUFACTURE OF PLASTICS IN PRIMARY FORM The economic activity includes: (i) production of resins, especially biodegradable and compostable polyesters and copolyesters, derived in whole or in part from renewable raw materials; (ii) production of biodegradable and compostable plastics, i.e., blends of resins derived in whole or in part from renewable raw materials.

The economic activity ‘Manufacture of plastics in primary form’ is a transitional activity as of Article 10, paragraph 2, of Regulation (EU) 2020/852 if it meets the technical screening criteria described at the point 3.17 of Regulation (EU) 2021/2139.

Substantial contribution to climate change mitigation For the assessment of substantial contribution to climate change mitigation, criterion c) related to activity 3.17 as stated in EU Regulation 2021/2139 was applied, as follows: c) derived in whole or in part from renewable raw materials, and the greenhouse gas emissions over their lifecycle are lower than the greenhouse gas emissions in the lifecycle of equivalent primary form plastics manufactured from fossil fuels.
Greenhouse gas emissions over the lifecycle are calculated using Recommendation 2013/179/EU or, alternatively, ISO 14067:2018 or ISO 14064-1:2018.
Greenhouse gas emissions quantified over the lifecycle are verified by an independent third party.
In this context, chemicals derived from hydrocarbons were identified as equivalent to resins and plastics derived in whole or in part from renewable raw materials.
These equivalent chemicals were identified considering chemical equivalence in terms of composition and equivalence in the chemical family.
For both product lines, the hydrocarbon-derived equivalent is PBAT.
This analysis confirmed compliance with the stated criterion ‘c’ of the Taxonomy.

Do No Significant Harm (‘DNSH’) Climate change adaptation The Group has performed a risk assessment of the exposure of all aligned activities to acute and chronic weather events as required by Appendix ‘A’ to the Climate Delegated Regulation based on the Company’s methodology described herein.

The management has assessed the risk of exposure of the Group’s assets to climate-related acute and chronic hazards, following the guidelines of Appendix A to the Climate Delegated Regulation, setting generic criteria for DNSH to climate change adaptation.

The Group has put in place management control systems and procedures to identify, evaluate and mitigate physical climate risks, which the Company defines as the risk that potential perspective changes in meteorological patterns, extreme weather phenomena and gradual changes in weather conditions and in the physical environment linked to climate change may adversely and significantly affect assets’ future performance, safety of operations and future expected cash flows, so to significantly harm the objective of climate change adaptation.

The management regularly reviews the exposure of the Group’s assets to the acute and chronic climate-related hazards described in the above- mentioned Appendix A and other natural hazards based on a proprietary methodology to identify physical climate risks over a long-term horizon.

The purpose of this risk assessment is to define and execute mitigation plans designated to adapt the Group assets to current or expected risks, considering the already existing barriers at each Company’s asset.

This assessment considers various timing horizons based on assets’ useful lives (about thirty years for solar installation, wind mills and biorefineries, whereas recharging points for EV have seven years of useful live).

Eni’s assessment methodology of assets’ exposure to natural hazards features the following steps: • it utilizes input data furnished by an external provider, which has elaborated detailed geographic maps of prospective climate-related risks ensuring a full coverage of onshore and offshore areas where Eni’s assets are located.

Those climate maps combine the most updated climate forecast models, also incorporating historical weather patterns, to provide expected quantitative trends in the evolution of climate- related events (like expected number of days with temperatures above or below historical averages, wind strength, rain intensity, etc.
and systems and procedures (early warning systems, procedures to put in safety plants and equipment, existence of monitoring and verification plans, etc.).

The outcome of that review informs the management of the residual riskand: • in case of chronic climate-related hazards, monitoring activities are designed, planned, and carried out leading to the possible implementation and follow-up of remediation measures; • in case of acute climate-related hazards, asset integrity process is activated which can lead to the definition and activation of an adaptation plan.
Based on the described procedure and methodology, Eni’s installations for the production of plastics in primary form show marginal residual exposure to prospective adverse weather events.

For these risks, a site-specific risk assessment is planned during 2026 in order to define the corresponding monitoring plan and actions, with the objective of making the activity climate change adapted within the timeframe of the plan.
No violations of the DNSH principle were found in relation to the other environmental objectives.

Compliance with the Minimum Safeguards (Ms) - Article 3 ‘c’ of the EU Taxonomy Regulation The criteria for the eco-sustainability of economic activities outlined in article 3 of the Taxonomy Regulation call for respecting minimum safeguards when conducting business (referred to in paragraph ‘c’).

The rule under Article 18 identifies the MS with the procedures implemented by a company to ensure that business conduct complies with the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises and the United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights.

Compliance with the MS includes the principles and rights set out in the eight core conventions identified in the International Labour Organisation’s Declaration on Fundamental Principles and Rights at Work and in the International Bill of Human Rights.

When companies implement these procedures, they must also comply with the ‘do no significant harm’ principle outlined in Article 2, paragraph 17 of Regulation (EU) 2019/2088, the Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation (SFDR).

The SFDR requires financial market participants to assess the ESG risk of the investments within the financial products they intend to offer investors, measuring the ESG performance of the investee companies against a predefined set of key impact indicators in critical ‘principal adverse impact’ areas.

Five of these indicators have a social nature: (i) violations of the UN Global Compact principles and the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises; (ii) lack of processes and compliance mechanisms to monitor compliance with the previous point’s principles; (iii) unadjusted gender pay gap; (iv) Board gender diversity; and (v) exposure to controversial weapons.

The definition of sustainable investment in article 2 (17) of the SFDR states that an investment is sustainable if it contributes to broadly defined environmental or social objectives, provided that it does not harm any of these objectives.

Thus, Eni assumes that in complying with the SFDR principle ‘do no significant harm’, it is understood to refer to the five social impact indicators described above, four of which are included in Eni’s human rights due diligence processes.
Regarding the fifth, Eni confirms that it does not have any exposure to controversial weapons.

The OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises are principles for responsible business conduct related to eight business areas: • three relate to the issues of human rights (human rights, consumer protection, employment and industrial relations); • Anti-Corruption; • fair competition; • taxation.
Finally, environmental protection is treated by the sustainability performance criteria set article 3 of the Taxonomy Regulation, while science/ technology are out of the scope.
The ILO’s eight labor conventions are comprised in the wider issue of respect for human rights.

Observance of the fundamental principles of human rights contained in the International Bill of Human Rights (Universal Declaration of Human Rights, International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights and International Covenant on Economic Social and Cultural Rights) is ensured by Eni’s compliance with the Italian Constitution and rules intended to implement it, which embody human rights principles.
As a company incorporated in Italy, Eni is obliged to observe them.

Compliance with the safeguard clause is based on establishing and maintaining adequate company due diligence processes and company’s management systems in the following areas: • human rights; • Anti-corruption; • compliance with competition law; • business taxation.

Within the context of the Company’s zero tolerance for corruption, Eni has adopted a controlled environment that includes processes and controls designed to minimize the risk of behavior or transactions that could lead to willful or negligent acts of corruption.

This aims to ensure constant and strict compliance of Eni’s employees, contractors and other individuals working or acting on behalf of Eni with the anti-corruption laws in force in the Countries where the Company operates.
This system also applies to money laundering.
The control environment is based on values the organization shares, starting with top management.

It includes establishing a code of ethics inspired by the principles of transparency, honesty, fairness, and good faith in conducting business, adherence to the UN Ten Principles of Corporate Responsibility, participation in the Global Compact and personnel training on ethical issues.

The processes and controls are designed to ensure accurate and transparent recording of corporate transactions, assessment of economic counterparties in significant transactions (acquisitions/divestment of subsidiaries, shareholdings and assets, mining rights, business combinations, etc.

), involvement of certain types of counterparties (business associates, joint venture partners, brokers) or in areas (trading, non-profit initiatives, sponsorships) exposed to corruption risks, as well as compliance of business conduct with internal rules under all circumstances where a breach of the code of ethics might be possible, to prevent any form of corruption in managing the business.

An integral part of Eni’s DD on Anti-Corruption is establishing and maintaining a whistleblowing mechanism even for managing anonymous reports received by the Company through a well-identified and recognizable channel of alleged violations of anti-corruption and money laundering regulations (this mechanism also applies to the DD on Human Rights).
In 2025, neither the Company nor members of senior management were party to criminal proceedings for violating anti-corruption regulations that resulted in a final verdict of conviction.
Please refer to the notes to the consolidated financial statements for more information on the status of the Group’s legal proceedings.
Eni has adopted a due diligence system for managing relations with the tax authorities of the Countries in which it operates.
The aim is to minimize the risk that business operations violate applicable tax regulations.

The Company’s tax guidelines provide for the payment of taxes in the Countries where operations take place according to the merit as well as the letter of local rules and rejects aggressive tax policy choices, including delocalization of economic activities to so-called tax havens.
The Company has a Tax Control Framework, i.e.
a specific tax risk control system.
Management is responsible for verifying consistency between tax management choices and the Board- approved strategy.
The control environment and processes/procedures are designed to mitigate the risk of violations which could trigger significant financial or reputational impact (tax risk).
In 2025, no Group company was party to any tax dispute for violations of tax rules or tax fraud resulting in a final verdict of conviction.
For more information on the status of the Group’s tax litigation, please refer to the notes to the consolidated financial statements.
These disputes relate to the technical interpretation of local tax regulations, which are often very complex.
They are managed with a view to reconciliation.

Eni has set up a controlled environment and a set of procedures and controls to minimize the risk that business and corporate activities violate the rules protecting competition in the various Countries where it operates.

Among the fundamental values of the Company are the principles of fair competition – understood as a market environment that encourages companies to excel in the quality and cost effectiveness of the products and/or services sold/supplied – and compliance with antitrust legislation.
Eni’s control system has three phases: prevention, risk monitoring/mitigation and counteracting unlawful conduct.
It is designed to minimize the risk that Eni’s business units and subsidiaries engage in anti-competitive conduct, adopt practices that restrict the free market or collude with competing companies.
Corporate transactions to increase market share (mergers/acquisitions) are executed after the antitrust authorities of the jurisdictions concerned have been informed.

Appropriate remediation plans are formulated in response to any comments received and in compliance with standstill obligations and the prohibition of unlawful exchange of information during the negotiation and due diligence phases.
In 2025, no Group company or senior management member was party to disputes for antitrust legislation violations that resulted in a final verdict of conviction.
On the reporting date, there was no significant pending antitrust disputes.
Human rights are at the heart of Eni’s vision as a responsible company and a core component of the organization’s values, culture, and management systems.

Eni is committed to respecting human rights in all business activities and places similar expectations on business partners operating on behalf of Eni or who are contracted over the course of Eni’s industrial activities.

Eni has adopted a human rights due diligence process that complies with the OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises, including OECD guidelines on Human Rights DD, and the United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights (UNGP).

MANAGEMENT REPORT CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ANNEX 257 • the risk of causing (or contributing to causing) actual or potential adverse impacts on Human Rights, with reference to the UNGPs and the OECD Guidelines; • the risk of incurring sanctions, significant financial losses, or reputational damage.

Eni assesses the Human Rights potential and actual impacts of its activities on an ongoing basis and identifies specifically tailored strategies and solutions, in the context of an ongoing effort to improve prevention and mitigation of such impacts.

In line with OECD guidelines, Eni has established a mechanism for collecting and evaluating complaints and concerns brought to the Company’s attention through appropriate channels for listening and for the receipt of communications by individuals, communities, or associations of individuals, aimed at ensuring that any possible violations of Human Rights are promptly detected, scrutinized, managed and – where ascertained – remedied.

In the event of alleged Human Rights violation the company provides two schemes of access to the Company: • a ‘Grievance Mechanism’, which includes a set of procedures that Eni makes available for the receipt and treatment of alleged violations, which include the options to lodge, in written or verbal form, complaints or grievances on part of affected stakeholders in relation to the Eni’s business activities and on their management and resolution.

Human rights grievances qualified as ‘significant’ entail a specific procedure for their scrutiny and response; • ‘Reporting’, which includes the channels available to employees or third parties to report, even confidentially or anonymously, issues concerning the Internal Control System or alleged violations of the principles stated in Eni’s Code of Ethics, such as business ethics, bullying, harassment, discrimination, and respect for Human Rights.
Eni also cooperates with other non-judicial redress mechanisms, such as the one provided and regulated by the OECD Guidelines and set up at OECD National Contact Points.

Eni is actively committed to reviewing complaints and providing or cooperating to provide remedies for adverse human rights impacts that it may have caused or contributed to, and to make every effort to promote the achievement of the same objective in cases where the impact is directly related to its operations.
Eni cooperates actively and in good faith with other access facilities to reach a judicial or non-judicial resolution to open issues.
In no case does Eni prohibit potential claimants access to remediation measures.
The company is committed to preventing reprisals against workers and other stakeholders for raising human rights concerns.
It does not tolerate or contribute to threats, intimidation, reprisals or attacks against human rights defenders and stakeholders involved with its operations.
An integral part of due diligence is the communication of the obtained results.
Eni publishes a yearly report ‘Eni for’ sustainability, which includes a dedicated section to human rights reporting to inform and update stakeholders on progress made to address human rights issues.
In 2025, Eni did not receive any final verdict of conviction for violations of laws, regulations or other regulatory institutions relating to human rights, bribery, competition or tax violations.
The Company is cooperating actively and in good faith with the OECD National Contact Points to resolve pending Specific Instances.
In conclusion, considering the draft Report ‘Minimum Safeguards’, Eni believes it complies with the safeguard clause of Article 3, paragraph ‘c’ of the EU Taxonomy Regulation.
